\sum W_i h_i^2 &= 3000 \times (3^2 + 6^2 + 9^2 + 12^2 + 15^2 + 18^2) \\
&= 3000 \times (9 + 36 + 81 + 144 + 225 + 324) \\
&= 3000 \times 819 \\
&= 2{,}457{,}000 \; \text{kN·m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4 — Force at 4th Floor (h₄ = 12 m)

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_4 &= V_B \cdot \frac{W_4 \cdot h_4^2}{\sum W_i h_i^2} \\
&= 959.04 \times \frac{3000 \times 12^2}{2{,}457{,}000} \\
&= 959.04 \times \frac{3000 \times 144}{2{,}457{,}000} \\
&= 959.04 \times \frac{432{,}000}{2{,}457{,}000} \\
&= 959.04 \times 0.1758 \\
&\approx 168.61 \; \text{kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Q₄ ≈ 168.6 kN**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the denominator of Aₕ — most students write $Z \cdot I \cdot S_a / R \cdot g$. **KILLS 1 mark.**
> - **Mixing up Z value** for Zone IV (it is **0.24**, not 0.16 or 0.36). **KILLS 1 mark.**
> - **Wrong Σ Wᵢ hᵢ²** — the vertical distribution formula uses **hᵢ²**, not $h_i$. **KILLS 1 mark.**
> - **Forgetting units** for V_B (kN) and Qᵢ (kN). **KILLS 0.5 mark.**
> - **Not using g = 9.81 m/s²** — since Z is normalized to g, the g's cancel and we can drop g in the ratio. Many students wrongly multiply by 9.81. **KILLS 1 mark.**

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- 🔴 **India has FOUR seismic zones** as per IS 1893 (Part 1) : 2016: **II, III, IV, V** (Zone I is omitted).
- 🔴 **Zone Factors (Z):** **0.10, 0.16, 0.24, 0.36** — memorize them in order.
- 🔴 **Zone V is most hazardous** — Kutch, NE India, parts of J&K, Uttarakhand, HP.
- 🔴 **Zone II is least hazardous** — interior peninsular India.
- 🔴 **Kerala falls in Zone III (Z = 0.16)** — important for KTU Kerala students.
- 🔴 **Design base shear formula (IS 1893):** $V_B = A_h \cdot W$ where $A_h = \dfrac{Z \cdot I \cdot S_a}{2 \cdot R \cdot g}$.
- 🔴 **The "2" in the denominator** is the DBE/MCE conversion — DO NOT omit it.
- 🔴 **Magnitude (M)** = energy released; **Intensity (I)** = damage observed.
- 🔴 **R-factor** depends on lateral-load-resisting system: SMRF = 5.0, OMRF = 4.0, Masonry = 1.5.
- 🔴 **PGA is the primary design input** for a seismic design.
- 🔴 **Vertical distribution** of base shear uses $W_i h_i^2$ weighting (parabolic distribution).
- 🔴 **Liquefaction** is a major concern in Zone IV & V sites with loose sandy subsoil.
- 🔴 **Engineering Geologist's role:** site selection, fault mapping, liquefaction assessment, slope stability under seismic loads.
- 🔴 **Gutenberg-Richter law:** $\log N = a - b \cdot M$ — useful for seismicity analysis (advanced).
- 🔴 **Always cite IS 1893 (Part 1) : 2016** in KTU exam answers for full credit.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mineralogy - Physical Properties of Minerals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET416 - ENGINEERING GEOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mineralogy - Physical Properties of Minerals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Mineral? (KTU 2024 Formal Definition)

A **mineral** is a naturally occurring, inorganic, homogeneous crystalline solid with a definite (but not fixed) chemical composition and an ordered atomic arrangement, formed by geological processes. The scientific study of minerals — their composition, structure, occurrence, and properties — is called **mineralogy**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *"A mineral is a naturally occurring inorganic substance, having a definite chemical composition (or range of composition) and a characteristic atomic structure, possessing physical properties that are constant within definite limits, and formed by the inorganic processes of nature."*

### 1.2 What is "Mineralogy" and Why Study Physical Properties?

**Mineralogy** is the branch of geology that deals with the study of minerals. In engineering geology, the **physical properties of minerals** are of paramount importance because:

- They provide a **rapid, field-friendly identification toolkit** without expensive laboratory equipment.
- They directly influence the **engineering behavior of rocks** (e.g., cleavage affects rock cleavage, hardness affects drillability, specific gravity affects slope stability calculations).
- They are the **first diagnostic test** a geologist performs on a rock-forming mineral.

> [!NOTE]
> A "rock" is an aggregate of one or more minerals. Therefore, understanding minerals is the gateway to understanding rocks — and rocks are what civil engineers build tunnels, dams, and foundations in.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: "Identifying a Mineral is Like Identifying a Person"

Imagine you meet a stranger at KTU campus. How do you identify them later? You observe:

| Human Feature | Equivalent Mineral Property |
|---------------|----------------------------|
| Face shape & skin color | **Color & Luster** |
| Hair texture (straight, curly) | **Crystal Habit / Form** |
| How the person breaks under stress (calm vs. brittle) | **Cleavage & Fracture** |
| Strength (can they lift a rock?) | **Hardness (Mohs Scale)** |
| Body density (light vs. heavy build) | **Specific Gravity** |
| A mark left when rubbed on paper | **Streak** |
| Magnetism in personality (attracts others) | **Magnetism** |

Just as no single human feature is enough, **no single mineral property is definitive** — engineers use a **combination of properties** to positively identify a mineral.

### 1.4 The Two Major Categories of Physical Properties

1. **Megascopic Properties** — Identifiable by naked eye, hand lens, or simple tools (streak plate, knife, magnet). **These are the most exam-relevant for KTU.**
2. **Microscopic / Optical Properties** — Identifiable only under a petrological microscope (pleochroism, extinction angle, interference colors, refractive index). These belong to advanced optical mineralogy.

> [!TIP]
> KTU 2024 questions in Module 2 predominantly test **megascopic physical properties** with one or two questions on Mohs hardness scale and cleavage geometry.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Complete Taxonomy of Physical Properties of Minerals

The physical properties of minerals are systematically classified into the following categories. Each is explained in detail below.

#### A. Properties Related to Light Interaction

**1. Color**
- The color of a mineral in reflected light.
- Caused by selective absorption of certain wavelengths by the mineral's atomic structure (mainly due to transition elements like Fe, Mn, Cr, Cu, Ni).
- **Most unreliable diagnostic property** because trace impurities can drastically change color.
- *Example:* Quartz (SiO₂) — pure is colorless, but impurities make it purple (amethyst), yellow (citrine), pink (rose quartz), or smoky (smoky quartz).

**2. Streak**
- The color of the **finely powdered mineral**, obtained by rubbing it on an unglazed porcelain plate called a **streak plate**.
- Streak is **more reliable than color** because impurities don't affect powder color.
- *Example:* Hematite — appears black/silver in hand specimen, but streak is **reddish-brown** (diagnostic).
- *Example:* Pyrite — yellow in color, streak is **greenish-black**.
- *Note:* Harder minerals (harder than streak plate, i.e., > 6.5) leave no streak.

**3. Luster**
- The **quality and intensity of light reflected** from a fresh mineral surface.
- **Metallic Luster:** Looks like polished metal (e.g., Galena, Pyrite, Magnetite, Native Gold). Opaque even on thin edges.
- **Non-Metallic Lusters** (sub-categorized):
  - *Vitreous* — Glass-like (Quartz, Calcite, Feldspar)
  - *Resinous* — Like resin (Sphalerite, Sulfur)
  - *Pearly* — Iridescent, like a pearl (Talc, Muscovite)
  - *Silky* — Like silk fibers (Satin Spar variety of Gypsum, Asbestos)
  - *Greasy* — Looks as if coated with oil (Nepheline, Talc)
  - *Adamantine* — Brilliant, like diamond (Diamond, Cassiterite, Rutile)
  - *Dull / Earthy* — No shine (Limonite, Kaolinite, Bauxite)
  - *Sub-metallic* — Slightly metallic but still translucent on thin edges (Hematite, Chromite)

**4. Transparency / Diaphaneity**
- The ability of a mineral to transmit light.
- *Transparent* — Image clearly visible through the mineral (e.g., Quartz, Calcite, Fluorite).
- *Translucent* — Light passes through, but image is blurred (e.g., Agate, Chalcedony).
- *Opaque* — No light passes through (e.g., Galena, Pyrite, Magnetite, Graphite).

#### B. Properties Related to Mechanical Behavior

**5. Hardness (H)**
- The **resistance of a mineral to scratching**.
- Quantified by the **Mohs Hardness Scale (1812, Friedrich Mohs)**, a relative 1–10 scale.
- The scale works on the principle: *"A mineral of hardness 'n' can scratch all minerals of hardness less than 'n', and can be scratched by all minerals of hardness greater than 'n'."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield: Mohs Hardness Scale (Must Memorize):**
>
> | Mohs No. | Mineral | Chemical Formula | Field Test |
> |---------|---------|------------------|------------|
> | 1 | Talc | Mg₃Si₄O₁₀(OH)₂ | Crushed by fingernail |
> | 2 | Gypsum | CaSO₄·2H₂O | Scratched by fingernail (2.5) |
> | 3 | Calcite | CaCO₃ | Scratched by copper coin (3.5) |
> | 4 | Fluorite | CaF₂ | Scratched by glass/knife easily |
> | 5 | Apatite | Ca₅(PO₄)₃(F,Cl,OH) | Scratched by knife with difficulty |
> | 6 | Orthoclase Feldspar | KAlSi₃O₈ | Scratches glass, scratched by knife |
> | 7 | Quartz | SiO₂ | Scratches steel and glass easily |
> | 8 | Topaz | Al₂SiO₄(F,OH)₂ | Scratches quartz |
> | 9 | Corundum | Al₂O₃ | Scratches topaz |
> | 10 | Diamond | C | Scratches everything |

**Useful Field Tools for Hardness Test (in increasing hardness):**
1. Fingernail ≈ **2.5**
2. Copper coin ≈ **3.5**
3. Steel knife blade / glass plate ≈ **5.5 to 6.0**
4. Quartz crystal (carried separately) = **7.0**

**6. Cleavage**
- The **tendency of a mineral to break along smooth, flat, parallel planes of weak atomic bonding**.
- Cleavage is described by:
  - **Number of directions** (basal cleavage, prismatic cleavage, etc.)
  - **Quality** (perfect, good, fair, poor)
  - **Angles between cleavage planes**

| # Cleavage Directions | Geometry | Example Minerals |
|----------------------|----------|------------------|
| 1 direction (basal) | Parallel sheets | Mica (Biotite, Muscovite) |
| 2 directions at 90° | Cubic box | Galena, Halite, Orthoclase |
| 2 directions at 90° + 1 less perfect | Prismatic, rectangular cross-section | Pyroxenes (Augite) |
| 2 directions at 60°/120° | Prismatic, hexagonal cross-section | Amphiboles (Hornblende) |
| 3 directions at 90° (cubic) | Cube | Galena, Halite, Fluorite |
| 3 directions at 90° (not cubic) | Rectangular blocks | Feldspar |
| 3 directions at 60°/120° (rhombohedral) | Rhombs | Calcite, Dolomite |
| 4 directions (octahedral) | Octahedrons | Fluorite, Diamond |
| 6 directions | Dodecahedral | Sphalerite |

**7. Fracture**
- The **breakage pattern of a mineral along surfaces that are NOT planes of cleavage** (i.e., random breaking).
- Types:
  - *Conchoidal* — Smooth, curved, shell-like surfaces (Quartz, Obsidian, Flint)
  - *Fibrous / Splintery* — Splinters (Asbestos, some Siderite)
  - *Hackly* — Jagged edges with sharp points (Native Copper, Native Silver)
  - *Uneven / Irregular* — Rough surface (most minerals)
  - *Earthy* — Crumbly (Limonite, Bauxite, Kaolinite)

> [!NOTE]
> **Cleavage vs. Fracture — A Classic KTU Question:** Cleavage is breakage along planes of **atomic weakness**; fracture is breakage along surfaces with **no atomic preference**. Cleavage surfaces are typically smooth and flat; fracture surfaces are not.

**8. Tenacity**
- The **behavior of a mineral under stress, pressure, or impact** (mechanical response):
  - *Brittle* — Breaks/powders easily (Quartz, Calcite, most sulfides)
  - *Malleable* — Can be hammered into thin sheets (Gold, Silver, Copper)
  - *Ductile* — Can be drawn into wires (Gold, Silver, Platinum)
  - *Sectile* — Can be cut with a knife (Gypsum, Talc, Graphite)
  - *Flexible* — Bends without breaking (thin Mica sheets)
  - *Elastic* — Bends and returns to original shape (Mica sheets — flexible AND elastic)
  - *Plastic* — Deforms permanently without breaking (Steatite)

#### C. Properties Related to Density and Heft

**9. Specific Gravity (G)**
- The **ratio of the weight of a mineral to the weight of an equal volume of water** at 4°C.
- Specific gravity is dimensionless.

$$G = \frac{W_{mineral}}{W_{water}} = \frac{\rho_{mineral}}{\rho_{water}}$$

Where:
- $W_{mineral}$ = Weight of the mineral in air
- $W_{water}$ = Weight of water displaced (weight in air − weight in water, by Archimedes' principle)
- $\rho_{water} = 1 \text{ g/cm}^3$ at 4°C

| Class | Specific Gravity Range | Example Minerals |
|-------|------------------------|------------------|
| Light | $G < 2.5$ | Gypsum (2.32), Halite (2.16), Graphite (2.2) |
| Average | $2.5 \le G \le 3.0$ | Quartz (2.65), Calcite (2.72), Feldspar (2.55–2.76) |
| Heavy | $3.0 < G \le 5.0$ | Pyrite (5.0), Hematite (5.2), Magnetite (5.2) |
| Very Heavy | $G > 5.0$ | Galena (7.5), Native Gold (19.3), Platinum (21.5) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:** Specific gravity is **critical for slope stability analysis, settlement calculations, and unit weight determination** of rock masses. Galena (G=7.5) feels "heavy" when held in hand — a quick field diagnostic.

#### D. Properties Related to Special Forces

**10. Magnetism**
- Attraction to a magnet.
- *Strongly Magnetic:* Magnetite (Fe₃O₄) — picks up iron filings, deflects compass needle.
- *Weakly Magnetic:* Pyrrhotite (Fe₁₋ₓS), Ilmenite (FeTiO₃) — slight compass deflection.
- *Non-Magnetic:* All other minerals.

**11. Electrical Properties**
- *Piezoelectric* — Generate electric charge under mechanical stress (Quartz, Tourmaline).
- *Triboluminescent* — Emit light when scratched (Fluorite).

**12. Radioactivity**
- Minerals containing U, Th, Ra emit radiation detectable with a Geiger counter.
- Examples: Pitchblende (Uraninite), Monazite, Allanite, Zircon.

**13. Solubility & Taste**
- *Soluble* minerals (in water or saliva): Halite (rock salt — *salty taste*), Sylvite (KCl — *bitter taste*).
- *Insoluble* — All other rock-forming minerals.

> [!WARNING]
> **NEVER taste unknown minerals in the lab.** Many minerals are toxic (e.g., Galena is lead-bearing, Cinnabar is mercury-bearing).

**14. Feel (Tactile Property)**
- *Soapy / Greasy feel* — Talc, Steatite, Pyrophyllite (very soft phyllosilicates).
- *Smooth / Soapy* — Chlorite, Serpentine.
- *Rough / Sandy* — Sandstone grains, some feldspars.
- *Cold feel* — Metallic minerals feel colder than non-metallic.

**15. Odor**
- *Garlic odor* — Arsenic minerals (Arsenopyrite) when struck.
- *Rotten egg odor* — Sulfur-bearing minerals (Sulfur, Pyrite, Marcasite) when struck/ground.
- *Clayey odor* — Kaolinite when breathed upon (water adsorption).

**16. Fluorescence & Phosphorescence**
- *Fluorescence* — Emits visible light when exposed to UV light (Fluorite, Scheelite, Calcite, Willemite).
- *Phosphorescence* — Continues to glow AFTER UV source is removed.

### 2.2 The Mohs Hardness Scale — Why It's Non-Linear

A common student misconception is that Mohs 10 is "10 times harder than Mohs 1." **This is false.** Mohs is a **relative ordinal scale**, not a linear or ratio scale. Actual Vickers Hardness Numbers (VHN) demonstrate the non-linearity:

| Mohs Hardness | Mineral | Vickers Hardness (kg/mm²) |
|---------------|---------|---------------------------|
| 1 | Talc | 1 |
| 2 | Gypsum | 2 |
| 3 | Calcite | 9 |
| 4 | Fluorite | 21 |
| 5 | Apatite | 48 |
| 6 | Orthoclase | 72 |
| 7 | Quartz | 100 |
| 8 | Topaz | 200 |
| 9 | Corundum | 400 |
| 10 | Diamond | 1600 |

> [!NOTE]
> Diamond is **~16× harder than Corundum** and **~1600× harder than Talc** in absolute Vickers hardness, despite the Mohs scale only ranging 1–10. The Mohs scale understates the true hardness of diamond.

### 2.3 KTU 2024 Formula Cheat Sheet

| Property | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|----------|---------------------|----------------|
| Specific Gravity | $G = \frac{W_{air}}{W_{air} - W_{water}}$ | Dimensionless |
| Density | $\rho = G \times \rho_{water}$ | $\rho_{water} = 1 \text{ g/cm}^3$ at 4°C |
| Unit Weight | $\gamma = \rho \times g$ | g = 9.81 m/s² |
| Mohs Hardness | Ordinal scratch-resistance scale 1 to 10 | Dimensionless |
| Hardness Ratio (H/R) | $H = \frac{\text{Mohs hardness}}{\text{Rock grade}}$ | Used in drillability |
| Protodyakonov Index | $f = \frac{R_c}{10}$ | $R_c$ = uniaxial compressive strength (MPa) |

### 2.4 Engineering Applications of Physical Properties

1. **Tunneling & Excavation:** Hardness of constituent minerals directly controls **drill bit wear, advance rate, and TBM cutter consumption**.
2. **Slope Stability:** High specific gravity minerals (e.g., Magnetite, Hematite, Galena) form **heavy, dense rocks** contributing to **higher overburden stress** in deep open-pit mines.
3. **Aggregate Selection:** Cleavage, fracture, and tenacity of rock-forming minerals determine whether a rock is a **good, fair, or poor aggregate** for concrete (cleavage planes act as planes of weakness).
4. **Decorative Stones:** Luster, color, and transparency of minerals (e.g., Quartz → glass; Corundum → sapphire/ruby) are key for ornamental use.
5. **Ore Identification:** Streak + luster + specific gravity (the "**heft test**") quickly narrows ore minerals in field surveys.
6. **Geophysical Exploration:** Magnetic minerals (Magnetite, Pyrrhotite) cause **magnetic anomalies** in aeromagnetic surveys.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Specific Gravity Using Archimedes' Principle

The principle of flotation (Archimedes' Principle, ~250 BC) states that **the buoyant force on a submerged object equals the weight of the fluid displaced**. This is the foundation of the specific gravity measurement.

**Step 1:** Let the volume of the mineral sample be $V \text{ cm}^3$.

**Step 2:** The weight of the mineral in air is the gravitational force on the mineral mass:
$$W_{air} = m_{mineral} \cdot g = (\rho_{mineral} \cdot V) \cdot g$$

**Step 3:** When fully submerged in water, the buoyant force is:
$$F_{buoy} = \rho_{water} \cdot V \cdot g$$

**Step 4:** The apparent weight of the mineral in water (by the spring balance reading) is:
$$W_{water} = W_{air} - F_{buoy} = (\rho_{mineral} - \rho_{water}) \cdot V \cdot g$$

**Step 5:** The weight of the displaced water (equal to the buoyant force by Newton's third law) is:
$$W_{disp} = \rho_{water} \cdot V \cdot g$$

**Step 6:** The specific gravity is defined as the ratio of mineral weight to displaced water weight:
$$G = \frac{W_{air}}{W_{disp}} = \frac{\rho_{mineral} \cdot V \cdot g}{\rho_{water} \cdot V \cdot g}$$

**Step 7:** The $V$ and $g$ cancel, giving the clean result:
$$G = \frac{\rho_{mineral}}{\rho_{water}}$$

**Step 8:** At the standard reference temperature of 4°C, $\rho_{water} = 1 \text{ g/cm}^3$, so numerically:
$$G = \rho_{mineral} \quad (\text{in g/cm}^3)$$

**Step 9:** For a sample weighed in a laboratory spring balance:
$$G = \frac{W_{air}}{W_{air} - W_{submerged}}$$

Where $W_{submerged}$ is the apparent weight when the sample is fully immersed in water (suspended by a thread).

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Specific Gravity

**Problem (KTU Practice Style):**
A mineral sample weighs **$78.4 \text{ g}$ in air** and **$58.8 \text{ g}$ when fully immersed in water**. Calculate its specific gravity and identify the mineral.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Apply the formula:
$$G = \frac{W_{air}}{W_{air} - W_{water}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values:
$$G = \frac{78.4}{78.4 - 58.8}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the denominator:
$$78.4 - 58.8 = 19.6 \text{ g}$$

**Step 4:** Divide:
$$G = \frac{78.4}{19.6} = 4.00$$

**Step 5:** Compare with the mineral table:
- $G = 4.0$ corresponds to **Sphalerite (ZnS)**, which has $G \approx 3.9$ to $4.1$.

**Engineering Insight:** The **heft test** (simply "feeling" the weight in hand) would also tell a trained geologist that this is a heavy mineral, narrowing the field to sulfide or oxide ore minerals.

### 3.3 Mohs Hardness — Inter-Position Calculation Logic

Although Mohs is non-linear, students are sometimes asked the **logical sequence** of scratch testing.

**Procedure for Field Identification:**

1. Attempt to scratch the unknown mineral with **fingernail (H=2.5)**.
   - If fingernail scratches it: H < 2.5 → Try fingernail **on Talc** (H=1) and **on Gypsum** (H=2) for fine resolution.
   - If fingernail does NOT scratch: H ≥ 2.5 → Move to next step.

2. Attempt to scratch the unknown with a **copper coin (H=3.5)**.
   - Scratched by coin: H < 3.5 → Likely Calcite (H=3) or close.
   - Not scratched by coin: H ≥ 3.5 → Try glass.

3. Attempt to scratch the **glass plate (H=5.5)** with the unknown mineral.
   - Scratches glass: H > 5.5 → Try knife blade next.
   - Does not scratch glass: 3.5 ≤ H ≤ 5.5 → Likely Apatite (H=5) range.

4. **Compare to common mineral samples** carried in the field kit:
   - Quartz crystal (H=7)
   - Steel knife (H=5.5)
   - Glass plate (H=5.5)

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Caution:** Scratch testing creates powder. Always test on a **fresh, unweathered surface** and on a **discrete area** of the sample, as weathering alters hardness. Also, test by **trying to scratch the unknown with the known**, then **trying the known with the unknown** to confirm — sometimes, softer materials leave a streak that looks like a "scratch."

### 3.4 Cleavage Geometry — Deriving the Angle

Cleavage planes correspond to **planes of weakest atomic bonding** in the crystal lattice. The angle between two intersecting cleavage planes is determined by the **crystal symmetry**.

**Examples from Real Lattices:**

**A. Galena (PbS) — Cubic (Isometric) System**
- Galena has cubic symmetry, with three axes at 90° to each other and of equal length.
- Cleavage is parallel to the {100} planes (the cube faces).
- The angle between any two cleavage planes is therefore:
$$\theta_{cleavage} = 90°$$

**B. Calcite (CaCO₃) — Trigonal System**
- Calcite has 3-fold symmetry along the c-axis.
- Cleavage is parallel to the {10-11} rhombohedron faces.
- The cleavage angle (between cleavage faces) is:
$$\theta_{cleavage} = 74° 55' \text{ (or approximately } 75° \text{)}$$

This is a **diagnostic angle** for distinguishing Calcite from Dolomite (which has similar cleavage but $\theta \approx 73° 45'$).

**C. Amphibole (Hornblende) — Monoclinic System**
- Two prominent cleavages parallel to {110} prismatic planes.
- The intersection angle in the basal cross-section is:
$$\theta_{cleavage} = 56° \text{ and } 124° \text{ (i.e., } 60°/120° \text{ visually)}$$

**D. Pyroxene (Augite) — Monoclinic System**
- Two cleavages parallel to {110} but at a different intersection angle.
$$\theta_{cleavage} = 87° \text{ and } 93° \text{ (i.e., } 90° \text{ visually)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favorite Question:** *"Distinguish Pyroxene from Amphibole using cleavage angle."*
> **Answer:** Pyroxenes show two cleavages at ~87°/93° (≈ 90°); Amphiboles show two cleavages at ~56°/124° (≈ 60°/120°). Both cleavages meet at the **apex** in pyroxene and **do not meet** at the apex in amphibole (they're truncated by the basal plane).

### 3.5 Master Identification Algorithm (Decision Matrix)

The following decision matrix allows rapid field identification by sequentially testing physical properties:

```
Step 1: Check LUSTER
   ├── METALLIC → Go to Step 2
   └── NON-METALLIC → Go to Step 3

Step 2: (Metallic Minerals)
   ├── STREAK black + Hardness ~6 + SG ~5 → HEMATITE / MAGNETITE?
   │      └── Test with magnet: Magnetite attracts, Hematite does not.
   ├── STREAK greenish-black + Hardness ~6.5 + SG ~5 + Brassy yellow → PYRITE
   ├── STREAK lead-gray + Hardness ~2.5 + SG ~7.5 + Cubic cleavage → GALENA
   └── STREAK reddish-brown + Hardness ~5.5 + SG ~5 + Brassy + Tarnished → CHALCOPYRITE? No (chalcopyrite streak is greenish-black)

Step 3: (Non-Metallic Minerals)
   ├── Hardness = 1 (Greasy feel) → TALC
   ├── Hardness = 2 (Cleavable sheets) → GYPSUM / MUSCOVITE
   ├── Hardness = 3 (Rhombohedral cleavage, effervesces in HCl) → CALCITE
   ├── Hardness = 4 (Octahedral cleavage, fluorescent) → FLUORITE
   ├── Hardness = 5 (Hexagonal cross-section, dark green/brown) → APATITE
   ├── Hardness = 6 (Two cleavages at 90°, pink/white) → ORTHOCLASE
   ├── Hardness = 7 (Conchoidal fracture, no cleavage, glassy) → QUARTZ
   ├── Hardness = 6 (Striated, two cleavages at 90°, white/gray) → PLAGIOCLASE
   └── Hardness = 2.5-3 (Perfect basal cleavage, black/dark) → BIOTITE
```

This decision matrix is a **high-yield KTU 2024 expectation** — examiners often ask for a tabular identification key of common rock-forming minerals.

### 3.6 Master Summary Table: Common Rock-Forming Minerals

| Mineral | H | G | Cleavage | Luster | Streak | Color | Diagnostic |
|---------|---|---|----------|--------|--------|-------|------------|
| Quartz | 7 | 2.65 | None (Conchoidal fracture) | Vitreous | White | Colorless, varied | Glassy, no cleavage |
| Orthoclase | 6 | 2.55 | 2 @ 90° | Vitreous | White | Pink, white | Carlsbad twin, pink |
| Plagioclase | 6 | 2.65 | 2 @ 90° | Vitreous | White | White to dark | Polysynthetic twinning (striations) |
| Muscovite | 2-2.5 | 2.8 | 1 (basal, perfect) | Vitreous to Pearly | Colorless | Colorless/silver | Thin flexible sheets |
| Biotite | 2.5-3 | 3.0 | 1 (basal, perfect) | Vitreous | Colorless to gray | Black/dark brown | Black sheets, "salt-pepper" |
| Hornblende | 5-6 | 3.2 | 2 @ 60°/120° | Vitreous | White-green | Dark green to black | Amphibole cleavage |
| Augite | 5-6 | 3.4 | 2 @ 90° | Vitreous | White-green | Dark green to black | Pyroxene cleavage |
| Calcite | 3 | 2.72 | 3 (rhombohedral) | Vitreous | White | Colorless, white | Effervesces in HCl |
| Dolomite | 3.5-4 | 2.85 | 3 (rhombohedral) | Vitreous | White | Pink, white | Effervesces in warm HCl |
| Gypsum | 2 | 2.32 | 1 (perfect) | Vitreous to Silky | White | Colorless, white | Alabaster/Satin Spar varieties |
| Halite | 2.5 | 2.16 | 3 (cubic) | Vitreous | White | Colorless, white | Salty taste |
| Pyrite | 6-6.5 | 5.0 | None (Conchoidal) | Metallic | Greenish-black | Brassy yellow | "Fool's Gold" |
| Magnetite | 5.5-6.5 | 5.2 | None | Metallic to Sub-metallic | Black | Black | Strongly magnetic |
| Hematite | 5-6 | 5.2 | None | Metallic to Dull | Red-brown | Red to black | Red streak |
| Galena | 2.5 | 7.5 | 3 (cubic, perfect) | Metallic | Lead-gray | Lead-gray | Very heavy |
| Calcite vs. Quartz | Distinguishing Tests |
| Property | Calcite | Quartz |
| Hardness | 3 (scratched by nail) | 7 (scratches steel) |
| Cleavage | 3 directions (rhombs) | None (conchoidal) |
| Acid test | Effervesces vigorously | No reaction |
| Specific Gravity | 2.72 | 2.65 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mineral Identification Workflow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Unknown Mineral Sample] --> B{Step 1: Check LUSTER}
    B -->|Metallic / Sub-metallic| C{Step 2: Check STREAK}
    B -->|Non-metallic| D{Step 3: Check HARDNESS}
    C -->|Red-brown streak| E[HEMATITE]
    C -->|Black streak| F{Step 4: Test with MAGNET}
    C -->|Greenish-black streak| G[PYRITE]
    C -->|Lead-gray streak| H[GALENA: Check SG > 7]
    F -->|Magnetic| I[MAGNETITE]
    F -->|Non-magnetic| J[Possibly ILMENITE or HEMATITE ore]
    D -->|H less than 2.5| K[TALC / GYPSUM / MUSCOVITE]
    D -->|H between 2.5 and 5.5| L{Step 5: Check CLEAVAGE}
    D -->|H greater than 5.5| M{Step 6: Check CLEAVAGE}
    L -->|3 directions, rhombs| N[CALCITE - Confirm with HCl effervescence]
    L -->|1 direction, sheets| O[BIOTITE / MUSCOVITE]
    M -->|None, conchoidal fracture| P[QUARTZ: Confirm H=7, glassy]
    M -->|2 directions at 90| Q{Step 7: Check TWINNING / STRIATIONS}
    Q -->|Pink, simple twin| R[ORTHOCLASE]
    Q -->|White-gray, striated| S[PLAGIOCLASE]
    M -->|2 directions at 60 or 120| T[AMPHIBOLE - Hornblende]
```

### 4.2 Mohs Hardness Scale (Mermaid Comparative Ladder)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1[1 Talc - Softest<br>Fingernail] -->|Scratched by| H2[2 Gypsum]
    H2 -->|Scratched by| H3[3 Calcite]
    H3 -->|Scratched by| H4[4 Fluorite]
    H4 -->|Scratched by| H5[5 Apatite]
    H5 -->|Scratched by| H6[6 Orthoclase Feldspar<br>Steel Knife]
    H6 -->|Scratched by| H7[7 Quartz<br>Glass Plate]
    H7 -->|Scratched by| H8[8 Topaz]
    H8 -->|Scratched by| H9[9 Corundum]
    H9 -->|Scratched by| H10[10 Diamond - Hardest]
```

### 4.3 Cleavage Geometry Comparison (Mermaid Schematic)

```mermaid
subgraph cleavage_diagrams
    direction TB
    fig1[1 DIRECTION - Basal<br>Mica sheets] --> fig1a[Planes parallel to base]
    fig2[2 DIRECTIONS at 90 deg<br>Pyroxene / Feldspar] --> fig2a[Rectangular prisms]
    fig3[2 DIRECTIONS at 60-120 deg<br>Amphibole] --> fig3a[Hexagonal cross-section]
    fig4[3 DIRECTIONS at 90 deg CUBIC<br>Galena / Halite] --> fig4a[Cube forms]
    fig5[3 DIRECTIONS RHOMBOHEDRAL<br>Calcite / Dolomite] --> fig5a[Rhombohedron forms]
    fig6[4 DIRECTIONS OCTAHEDRAL<br>Fluorite / Diamond] --> fig6a[Octahedron forms]
end
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L[LUSTER] --> L1{Metallic?}
    L1 -->|Yes| L1A[Sub-metallic<br>Hematite, Chromite]
    L1 -->|No| L2[Non-metallic sub-classes]
    L2 --> L2A[Vitreous - Glass-like<br>Quartz, Calcite]
    L2 --> L2B[Resinous - Resin-like<br>Sphalerite, Sulfur]
```

```mermaid
    AGG[Aggregate Mineral Properties] --> A1{Mica Content?}
    A1 -->|High| A2[Reject - Cleavage planes reduce shear strength]
    A1 -->|Low| A3{Cleavage Type?}

    A4 --> A5
    A2 --> A6[Use Mica-poor aggregate<br>or alternative source]
    A5 --> A7{Specific Gravity Check}

    A7 -->|G greater than 3.0| A9[Heavyweight aggregate for radiation shielding]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "Mineral" and list any four physical properties used for its identification.**

**Model Answer:**

A mineral is a naturally occurring, inorganic, homogeneous crystalline solid with a definite (or definable) chemical composition and an ordered atomic arrangement, formed by geological processes.

**Four physical properties used for identification:**

1. **Hardness** — Resistance to scratching (Mohs scale 1 to 10).
2. **Streak** — Color of the powdered mineral on a streak plate.
3. **Cleavage** — Tendency to split along smooth, flat, parallel planes of atomic weakness.
4. **Specific Gravity** — Ratio of mineral weight to weight of equal volume of water.

*Other acceptable answers: Color, Luster, Fracture, Crystal Form, Tenacity, Magnetism, Transparency, Diaphaneity.*

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse **color** with **streak**. Hematite is black in hand specimen but has a **reddish-brown streak**. Mentioning streak instead of (or alongside) color earns higher marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**What is the Mohs scale of hardness? Arrange the following minerals in increasing order of hardness: Calcite, Quartz, Talc, Topaz, Orthoclase, Diamond, Gypsum, Apatite, Corundum, Fluorite.**

**Model Answer:**

The Mohs scale of hardness, devised by **Friedrich Mohs in 1812**, is a relative ordinal scale from 1 to 10 used to quantify a mineral's resistance to scratching. A mineral with hardness 'n' can scratch all minerals of hardness < n, and is scratched by all minerals of hardness > n.

**Arranged in increasing order of hardness:**

1. Talc (H = 1)
2. Gypsum (H = 2)
3. Calcite (H = 3)
4. Fluorite (H = 4)
5. Apatite (H = 5)
6. Orthoclase (H = 6)
7. Quartz (H = 7)
8. Topaz (H = 8)
9. Corundum (H = 9)
10. Diamond (H = 10)

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Writing "Diamond is 10 times harder than Talc" is **factually wrong**. The Mohs scale is **non-linear**; diamond is approximately 1600 times harder than talc in absolute (Vickers) hardness.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the physical properties "Cleavage" and "Fracture" of minerals, with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the Mohs scale of hardness. Discuss its limitations. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Cleavage:**

Cleavage is the tendency of a crystalline mineral to break along smooth, flat, parallel planes of weak atomic bonding. These planes correspond to crystallographic directions of low bond strength.

**Described by 3 parameters:**

1. **Number of directions** of cleavage.
2. **Quality** (perfect, good, fair, poor) — judged by ease and smoothness.
3. **Angle** between intersecting cleavage planes.

**Examples of cleavage patterns:**

| Cleavage Type | Geometry | Mineral Examples |
|---------------|----------|------------------|
| 1 direction, basal | Parallel sheets | Mica (Muscovite, Biotite), Graphite, Talc |
| 2 directions @ 90° | Rectangular cross-section | Orthoclase, Plagioclase Feldspar, Pyroxene (Augite) |
| 2 directions @ 60°/120° | Hexagonal cross-section | Hornblende (Amphibole) |
| 3 directions @ 90° (cubic) | Cube | Galena, Halite |
| 3 directions (rhombohedral) @ 75° | Rhomb | Calcite, Dolomite |
| 4 directions (octahedral) | Octahedron | Fluorite, Diamond |
| 6 directions (dodecahedral) | Dodecahedron | Sphalerite |

**Engineering Significance:** Cleavage controls the **drillability, breakage pattern, and strength anisotropy** of rocks. Mica-rich rocks (e.g., Schist) split easily along foliation due to aligned mica cleavage, which is a critical consideration in **tunnel support design and slope stability**.

**Fracture:**

Fracture is the breakage of a mineral along **surfaces other than cleavage planes**, i.e., along random or non-preferred crystallographic directions.

**Types of fracture:**

1. **Conchoidal** — Smooth, curved, concentric shell-like surfaces, often with sharp edges. *Example: Quartz, Obsidian (volcanic glass), Flint.*
2. **Fibrous / Splintery** — Splinter-like projections. *Example: Siderite, some Amphiboles, Chrysotile Asbestos.*
3. **Hackly** — Jagged surfaces with sharp, irregular points. *Example: Native Copper, Native Silver, Native Gold.*
4. **Uneven / Irregular** — Rough, random surfaces. *Example: Most sulfide ores (Pyrite, Galena when broken non-cleavage).*
5. **Earthy** — Crumbly, soil-like break. *Example: Limonite, Bauxite, Kaolinite (clay minerals).*

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagnostic point:** Quartz shows **excellent conchoidal fracture** and **no cleavage** — a unique combination. Prehistoric humans used this property to knap flint tools.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**The Mohs Scale of Hardness:**

The Mohs scale of hardness, developed by Austrian mineralogist **Friedrich Mohs in 1812**, is a **relative ordinal scale** ranking ten reference minerals by their resistance to scratching, from 1 (softest) to 10 (hardest).

**The Ten Reference Minerals:**

| # | Mineral | Field Test |
|---|---------|------------|
| 1 | Talc | Crushed by fingernail |
| 2 | Gypsum | Scratched by fingernail |
| 3 | Calcite | Scratched by copper coin |
| 4 | Fluorite | Scratched by glass easily |
| 5 | Apatite | Scratched by knife with difficulty |
| 6 | Orthoclase | Scratches glass; scratched by knife |
| 7 | Quartz | Scratches steel and glass easily |
| 8 | Topaz | Scratches quartz |
| 9 | Corundum | Scratches topaz |
| 10 | Diamond | Scratches everything |

**Principle:** If mineral X scratches mineral Y, then H(X) > H(Y). Conversely, if Y scratches X, then H(Y) > H(X). Equality results in mutual scratching (or neither scratches the other).

**Limitations of Mohs Scale:**

1. **Non-linear / Ordinal nature:** Mohs 10 is NOT 10 times harder than Mohs 1. Diamond is ~1600 times harder than Talc in absolute Vickers hardness. The scale understates the gap between hard and very-hard minerals.
2. **Large gaps:** The interval between Corundum (9) and Diamond (10) is enormous (VHN: 400 to 1600), but Mohs treats it as a single step.
3. **Directional variation:** Some minerals (e.g., Kyanite) have different hardness on different crystallographic directions: **H = 4-5 parallel to length, H = 6-7 across length**. Mohs cannot capture this.
4. **Subjective and rough:** The scale uses scratch testing, which is qualitative and depends on applied force, freshness of surface, and operator judgment.
5. **Not applicable to fine-grained aggregates:** Cannot measure individual mineral hardness within a fine-grained rock.
6. **Single hardness value per mineral:** Cannot distinguish between polymorphs with same Mohs but different actual hardness.

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners love asking the **non-linear nature** of Mohs scale as a follow-up. Always mention at least **3 limitations** in a 7-mark answer.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** What is Specific Gravity? Explain the experimental method to determine the specific gravity of a mineral sample. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the physical properties used to distinguish the following mineral pairs: **(7 Marks)**
   - (i) Calcite and Quartz
   - (ii) Pyrite and Galena
   - (iii) Muscovite and Biotite

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Definition:**

**Specific gravity (G)** of a mineral is defined as the **ratio of the weight of the mineral to the weight of an equal volume of water at 4°C**.

$$G = \frac{W_{air}}{W_{water}} = \frac{\rho_{mineral}}{\rho_{water}}$$

It is a **dimensionless** quantity. At 4°C, $\rho_{water} = 1 \text{ g/cm}^3$, so numerically G equals the mineral's density in g/cm³.

**Experimental Method (Spring Balance / Archimedes Method):**

**Apparatus Required:**
- Spring balance (sensitive, 0.1 g least count)
- Thread (fine, non-stretchable)
- Beaker with water
- Mineral sample (~50-100 g)
- Thermometer

**Procedure:**

1. **Weigh the mineral in air:** Suspend the clean, dry mineral sample from the spring balance using a fine thread. Record the weight:
$$W_1 = W_{air}$$

2. **Weigh the mineral in water:** Immerse the sample completely in a beaker of water, ensuring the sample does not touch the beaker walls or base. Wait until the reading stabilizes. Record the apparent weight:
$$W_2 = W_{submerged}$$

3. **Compute the specific gravity:**
$$G = \frac{W_1}{W_1 - W_2}$$

**Precautions:**

- Use **distilled water** to avoid ion interference.
- Ensure **no air bubbles** cling to the sample (boil or brush if needed).
- Maintain water at **standard temperature** (4°C for theoretical, room temperature acceptable in practice with small correction).
- For **porous minerals** (e.g., Pumice, some Limestones), the sample must be **pre-saturated** (soaked in water and surface-dried) to prevent water absorption during the test, which would otherwise give an **erroneously high G**.
- For **soluble minerals** (e.g., Halite, Sylvite), use a **non-aqueous liquid** of known specific gravity (e.g., kerosene, toluene).

**Engineering Significance:** Specific gravity is used to calculate the **unit weight of rock masses** for **slope stability, foundation bearing capacity, and overburden stress** in mining and tunneling.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**(i) Calcite vs. Quartz:**

| Property | Calcite (CaCO₃) | Quartz (SiO₂) |
|----------|-----------------|---------------|
| Hardness | 3 (scratched by fingernail) | 7 (scratches steel) |
| Cleavage | 3 directions, rhombohedral, perfect | None; conchoidal fracture |
| Reaction with dilute HCl | **Vigorous effervescence** (CO₂ released) | No reaction |
| Specific Gravity | 2.72 | 2.65 |
| Crystal Form | Rhombohedral, scalenohedral | Hexagonal prisms with pyramids |
| Common Color | White, colorless, pale shades | Colorless, white, varied (amethyst, smoky) |

**Diagnostic test:** The **acid test** is decisive — Calcite effervesces vigorously with cold dilute HCl, while Quartz shows no reaction.

**(ii) Pyrite vs. Galena:**

| Property | Pyrite (FeS₂) | Galena (PbS) |
|----------|---------------|--------------|
| Hardness | 6 to 6.5 | 2.5 (scratched by fingernail) |
| Color | Brassy yellow | Lead-gray |
| Streak | Greenish-black to black | Lead-gray (shiny) |
| Specific Gravity | 5.0 | 7.5 (very heavy) |
| Cleavage | None; conchoidal fracture | 3 directions, cubic, perfect |
| Crystal Form | Cubic (often pyritohedron) | Cubic |
| Luster | Metallic | Metallic |

**Diagnostic test:** **Streak and Specific Gravity**. Pyrite's greenish-black streak and G = 5.0 differ markedly from Galena's lead-gray streak and G = 7.5 (the heft test alone can distinguish them — Galena feels distinctly heavier).

**(iii) Muscovite vs. Biotite:**

| Property | Muscovite (KAl₃Si₃O₁₀(OH)₂) | Biotite (K(Mg,Fe)₃AlSi₃O₁₀(OH)₂) |
|----------|------------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| Color | Colorless, silver, light tints | Black, dark brown, dark green |
| Streak | Colorless | Colorless to grayish |
| Luster | Vitreous to pearly (more pearly) | Vitreous to submetallic (splendent) |
| Hardness | 2 to 2.5 | 2.5 to 3 |
| Cleavage | 1 perfect basal | 1 perfect basal |
| Specific Gravity | 2.8 to 3.0 | 3.0 to 3.4 |
| Tenacity | Elastic (flexible sheets) | Elastic (flexible sheets) |
| Composition | Al-rich, no Fe²⁺ | Mg-Fe-rich, contains Fe²⁺ |

**Diagnostic test:** **Color** alone is the quickest field distinction — Muscovite is **light-colored (silvery/pale)**, Biotite is **dark (black/brown)**. Both belong to the mica group and share perfect basal cleavage and elastic sheets.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students confuse **cleavage** with **fracture** when describing the pairs. Always state: *"Calcite shows cleavage (3 directions, rhombs); Quartz shows fracture (conchoidal, no cleavage)."* This phrasing earns full credit.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Common Mistakes Students Make in Mineralogy (Module 2):**
>
> 1. **Conflating color and streak.** Color is variable; streak is diagnostic. "Hematite is black" is **incomplete** — you must add "but has a reddish-brown streak."
> 2. **Stating Mohs 10 = 10× Mohs 1.** This is a **factual error**. Mohs is non-linear.
> 3. **Confusing cleavage with fracture.** Cleavage = smooth, flat, parallel planes; fracture = irregular surfaces.
> 4. **Forgetting the acid test for Calcite.** Calcite is the only common mineral that effervesces vigorously with cold dilute HCl. This is a **free 1-mark bonus** if mentioned.
> 5. **Stating "Pyrite is fool's gold" without properties.** Always justify with: brassy yellow color, greenish-black streak, H = 6.5, G = 5.0, NO magnetism (unlike gold, which is malleable and has G = 19.3).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Mineral Definition:** Naturally occurring, inorganic, homogeneous, crystalline solid with definite composition and ordered atomic structure.
- [ ] **Mohs Scale:** 1-Talc, 2-Gypsum, 3-Calcite, 4-Fluorite, 5-Apatite, 6-Orthoclase, 7-Quartz, 8-Topaz, 9-Corundum, 10-Diamond. **Non-linear, ordinal.**
- [ ] **Field Hardness Tools:** Fingernail = 2.5, Copper coin = 3.5, Steel knife/Glass = 5.5, Quartz = 7.
- [ ] **Streak > Color** for diagnostic reliability. Harder minerals than streak plate (>6.5) leave no streak.
- [ ] **Luster:** Metallic, Sub-metallic, Vitreous, Resinous, Pearly, Silky, Greasy, Adamantine, Dull.
- [ ] **Cleavage vs. Fracture:** Cleavage = smooth, flat, atomic weakness planes. Fracture = random breakage. Quartz = no cleavage, conchoidal fracture.
- [ ] **Cleavage Angles:**
  - Pyroxene = 87°/93° (≈ 90°)
  - Amphibole = 56°/124° (≈ 60°/120°)
  - Calcite = 75° (rhombohedral)
- [ ] **Specific Gravity Formula:** $G = \frac{W_{air}}{W_{air} - W_{submerged}}$ (Archimedes' principle).
- [ ] **SG Categories:** Light < 2.5, Average 2.5-3.0, Heavy 3.0-5.0, Very Heavy > 5.0.
- [ ] **Diagnostic Pair Distinctions:**
  - Calcite (H=3, fizzes in HCl) vs. Quartz (H=7, no reaction).
  - Pyrite (H=6.5, greenish-black streak, G=5) vs. Galena (H=2.5, lead-gray streak, G=7.5).
  - Muscovite (light) vs. Biotite (dark) — both micas with perfect basal cleavage.
- [ ] **Magnetism:** Magnetite is the only strongly magnetic common mineral; deflects compass needle.
- [ ] **Tenacity Types:** Brittle, Malleable, Ductile, Sectile, Flexible, Elastic, Plastic.
- [ ] **Mica Tenacity:** **Elastic** — bends and springs back (not just "flexible").
- [ ] **Native metals:** Gold (G=19.3, malleable, yellow streak), Silver, Copper, Platinum.
- [ ] **Fool's Gold = Pyrite:** Brassy yellow but has greenish-black streak, no malleability.
- [ ] **Effervescence with HCl:** Calcite = vigorous cold, Dolomite = only when powdered/warm, Quartz = no reaction.
- [ ] **Engineering Applications:** Hardness → drillability, Cleavage → rock anisotropy, Specific Gravity → slope stability unit weight, Magnetism → geophysical exploration, Streak → ore identification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** For 14-mark questions on mineralogy, structure your answer as: (1) **Definition**, (2) **Principle/Theory**, (3) **Types/Classification with examples** (use a table!), (4) **Engineering significance**, (5) **Diagrams/sketches** where applicable. This format aligns with KTU's Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Apply, Analyze, Evaluate levels) and consistently scores 12-14 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physical Properties and Chemical Composition of Minerals like Quartz
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET416 - ENGINEERING GEOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Physical Properties and Chemical Composition of Minerals like Quartz**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Mineral? (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)

A **mineral** is a naturally occurring, inorganic, homogeneous solid substance with a definite (but not fixed) chemical composition and a highly ordered atomic arrangement (crystalline structure). It is the fundamental building block of rocks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Criteria — The "5 D's of a Mineral":**
> 1. **Naturally occurring** (not man-made)
> 2. **Inorganic** (not formed by living organisms)
> 3. **Solid** (under normal Earth surface conditions)
> 4. **Homogeneous** (uniform chemical composition throughout)
> 5. **Definite crystalline structure** (ordered atomic arrangement)

> [!NOTE]
> **Amorphous exception:** Some natural solids like *opal* (SiO₂·nH₂O) and *mercury* (liquid) are sometimes debated, but true minerals are strictly crystalline solids.

## 1.2 What are Physical Properties of Minerals?

**Physical properties** are the observable, measurable, and testable characteristics of a mineral that allow geologists to identify it in hand specimen (without advanced laboratory equipment). These properties arise directly from the mineral's **internal crystal structure** and **chemical composition**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Geology Significance:** For civil engineers, identifying minerals like quartz, feldspar, mica, and calcite is crucial because they directly influence the **strength, durability, weathering behavior, and load-bearing capacity** of rocks used as foundation material, aggregate, and building stone.

## 1.3 The Reference Mineral — QUARTZ

**Quartz** is the most abundant mineral in the Earth's continental crust (~12% by volume). It serves as the standard example for studying physical properties because:
- It is extremely common in igneous, sedimentary, and metamorphic rocks.
- It is chemically simple (only **two elements**: Si and O).
- Its properties are well-defined and easily testable in the field.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quartz Chemical Formula:** **SiO₂** (Silicon Dioxide)
> **Chemical Composition by Weight:** Silicon (Si) = 46.7%, Oxygen (O) = 53.3%

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — "Mineral Identification Like a Doctor's Check-up"

Think of identifying a mineral just like a doctor diagnosing a patient:

| Doctor's Test | Mineral Property Tested |
|---|---|
| Checking skin color | **Color** of the mineral |
| Listening to heart | **Density / Specific Gravity** |
| Testing reflexes | **Hardness** (scratch test) |
| X-ray imaging | **Crystal form / structure** |
| Blood test | **Chemical composition** |

Just as a doctor uses multiple indicators to confirm a diagnosis, a geologist uses a *combination* of physical properties to confirm the identity of a mineral — no single property is ever diagnostic on its own!

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Physical Properties of Minerals

Physical properties are broadly classified into two groups based on how they are determined:

### A. Megascopic Properties (Observable by Naked Eye / Hand Lens)
These can be determined in the field without instruments:
- **Color**
- **Streak**
- **Luster**
- **Hardness**
- **Cleavage**
- **Fracture**
- **Crystal Form / Habit**
- **Specific Gravity**
- **Transparency**
- **Tenacity**
- **Feel / Touch**
- **Magnetism**
- **Smell / Odor**
- **Taste**

### B. Microscopic / Optical Properties (Require Petrographic Microscope)
- Refractive Index
- Birefringence
- Pleochroism
- Extinction angle
- Interference colors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Focus:** The 2024 syllabus emphasizes **megascopic identification** — the properties you can determine with a hand lens, knife, streak plate, and dilute HCl. Microscopic techniques are covered separately.

## 2.2 Detailed Description of Each Property (with Quartz as Example)

### 2.2.1 Color
- The visible color observed on a fresh surface of the mineral.
- Caused by the **absorption of certain wavelengths of light** due to trace elements (chromophores).
- For Quartz: Colorless when pure; colored varieties exist due to impurities:
  - **Amethyst** — purple (Fe³⁺ impurity + natural irradiation)
  - **Citrine** — yellow-brown (Fe³⁺ impurity)
  - **Rose Quartz** — pink (Ti, Fe, Mn traces; or micro-fibers of dumortierite)
  - **Smoky Quartz** — brown-black (natural irradiation of Al impurities)
  - **Milky Quartz** — white (tiny fluid inclusions)
  - **Rock Crystal** — colorless (pure SiO₂)

> [!WARNING]
> **Why color is the LEAST reliable property:** Quartz comes in 7+ colors. The same mineral can have multiple colors, and different minerals can share the same color. Always confirm with streak and hardness.

### 2.2.2 Streak
- The color of the mineral in its **powdered form**, obtained by rubbing the mineral across a **streak plate** (unglazed porcelain, Mohs hardness ~7).
- More reliable than color because powdering removes surface weathering effects.
- **Quartz streak = White** (always, regardless of body color).

### 2.2.3 Luster
- The quality and intensity of light **reflected from a fresh mineral surface** (NOT the color of the mineral itself).
- Two main categories:
  - **Metallic Luster:** Looks like polished metal (e.g., Galena, Pyrite, Magnetite).
  - **Non-Metallic Luster:** Subdivided into:
    - *Vitreous* — glassy (Quartz ✓)
    - *Resinous* — like resin (Sphalerite)
    - *Pearly* — like pearl (Talc, Muscovite)
    - *Silky* — like silk (Satin Spar, fibrous Gypsum)
    - *Greasy* — feels soapy/greasy (Nepheline, some Quartz)
    - *Adamantine* — brilliant diamond-like (Diamond, Corundum)
    - *Dull / Earthy* — no shine (Limonite, Kaolinite)

> [!NOTE]
> **Quartz Luster:** **Vitreous (glassy)** on fresh conchoidal surfaces.

### 2.2.4 Hardness (H)
- The **resistance of a mineral to scratching**, defined relative to the **Mohs Scale of Hardness** (a relative, non-linear scale from 1 to 10).
- Determined using the **scratch test**: a harder mineral scratches a softer one.
- **Quartz Hardness = 7** (can scratch glass and steel).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mohs Scale of Hardness (KTU-MUST):**
>
> | Mohs No. | Reference Mineral | Field Test |
> |---|---|---|
> | 1 | Talc | Scratched by fingernail |
> | 2 | Gypsum | Scratched by fingernail (~2.5) |
> | 3 | Calcite | Scratched by copper coin (~3.5) |
> | 4 | Fluorite | Scratched by steel knife (~5.5) |
> | 5 | Apatite | Scratched by steel knife |
> | 6 | Orthoclase | Scratches glass |
> | 7 | **QUARTZ** | Scratches glass & steel easily |
> | 8 | Topaz | — |
> | 9 | Corundum | — |
> | 10 | Diamond | — |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering memory trick:** "Talc Gneiss, Calcite Climbs, Feldspar Flints, Quartz Cuts." (Or simply remember: fingernail = 2.5, coin = 3.5, knife = 5.5, glass = 5.5–6.)

### 2.2.5 Cleavage
- The tendency of a mineral to split along **smooth, flat, planar surfaces** of weak atomic bonding.
- Described by **number of directions** and **quality** (perfect, good, poor).
- **Quartz has NO cleavage.** (Atoms are bonded with equal strength in all directions — Si–O tetrahedra form a 3D network.)

### 2.2.6 Fracture
- The way a mineral breaks when it does **NOT** follow cleavage planes.
- Types:
  - **Conchoidal** — smooth, curved, shell-like (Quartz ✓, Obsidian)
  - **Fibrous / Splintery**
  - **Hackly** — jagged with sharp edges
  - **Uneven / Irregular**
  - **Earthy**

> [!NOTE]
> **Quartz Fracture = Conchoidal.** This is why ancient humans used quartz to make sharp tools and arrowheads!

### 2.2.7 Specific Gravity (G or ρ)
- The ratio of the mineral's density to the density of water at 4°C.
- $$G = \frac{\rho_{mineral}}{\rho_{water}} = \frac{W_{air}}{W_{air} - W_{water}}$$
- **Quartz Specific Gravity = 2.65** (a key diagnostic value).

### 2.2.8 Crystal Form / Habit
- The external geometric shape of a mineral reflecting its internal atomic arrangement.
- **Quartz crystal system = Trigonal (Rhombohedral)**
- Common habit: **Hexagonal prismatic crystals terminated by rhombohedral faces** (well-known six-sided prismatic crystals with pyramidal terminations).
- Massive forms: granular, cryptocrystalline (Chalcedony).

### 2.2.9 Other Properties of Quartz
- **Transparency:** Transparent to translucent
- **Tenacity:** Brittle
- **Magnetism:** None
- **Reaction with HCl:** No effervescence (quartz is non-calcareous)
- **Optical sign:** Uniaxial positive

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Mineral Properties)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table — it is the most tested content for Module 2.**

| Sl. No. | Property | Test Method | Value for Quartz | Engineering Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Chemical Formula | Chemical analysis | **SiO₂** | Determines weathering behavior |
| 2 | Crystal System | Crystallography | **Trigonal** | Affects cleavage & fracture |
| 3 | Hardness | Mohs scratch test | **7** | Aggregate suitability for concrete |
| 4 | Specific Gravity | Jolly's balance / heavy liquids | **2.65** | Foundation design, rock density |
| 5 | Color | Visual observation | Variable (colorless to many) | Aesthetic (building stone) |
| 6 | Streak | Streak plate | **White** | Diagnostic identifier |
| 7 | Luster | Visual (fresh surface) | **Vitreous** | Quick field identification |
| 8 | Cleavage | Hammer test | **Absent (None)** | Determines breakage pattern |
| 9 | Fracture | Hammer test | **Conchoidal** | Tool-making, aggregate shape |
| 10 | Transparency | Visual | Transparent to translucent | Optical quartz uses |
| 11 | Tenacity | Manual pressure | **Brittle** | Crushing behavior |
| 12 | Reaction with HCl | 10% HCl drop | **No effervescence** | Distinguishes from calcite |
| 13 | Magnetism | Magnet test | **Non-magnetic** | Distinguishes from magnetite |
| 14 | Pleochroism | Polarizing microscope | None (uniaxial) | Microscopic ID |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why engineers MUST know mineral properties:**
> 1. **Aggregate Selection for Concrete:** Quartz-rich aggregates (granite) are hard (H=7), durable, and chemically inert — excellent for high-strength concrete.
> 2. **Tunnel & Dam Foundation:** Rocks rich in quartz withstand high stress; micaceous rocks may cause slippage.
> 3. **Weathering Resistance:** Quartz is highly resistant to chemical weathering → quartzite and quartz-rich sandstones are excellent building stones.
> 4. **Decay of Concrete (Alkali-Silica Reaction):** Reactive silica in quartz aggregates can react with cement alkalis → engineers must select non-reactive quartz varieties.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Calculating Specific Gravity of Quartz

A sample of quartz weighs **65.2 g in air**. When suspended in water, it weighs **40.6 g**. Calculate its specific gravity and comment on whether this matches the standard value.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify the given data.**

$$W_{air} = 65.2 \text{ g}$$

$$W_{water} = 40.6 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2 — Recall the formula for specific gravity using Archimedes' principle.**

$$G = \frac{W_{air}}{W_{air} - W_{water}}$$

> This formula works because $(W_{air} - W_{water})$ equals the **weight of water displaced**, which equals the **buoyant force**. By Archimedes' principle, this equals the volume of the sample (in cm³, since ρ_water = 1 g/cm³).

**Step 3 — Calculate the apparent loss of weight in water (i.e., the buoyant force).**

$$W_{air} - W_{water} = 65.2 - 40.6 = 24.6 \text{ g}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the specific gravity formula.**

$$G = \frac{65.2}{24.6} = 2.6504 \approx 2.65$$

**Step 5 — Comment on the result.**

Since the calculated value **G = 2.65 matches the standard specific gravity of quartz (2.65)**, the mineral is confirmed to be **quartz**. Any deviation > ±0.02 would suggest an incorrect identification or the presence of impurities.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Mohs Hardness Identification Problem

A mineral specimen has the following properties:
- Color: milky white
- Streak: white
- Hardness: **scratches glass easily, but is scratched by topaz**
- Luster: vitreous
- Cleavage: none
- Fracture: conchoidal
- Crystal form: hexagonal prismatic

**Identify the mineral with justification.**

### Step-by-Step Identification:

**Step 1 — Analyze hardness using Mohs scale logic.**

From the given data:
- Scratches glass (H > 5.5)
- Scratched by topaz (H < 8)
- Therefore: **5.5 < H < 8**

> Narrowing this down, the standard mineral with H = 7 in this range is **quartz** (or possibly a close variety).

**Step 2 — Cross-check using cleavage.**

The mineral has **no cleavage** → eliminates feldspar (H=6, has cleavage), calcite (H=3, has cleavage), topaz (H=8, has perfect cleavage).

**Step 3 — Cross-check using fracture.**

**Conchoidal fracture** is highly characteristic of quartz (and obsidian).

**Step 4 — Cross-check using luster.**

**Vitreous luster** is the typical luster of quartz.

**Step 5 — Cross-check using crystal form.**

**Hexagonal prismatic crystals** with pyramidal terminations are the diagnostic crystal habit of quartz (trigonal system).

**Conclusion:**

> All seven properties are consistent with **QUARTZ (SiO₂)**. Identification: **Confirmed as Quartz**.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Distinguishing Quartz from Calcite in the Field

Both quartz and calcite are common in rocks and may appear similar. Show how a geologist distinguishes them using a single test, with full chemical justification.

### Step-by-Step Comparison:

| Property | Quartz (SiO₂) | Calcite (CaCO₃) | Distinguishing Test |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hardness | 7 | 3 | **Quartz scratches calcite; calcite scratched by fingernail** |
| Reaction with HCl | No reaction | **Violent effervescence** (CO₂ released) | **HCl drop test** — calcite fizzes |
| Cleavage | None | Perfect rhombohedral (3 directions) | Breakage pattern |
| Specific Gravity | 2.65 | 2.71 | Heavy liquids test |
| Luster | Vitreous | Vitreous to dull | Indistinguishable — don't use this |

**The HCl Test — Chemical Justification:**

When dilute HCl reacts with calcite:

$$CaCO_3 + 2HCl \rightarrow CaCl_2 + H_2O + CO_2 \uparrow$$

The escaping **CO₂ gas** produces visible bubbling/effervescence.

For quartz, there is **no carbonate group (CO₃²⁻)** in its structure, so no reaction occurs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Field Tip:** Cold dilute HCl (10%) produces vigorous effervescence in calcite. Quartz shows **zero effervescence** — this single test confirms identity instantly.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Identification Logic Flow

The following logic matrix is the systematic way a geologist identifies an unknown mineral in the field:

| Step | Test Conducted | Observation → Conclusion |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Observe color | Gives initial clue; classify as light/dark |
| 2 | Test streak on streak plate | Distinguishes metallic vs non-metallic minerals |
| 3 | Observe luster on fresh surface | Metallic / Vitreous / Pearly / etc. |
| 4 | Scratch test using knife, glass, fingernail | Determine Mohs hardness range |
| 5 | Hammer test (or rotate under light) | Determine cleavage vs fracture |
| 6 | Heft in hand / heavy liquid test | Estimate specific gravity |
| 7 | HCl drop test | Carbonate vs non-carbonate |
| 8 | Magnet test | Magnetic vs non-magnetic |
| 9 | Observe crystal form | Trigonal / Cubic / Hexagonal / etc. |
| 10 | Confirm using all properties together | Final identification |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mandate:** Never identify a mineral based on a single property. Use the **"Total Property Approach"** — at least 4–5 matching properties are required for confident identification.

### Q1. List any six physical properties used to identify minerals in hand specimen. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The six physical properties used for megascopic identification of minerals are:

1. **Color** — the visible color of the mineral on a fresh surface.
2. **Streak** — the color of the mineral in powdered form (tested on a streak plate).
3. **Luster** — the quality of light reflected from a fresh surface (vitreous, metallic, pearly, etc.).
4. **Hardness** — resistance to scratching, measured on the Mohs scale (1 to 10).
5. **Cleavage** — the tendency to split along smooth, flat planes of weak bonding.
6. **Specific Gravity** — the ratio of the mineral's density to that of water.

*(Other acceptable properties: fracture, crystal form, tenacity, magnetism, transparency, reaction with HCl.)*
