## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper, Module 2]**

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. (a)** Describe the physical properties of quartz in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Quartz (SiO₂) is the most abundant mineral in the Earth's continental crust. Its physical properties are described below:

**1. Color:**
Quartz is colorless when pure (variety: *rock crystal*). It occurs in various colors due to trace impurities: violet *amethyst* (Fe³⁺), yellow *citrine* (Fe³⁺), pink *rose quartz* (Ti/Mn), brown *smoky quartz* (Al + irradiation), and white *milky quartz* (fluid inclusions).

**2. Streak:**
The streak of quartz is **white** and is constant regardless of the body color, making it a highly reliable diagnostic property.

**3. Luster:**
Quartz exhibits a **vitreous (glassy) luster** on fresh conchoidal surfaces. Some massive varieties show a greasy luster.

**4. Hardness:**
Quartz has a Mohs hardness of **7**. It can scratch glass, steel, and most common minerals. This makes it extremely durable as an aggregate and a building stone.

**5. Cleavage:**
Quartz shows **no cleavage**. The strong 3D network of Si–O covalent bonds does not have any preferential plane of weakness.

**6. Fracture:**
Quartz displays a characteristic **conchoidal fracture** (smooth, curved, shell-like surfaces). This property made it valuable historically for making sharp tools.

**7. Specific Gravity:**
The specific gravity of quartz is **2.65**, a standard reference value used in the identification of many silicate rocks.

**8. Crystal Form:**
Quartz crystallizes in the **trigonal (rhombohedral) system**. It commonly forms **hexagonal prismatic crystals** terminated by **rhombohedral faces** (pyramidal terminations).

**9. Other Properties:**
- Transparency: Transparent to translucent
- Tenacity: Brittle
- Optical: Uniaxial positive, no pleochroism
- Reaction with HCl: No effervescence (non-calcareous)
- Magnetism: Non-magnetic

---

**Q.A. (b)** Explain the Mohs scale of hardness. How is it used in the field for mineral identification? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
The **Mohs scale of hardness**, developed by Friedrich Mohs in 1812, is a **relative, ordinal scale (1 to 10)** that ranks minerals based on their resistance to scratching. It is non-linear; the absolute hardness difference between successive numbers is not equal.

**The Mohs Scale of 10 Reference Minerals:**

| Mohs No. | Mineral | Field Test Reference |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Talc | Scratched by fingernail (H ≈ 2.5) |
| 2 | Gypsum | Scratched by fingernail |
| 3 | Calcite | Scratched by copper coin (H ≈ 3.5) |
| 4 | Fluorite | Scratched by glass / knife (H ≈ 5.5) |
| 5 | Apatite | Scratched by steel knife |
| 6 | Orthoclase | Scratches glass with effort |
| 7 | **Quartz** | Scratches glass and steel easily |
| 8 | Topaz | — |
| 9 | Corundum | — |
| 10 | Diamond | — |

**Field Identification Procedure:**

1. **Try to scratch the mineral with a fingernail** (H = 2.5). If it scratches → H ≤ 2.
2. **Try a copper coin or brass pin** (H = 3.5). If it scratches → H ≤ 3.
3. **Try a steel knife blade or glass plate** (H = 5.5). If the mineral scratches glass → H ≥ 6.
4. **Compare with streak plate** (H = 7). If the mineral scratches the streak plate → H ≥ 7.
5. **Narrow the mineral's identity** to a hardness range and cross-check with other properties.

**Limitations of Mohs Scale:**

- It is a **relative** scale, not a linear scale (e.g., diamond is 4 times harder than corundum in absolute terms, not 1.4 times).
- It is **not suitable for very soft or very hard** materials beyond its range.
- It does not measure **absolute hardness** (Vickers or Knoop scales are used for that).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE

**Q.B. (a)** Describe the chemical composition of quartz. Explain the SiO₄ tetrahedron and how it influences the physical properties of quartz. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Chemical Composition of Quartz:**

- **Chemical Formula:** **SiO₂** (Silicon Dioxide)
- **Elements Present:** Two elements only — Silicon (Si) and Oxygen (O)
- **Composition by Weight:**
  - Silicon: **46.7%**
  - Oxygen: **53.3%**
- **Atomic Ratio:** 1 Si : 2 O
- **Crystal System:** Trigonal (Rhombohedral)
- **Polymorphs of SiO₂:** Quartz has several polymorphs depending on temperature and pressure — α-quartz (stable below 573°C), β-quartz (573–870°C), tridymite, cristobalite, coesite, and stishovite.

**The SiO₄ Tetrahedron:**

The fundamental building block of quartz is the **silica tetrahedron (SiO₄)⁴⁻**:

- One **silicon cation (Si⁴⁺)** is positioned at the **center**.
- Four **oxygen anions (O²⁻)** are arranged at the four corners of a regular tetrahedron.
- The Si–O bonds are **strong, directional, and partially covalent (50% ionic + 50% covalent)**.

**3D Framework Structure:**

In quartz, every oxygen atom is **shared between two adjacent SiO₄ tetrahedra**. This creates a continuous, infinite **3D framework** in which the bonding strength is equal in all directions.

**Influence on Physical Properties:**

| Property | Influence of SiO₄ Network |
|---|---|
| High Hardness (7) | Strong covalent Si–O bonds resist scratching |
| No Cleavage | Equal bond strength in all directions — no plane of weakness |
| Conchoidal Fracture | When broken, the crack propagates through bonds of equal strength |
| High Melting Point (1710°C) | Strong bonds require high energy to break |
| Chemical Inertness | Strong Si–O bonds resist chemical weathering |
| Vitreous Luster | Strong bonds reflect light uniformly |

---

**Q.B. (b)** Compare and contrast the physical properties of quartz and calcite. How can you distinguish them in the field using simple tests? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Quartz (SiO₂) and Calcite (CaCO₃) are two of the most common rock-forming minerals. They can be distinguished using a few simple field tests.

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | Quartz (SiO₂) | Calcite (CaCO₃) |
|---|---|---|
| Chemical Formula | SiO₂ | CaCO₃ |
| Mohs Hardness | **7** | **3** |
| Specific Gravity | **2.65** | **2.71** |
| Crystal System | Trigonal | Trigonal |
| Cleavage | **None** | **Perfect rhombohedral (3 directions)** |
| Fracture | **Conchoidal** | Conchoidal (rarely observed due to cleavage) |
| Streak | **White** | **White** |
| Luster | Vitreous | Vitreous to dull |
| Color | Variable (colorless, white, etc.) | Colorless, white, various |
| Reaction with 10% HCl | **No effervescence** | **Violent effervescence (CO₂ released)** |
| Magnetism | None | None |
| Tenacity | Brittle | Brittle |
| Transparency | Transparent to translucent | Transparent to translucent |

**Field Tests for Distinguishing Quartz from Calcite:**

1. **HCl Drop Test (Most Diagnostic):**
   - Calcite: **Vigorous fizzing/effervescence** due to CO₂ release: $CaCO_3 + 2HCl \rightarrow CaCl_2 + H_2O + CO_2 \uparrow$
   - Quartz: **No reaction** (no carbonate content).

2. **Fingernail / Knife Scratch Test:**
   - Calcite (H=3): Scratched easily by a copper coin or fingernail.
   - Quartz (H=7): Cannot be scratched by knife or glass; scratches them instead.

3. **Hammer Test (Cleavage vs Fracture):**
   - Calcite: Breaks into small **rhombohedral cleavage fragments**.
   - Quartz: Breaks with **smooth, curved conchoidal surfaces**.

4. **Heft Test:**
   - Calcite feels slightly heavier (G=2.71) than quartz (G=2.65), but this is rarely distinguishable by hand.

**Conclusion:**
The **HCl drop test** is the single most reliable and fastest field test to distinguish calcite from quartz. A backup is the **scratch test** using a fingernail or knife.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that KTU Examiners Penalize:**
>
> 1. **Confusing color with streak:** Color is variable and unreliable (Quartz comes in 7+ colors). Streak is consistent. Students often write "color = white" for quartz — WRONG if the specimen is smoky quartz.
>
> 2. **Confusing cleavage with fracture:** Writing "Quartz has conchoidal cleavage" is a fatal error. Conchoidal is a type of **fracture**, NOT cleavage. Quartz has **NO cleavage**.
>
> 3. **Wrong Mohs hardness values:** Writing H = 6 for quartz is a common error. The correct value is **7** — this is the most tested number in Module 2.
>
> 4. **Omitting percentage composition:** KTU examiners often expect both **46.7% Si and 53.3% O**. Writing only "SiO₂" without percentages may lose 1 mark.
>
> 5. **HCl Test Confusion:** Some students write that quartz "fizzes slightly" with HCl. **Quartz does NOT react with HCl at all.** Only carbonates (calcite, dolomite) effervesce.
>
> 6. **Single-property identification:** Identifying a mineral based only on color is penalized. Always use **3 or more** properties together.
>
> 7. **Forgetting the crystal system:** Many students describe the shape of quartz but forget to write "**Trigonal crystal system**" — losing half a mark unnecessarily.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Physical Properties & Chemical Composition of Minerals (Quartz)**
>
> ✅ **Definition of Mineral:** Naturally occurring, inorganic, solid, homogeneous substance with a definite crystalline structure and (mostly) definite chemical composition.
>
> ✅ **Quartz Formula:** **SiO₂** (Silicon Dioxide) — the most abundant mineral in Earth's continental crust.
>
> ✅ **Composition by Weight:** Si = 46.7%, O = 53.3%.
>
> ✅ **Crystal System of Quartz:** **Trigonal (Rhombohedral)** — forms hexagonal prismatic crystals with pyramidal terminations.
>
> ✅ **Mohs Hardness = 7** — the **most important** number to remember. Quartz can scratch glass and steel.
>
> ✅ **Specific Gravity = 2.65** — the standard reference density for quartz.
>
> ✅ **Streak = White** (always, regardless of color).
>
> ✅ **Luster = Vitreous (glassy)** on fresh conchoidal surfaces.
>
> ✅ **Cleavage = None** (no planes of weakness due to 3D Si–O framework).
>
> ✅ **Fracture = Conchoidal** (smooth, curved, shell-like).
>
> ✅ **Reaction with HCl = No effervescence** (distinguishes quartz from calcite).
>
> ✅ **Color Varieties of Quartz:** Rock crystal (colorless), Amethyst (purple), Citrine (yellow), Rose Quartz (pink), Smoky Quartz (brown), Milky Quartz (white).
>
> ✅ **SiO₄ Tetrahedron:** One Si⁴⁺ at the center, four O²⁻ at the corners, strong 3D covalent network, every O shared between two tetrahedra.
>
> ✅ **Why No Cleavage:** Equal bond strength in all directions of the 3D SiO₄ framework.
>
> ✅ **Why Hard:** Strong, partially covalent Si–O bonds require high energy to break.
>
> ✅ **Mohs Scale Reference Minerals (Memorize):** Talc(1), Gypsum(2), Calcite(3), Fluorite(4), Apatite(5), Orthoclase(6), **Quartz(7)**, Topaz(8), Corundum(9), Diamond(10).
>
> ✅ **Field Test Tools:** Fingernail (2.5), Copper coin (3.5), Steel knife (5.5), Glass plate (5.5), Streak plate (7).
>
> ✅ **Quartz vs Calcite:** HCl test is the fastest distinguisher — calcite fizzes, quartz doesn't.
>
> ✅ **Engineering Significance:** Quartz is hard, durable, chemically inert — excellent as concrete aggregate and building stone. But reactive silica can cause Alkali-Silica Reaction (ASR) in concrete.
>
> ✅ **KTU-Mandated Identification Rule:** Always use **at least 4–5 properties together** — never rely on a single property.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Orthoclase
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET416 - ENGINEERING GEOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Orthoclase

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition
**Orthoclase** (also known as **Potash Feldspar** or **K-Feldspar**) is a rock-forming **tectosilicate mineral** belonging to the **feldspar group**, with the ideal chemical formula **KAlSi₃O₈** (Potassium Aluminosilicate). It crystallizes in the **monoclinic crystal system**, typically forming prismatic or tabular crystals. It is the **potassium-rich end-member** of the alkali feldspar solid solution series (KAlSi₃O₈ – NaAlSi₃O₈).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Orthoclase is a critical **rock-forming mineral** and a key **petrogenetic indicator** in igneous petrology. It helps geologists classify and interpret the cooling history of plutonic and volcanic rocks.

## Intuitive Analogy
Think of **Orthoclase** as the **"pinkish building block"** of the Earth's crust — just like LEGO bricks come in different colors and shapes, minerals are the building blocks of rocks. Orthoclase is one of the most common "LEGO bricks" you'll find in granite countertops and pink-colored building stones. If you spot a **pink grain** sparkling in a granite rock, you are almost certainly looking at Orthoclase!

## Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics
- **Hardness (Mohs Scale):** **6**
- **Specific Gravity:** **2.55 – 2.63**
- **Crystal System:** **Monoclinic**
- **Refractive Index:** ~1.52
- **Color:** Pink, flesh-red, white, grey, colorless
- **Luster:** Vitreous to pearly
- **Streak:** White
- **Fracture:** Conchoidal to uneven
- **Cleavage:** Two perfect cleavages at approximately **90°** (basal and side pinacoidal)

> [!NOTE]
> **Did you know?** The name "Orthoclase" comes from the Greek word *"orthos"* (meaning **straight**) and *"klasis"* (meaning **fracture**), referring to its two cleavage planes intersecting at right angles.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Theoretical Breakdown

### A. Chemical Composition
- **Ideal Formula:** **KAlSi₃O₈**
- **Key Cations:** K⁺ (Potassium), Al³⁺, Si⁴⁺
- **Structural Framework:** Three-dimensional framework of (Si,Al)O₄ tetrahedra linked at corners, with K⁺ ions occupying the cavities.
- **Solid Solution:** Partial substitution of K⁺ by Na⁺ forms the alkali feldspar series (Orthoclase → Sanidine → Anorthoclase → Albite).

### B. Polymorphs of KAlSi₃O₈
The K-feldspar polymorphs differ by the degree of Al/Si ordering in the tetrahedral sites:

| Polymorph | Crystal System | Formation Condition | Stability Temperature |
|-----------|---------------|---------------------|----------------------|
| **Sanidine** | Monoclinic | High temperature (volcanic) | > **900°C** (disordered) |
| **Orthoclase** | Monoclinic | Intermediate temperature (plutonic) | **500–900°C** (partially ordered) |
| **Microcline** | Triclinic | Low temperature (pegmatitic/metamorphic) | < **500°C** (fully ordered) |

### C. Twinning in Orthoclase
- **Carlsbad Twin:** Most common; contact twin with composition plane parallel to the side pinacoid (010).
- **Baveno Twin:** Contact twin with composition plane parallel to (021).
- **Manebach Twin:** Contact twin with composition plane parallel to (001).

> [!TIP]
> **Why does twinning matter in Engineering Geology?** Twinning affects the **mechanical strength, cleavage behavior, and weathering resistance** of rocks containing orthoclase, which directly impacts slope stability and foundation design.

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Property | Value/Description | Units |
|----------|------------------|-------|
| Chemical Formula | KAlSi₃O₈ | — |
| Hardness | 6 | Mohs |
| Specific Gravity | 2.55 – 2.63 | dimensionless |
| Crystal System | Monoclinic | — |
| Cleavage Angle | ~90° | degrees |
| Cleavage Planes | (001) perfect, (010) perfect | — |
| Fracture | Conchoidal to uneven | — |
| Luster | Vitreous to pearly | — |
| Color | Pink, flesh-red, white, grey | — |
| Diaphaneity | Transparent to translucent | — |
| Polymorphs | Sanidine, Orthoclase, Microcline | — |
| Twin Laws | Carlsbad, Baveno, Manebach | — |

## Real-World Engineering Utility
1. **Petrogenetic Indicator:** Used to determine the cooling history of igneous rocks.
2. **Ceramic & Porcelain Industry:** Raw material in manufacturing glass, ceramics, and glazes.
3. **Construction Stone:** Pink granite (rich in orthoclase) is widely used as a dimension stone.
4. **Weathering Studies:** Orthoclase weathers to **kaolinite** clay, important in soil mechanics and foundation engineering.
5. **Geochronology:** K-bearing minerals are used in **K-Ar and Ar-Ar dating** for determining rock ages.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation: Crystal System Classification of Orthoclase

### Step 1: Identify the Lattice Parameters
Orthoclase belongs to the **monoclinic crystal system**, characterized by three crystallographic axes of unequal lengths with one axis inclined to the other two.

$$
a \neq b \neq c
$$

$$
\alpha = \gamma = 90°, \quad \beta \neq 90°
$$

### Step 2: Express the Standard Monoclinic Angles
For orthoclase, the typical unit cell parameters are:

$$
a = 8.56 \; \text{Å}, \quad b = 12.96 \; \text{Å}, \quad c = 7.20 \; \text{Å}
$$

$$
\beta = 116.0°
$$

### Step 3: Calculate the Cleavage Angle
The two cleavages in orthoclase occur on **(001)** and **(010)** planes. The angle between these two cleavages can be calculated using the angle between the c-axis (perpendicular to 001) and the b-axis (perpendicular to 010):

$$
\theta_{\text{cleavage}} = \beta - 90°
$$

$$
\theta_{\text{cleavage}} = 116.0° - 90° = 26.0°
$$

But the **interfacial angle** between (001) and (010) cleavage surfaces is **90°**, hence the name "Orthoclase" (straight fracture).

## Analytical Matrix: Polymorph Differentiation

| Feature | Sanidine | **Orthoclase** | Microcline |
|---------|----------|----------------|------------|
| Crystal System | Monoclinic | **Monoclinic** | Triclinic |
| Al/Si Order | Disordered | **Partially ordered** | Fully ordered |
| 2V (Optic) | Small (0–20°) | **Medium (40–80°)** | Large (65–88°) |
| Extinction | Straight | **Straight** | Inclined (tartan twinning) |
| Occurrence | Volcanic (rapid cooling) | **Plutonic (intermediate cooling)** | Pegmatitic/Metamorphic (slow cooling) |
| Stability Temp | > 900°C | **500–900°C** | < 500°C |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Explained:** Step 1 identifies lattice inequality — monoclinic systems have one oblique angle (β). Step 2 substitutes standard K-feldspar cell parameters measured by X-ray diffraction. Step 3 uses geometric subtraction (β − 90°) to confirm the right-angle cleavage signature that defines orthoclase.

## Composition Variation: Solid Solution Series

Orthoclase forms a solid solution with **albite (NaAlSi₃O₈)** via the substitution:

$$
\text{KAlSi}_3\text{O}_8 \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{NaAlSi}_3\text{O}_8
$$

The degree of substitution (Or – Ab – An ternary classification):

$$
\text{Or} = \frac{\text{K}}{\text{K} + \text{Na} + \text{Ca}} \times 100\%
$$

where **Or** = orthoclase component. Pure orthoclase has **Or = 100%**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1: Crystal Habit of Orthoclase (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Orthoclase Monoclinic Crystal] --> B[Prismatic Habit]
    A --> C[Tabular Habit]
    A --> D[Equant Habit]
    B --> E[Elongated along c-axis]
    C --> F[Flattened parallel to 010]
    D --> G[Stubby equidimensional form]
    E --> H[Twinning Patterns]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> H1[Carlsbad Twin - Simple Contact]
    H --> H2[Baveno Twin - Penetration]
    H --> H3[Manebach Twin - Basal Contact]
    H1 --> I[Cleavage at 90 degrees]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J[Pink Flesh Red Color in Hand Specimen]
```

## Diagram 2: Polymorph Transformation Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[High Temperature > 900C] -->|Rapid Cooling| B[Sanidine Monoclinic Disordered]
    B -->|Intermediate Cooling 500-900C| C[Orthoclase Monoclinic Partially Ordered]
    C -->|Slow Cooling Less than 500C| D[Microcline Triclinic Fully Ordered]
    D -->|Further Ordering| E[Maximum Microcline]
    A -->|Very Rapid Quench| F[Glass Sanidine]
    F -->|Devitrification| B
```

## Diagram 3: Weathering Pathway of Orthoclase

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Orthoclase KAlSi3O8] -->|Hydrolysis CO2 H2O| B[Kaolinite Al2Si2O5OH4]
    B -->|Further Weathering| C[Gibbsite AlOH3]
    B -->|Continued Alteration| D[Silica Gel SiO2 nH2O]
    A -->|Solution Removal of K| E[Potassium Ions K Plus Released to Soil]
    E --> F[Soil Fertility Enrichment]
    A -->|Feldspar Alteration| G[Sericite Muscovite]
    G -->|Continued Alteration| B
```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram Reading Strategy for KTU Exams:** Always use these flow diagrams to illustrate the **petrogenetic significance** of orthoclase in igneous, sedimentary, and metamorphic rock systems.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Orthoclase. Mention its chemical formula and crystal system. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**Model Answer:**
Orthoclase is a **potassium aluminosilicate mineral** belonging to the feldspar group with the chemical formula **KAlSi₃O₈**. It crystallizes in the **monoclinic crystal system** and is characterized by two perfect cleavages intersecting at approximately **90°**. It commonly occurs in granites, syenites, and pegmatites, and is recognized by its pink to flesh-red color and vitreous luster.

---

### Q2. List any three physical properties used to identify Orthoclase in hand specimen. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Model Answer:**
The three diagnostic physical properties are:
1. **Color:** Pink, flesh-red, or white.
2. **Hardness:** 6 on the Mohs scale (can scratch glass).
3. **Cleavage:** Two perfect cleavages intersecting at right angles (~90°).
Additional property: **Specific gravity** of 2.55 – 2.63.

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Describe the physical, optical, and chemical properties of Orthoclase. Add a note on its polymorphs. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Physical Properties:**
- **Color:** Pink, flesh-red, white, grey
- **Hardness:** 6 (Mohs)
- **Specific Gravity:** 2.55 – 2.63
- **Cleavage:** Two perfect cleavages (001 and 010) at 90°
- **Fracture:** Conchoidal to uneven
- **Luster:** Vitreous to pearly
- **Streak:** White
- **Tenacity:** Brittle

**Optical Properties (under petrological microscope):**
- **Crystal System:** Monoclinic
- **Extinction:** Straight (parallel extinction on (001))
- **2V (Optic Axial Angle):** 40°–80° (medium)
- **Sign:** Optically negative (−)
- **Twinning:** Carlsbad, Baveno, Manebach common
- **Relief:** Low (n ≈ 1.52)
- **Birefringence:** Low (0.007)

**Chemical Properties:**
- **Formula:** KAlSi₃O₈
- **Composition:** K₂O = 16.9%, Al₂O₃ = 18.4%, SiO₂ = 64.7%
- **Solubility:** Insoluble in ordinary acids; slowly attacked by HF
- **Solid Solution:** Forms series with albite (NaAlSi₃O₈)

**Polymorphs of KAlSi₃O₈:**
1. **Sanidine** — Monoclinic, high-temperature (>900°C), disordered Al/Si
2. **Orthoclase** — Monoclinic, intermediate temperature (500–900°C), partially ordered
3. **Microcline** — Triclinic, low-temperature (<500°C), fully ordered, shows tartan twinning

---

**(b) Discuss the geological occurrence and engineering significance of Orthoclase. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Geological Occurrence:**
- **Igneous Rocks:** Major constituent of **granite, syenite, granodiorite, pegmatite**, and **rhyolite**.
- **Metamorphic Rocks:** Present in **gneiss** and **schist**.
- **Sedimentary Rocks:** Detrital grains in **arkose sandstone** (feldspar-rich sandstone).
- **Hydrothermal Veins:** Common in **quartz-feldspar veins**.

**Engineering Significance:**
1. **Petrogenetic Indicator:** Helps geologists identify the parent rock type and thermal history.
2. **Weathering Behavior:** Orthoclase alters to **kaolinite** clay, which affects slope stability and permeability in foundation engineering.
3. **K-Ar Dating:** Used in isotopic age determination of igneous and metamorphic rocks.
4. **Construction Material:** Pink granites (orthoclase-rich) are used as **dimension stones** in building and monuments.
5. **Ceramic Industry:** Raw material for **porcelain, glass, and glaze manufacturing**.
6. **Soil Mechanics:** Weathered orthoclase contributes to clay minerals, influencing **plasticity, swelling, and shear strength** of soils.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Explain the crystal structure and twinning in Orthoclase. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Crystal Structure:**
Orthoclase has a **three-dimensional framework silicate (tectosilicate)** structure:
- Built from **(Si,Al)O₄ tetrahedra** linked at corner-sharing oxygen atoms.
- Each tetrahedron has Si⁴⁺ or Al³⁺ at the center and 4 oxygen atoms at the corners.
- The (Si,Al)O₄ tetrahedra form **4-membered and 8-membered rings**, creating channels and cavities.
- **K⁺ ions** (large ionic radius ~1.38 Å) occupy the structural cavities to balance the charge created by Al³⁺ substitution for Si⁴⁺.
- The partial ordering of Al/Si in tetrahedral sites gives orthoclase its **monoclinic symmetry** (β = 116°).

**Twinning in Orthoclase:**
1. **Carlsbad Twin:**
   - Most common in orthoclase.
   - Composition plane = (010)
   - Twin axis = c-axis
   - Two intergrown crystals appear as a single elongated prism with a re-entrant angle.

2. **Baveno Twin:**
   - Composition plane = (021)
   - Penetrating contact twin
   - Produces a nearly square cross-section.

3. **Manebach Twin:**
   - Composition plane = (001)
   - Simple contact twin
   - Less common than Carlsbad.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Twinning Matters:** Twinning in orthoclase influences the **mechanical anisotropy** of granitic rocks, affecting their resistance to weathering and their use as construction materials.

---

**(b) Differentiate between Orthoclase and Plagioclase feldspars. Add a note on their identification. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Differentiation Table:**

| Property | Orthoclase (K-Feldspar) | Plagioclase (Na-Ca Feldspar) |
|----------|-------------------------|------------------------------|
| Composition | KAlSi₃O₈ | NaAlSi₃O₈ to CaAl₂Si₂O₈ |
| End Members | Orthoclase (Or) | Albite (Ab) – Anorthite (An) |
| Crystal System | Monoclinic | Triclinic |
| Cleavage Angle | ~90° | ~86°24′ |
| Color | Pink, flesh-red, white | White, grey, rarely colored |
| Twinning | Carlsbad, Baveno, Manebach | Polysynthetic (albite law) |
| Striations | Absent | Present (albite twin striations on basal cleavage) |
| Specific Gravity | 2.55–2.63 | 2.60–2.76 (increases with Ca) |
| Weathering Product | Kaolinite | Kaolinite + Calcite |

**Identification in Hand Specimen:**
- **Striations on cleavage surfaces** → Plagioclase
- **Absence of striations + pink color** → Orthoclase
- Under microscope: Polysynthetic albite twinning → Plagioclase; Simple Carlsbad twin → Orthoclase.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Leading to Mark Deduction:**
> 1. **Confusing Orthoclase with Microcline:** Remember — Orthoclase is monoclinic; Microcline is triclinic (with tartan twinning). Examiners strictly check the crystal system.
> 2. **Writing "90° cleavage" loosely:** Always specify "(001) and (010)" cleavage planes and state the angle as ~90° (technically 90° is approximate due to monoclinic geometry).
> 3. **Skipping the polymorph table:** Many students forget to mention Sanidine, Orthoclase, and Microcline as a triad. This is a **high-frequency question** in KTU exams.
> 4. **Ignoring twinning laws:** Always mention Carlsbad, Baveno, and Manebach twins for full marks.
> 5. **Not writing chemical percentages:** Examiners award extra credit for mentioning K₂O, Al₂O₃, SiO₂ percentages.
> 6. **Mixing up weathering products:** Orthoclase → Kaolinite (not just clay). Specify the full chemical name.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Orthoclase = KAlSi₃O₈ = Potassium Feldspar = Monoclinic system**
- ✅ **Hardness = 6 (Mohs); Specific Gravity = 2.55–2.63**
- ✅ **Cleavage = Two perfect cleavages at ~90°** (defining feature)
- ✅ **Color = Pink / flesh-red** (most diagnostic in hand specimen)
- ✅ **Three Polymorphs:** Sanidine (high T) → Orthoclase (intermediate T) → Microcline (low T)
- ✅ **Three Twin Laws:** Carlsbad (most common), Baveno, Manebach
- ✅ **Major occurrence:** Granite, Syenite, Pegmatite, Gneiss, Arkose sandstone
- ✅ **Weathering product:** Kaolinite clay (K⁺ released to soil)
- ✅ **Engineering uses:** Dimension stone (pink granite), ceramics, K-Ar dating
- ✅ **Distinguishing feature vs. Plagioclase:** Plagioclase shows **polysynthetic striations**; Orthoclase does **not**.
- ✅ **Origin of name:** Greek *"orthos"* (straight) + *"klasis"* (fracture) → 90° cleavage
- ✅ **Solid solution series:** KAlSi₃O₈ — NaAlSi₃O₈ (Or–Ab series)
- ✅ **Framework silicate (Tectosilicate)** structure with K⁺ in cavities.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plagioclase
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET416 - ENGINEERING GEOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Plagioclase

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# PLAGIOCLASE — A Comprehensive Engineering Geology Treatise

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Module 2 of PECET416 covers the **Mineralogy of Rock-Forming Minerals**. Plagioclase is one of the **two most important rock-forming mineral groups** (the other being alkali feldspar) and is treated as a **high-weightage topic** in KTU University Examinations. It is foundational for understanding igneous rock classification, Bowen's Reaction Series, and petrographic thin-section analysis.

---

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):**
> **Plagioclase** is a member of the **feldspar group of minerals** that forms a **complete isomorphous solid-solution series** between the sodium end-member **albite (NaAlSi₃O₈)** and the calcium end-member **anorthite (CaAl₂Si₂O₈)**. Chemically, it is a **sodium–calcium aluminosilicate** belonging to the **triclinic crystal system**.

The general chemical formula is:

$$ \text{Plagioclase} = \text{Na}_{1-x}\text{Ca}_x \text{Al}_{1+x}\text{Si}_{3-x}\text{O}_8 $$

where $x$ varies continuously from $0$ (pure albite) to $1$ (pure anorthite). The substitution is of the coupled type:

$$ \text{Na}^+ + \text{Si}^{4+} \rightleftharpoons \text{Ca}^{2+} + \text{Al}^{3+} $$

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| **Crystal System** | **Triclinic** (pinacoidal class) |
| **Hardness (Mohs scale)** | **6 – 6.5** |
| **Specific Gravity** | **2.60 – 2.76** (increases with An content) |
| **Refractive Index** | **1.527 – 1.588** |
| **Luster** | **Vitreous to pearly** on cleavages |
| **Cleavage** | **Two directions, perfect at ~ 90°** |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — A "Salt-Pepper" Continuum

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Visualization:**
> Imagine you have a jar of **white salt** (representing pure albite, NaAlSi₃O₈) and a jar of **black pepper** (representing pure anorthite, CaAl₂Si₂O₈). You can mix these two powders in **any ratio** — from 100% salt + 0% pepper, to 0% salt + 100% pepper, and every combination in between. The resulting "seasoning blend" is **one continuous mixture**, not separate minerals. This is exactly what happens in nature: calcium and sodium atoms can substitute for each other in the plagioclase crystal lattice **in any proportion**, creating a continuous spectrum of mineral compositions — all sharing the same crystal structure (triclinic), but differing in chemistry and physical properties.

**Engineering Analogy for Civil Engineers:** Just as **cement-aggregate proportion** can be varied continuously in a concrete mix (from lean to rich), the **Ca–Na ratio in plagioclase** can be varied continuously — and this ratio is what controls the mineral's physical behavior, weathering resistance, and the type of rock it forms.

---

## 1.3 Why Plagioclase Matters in Engineering Geology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Plagioclase is found in virtually every igneous rock and many metamorphic and sedimentary rocks. Its **composition directly indicates the parent rock type, temperature of formation, and geological history** — all of which are critical inputs for **tunneling, dam-foundation, slope-stability, and aggregate-quality** assessments in civil engineering practice.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — The Plagioclase Solid-Solution Series

## 2.1 The Six Members of the Plagioclase Series

The plagioclase series is divided into **six members** based on the **mole percentage of the anorthite (An) component**:

| Member | An % Range | Ab % Range | Typical Occurrence |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Albite** | **0 – 10%** | **100 – 90%** | Granite, pegmatite, metamorphic rocks |
| **Oligoclase** | **10 – 30%** | **90 – 70%** | Granodiorite, monzonite |
| **Andesine** | **30 – 50%** | **70 – 50%** | Andesite, diorite |
| **Labradorite** | **50 – 70%** | **50 – 30%** | Gabbro, basalt, anorthosite |
| **Bytownite** | **70 – 90%** | **30 – 10%** | Mafic gabbros, some basalts |
| **Anorthite** | **90 – 100%** | **10 – 0%** | Ultramafic rocks, lunar basalts, contact metamorphic rocks |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mineralogical Convention:** Plagioclase composition is always reported as **Anₓ**, where $x$ is the mole percent of the anorthite component. For example, **An₆₅** means 65% anorthite and 35% albite — placing it firmly in the **labradorite** field.

---

## 2.2 Crystal Chemistry and Coupled Substitution

### 2.2.1 The Coupled Isomorphous Replacement

Plagioclase exhibits **coupled substitution** because replacing one ion requires a charge-balancing replacement of another:

$$ \text{Na}^+ \text{Si}^{4+} \longleftrightarrow \text{Ca}^{2+} \text{Al}^{3+} $$

The ionic radii are:

$$ r(\text{Na}^+) \approx 1.18 \, \text{Å}, \quad r(\text{Ca}^{2+}) \approx 1.12 \, \text{Å} $$

$$ r(\text{Al}^{3+}) \approx 0.535 \, \text{Å}, \quad r(\text{Si}^{4+}) \approx 0.26 \, \text{Å} $$

Because the ionic radii are **similar** (within 15%), the **substitution is continuous** at all temperatures — unlike the alkali feldspars (orthoclase–albite), which exhibit **exsolution** and unmixing at lower temperatures.

### 2.2.2 Structural Framework

Plagioclase has a **three-dimensional framework silicate (tectosilicate)** structure consisting of corner-sharing (Si,Al)O₄ tetrahedra. The (Na,Ca) cations occupy large, irregular cavities in this framework to balance the charge created by the Al³⁺ ↔ Si⁴⁺ substitution.

---

## 2.3 Physical and Optical Properties

| Property | Albite End (An₀) | Anorthite End (An₁₀₀) | Trend with ↑ An |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Color** | White, colorless | White, gray, reddish | Slightly darkens |
| **Specific Gravity** | **2.60** | **2.76** | **Increases** |
| **Hardness** | 6 – 6.5 | 6 – 6.5 | Roughly constant |
| **Refractive Index (α)** | 1.527 | 1.576 | Increases |
| **Refractive Index (γ)** | 1.538 | 1.588 | Increases |
| **Birefringence** | 0.011 | 0.012 | Slight increase |
| **2V angle** | ~ 77° | ~ 78° | Variable |
| **Cleavage** | {001} perfect, {010} perfect | Same | Both ~ 90° |
| **Twinning** | Polysynthetic (albite law) | Polysynthetic + Carlsbad | Diagnostic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note:** Plagioclase with **high An content (labradorite–bytownite)** is **less resistant to chemical weathering** than albite-rich plagioclase, because the larger Ca²⁺ ion creates a more disrupted, reactive lattice. This is why **Ca-rich plagioclase alters to clay minerals (kaolinite, smectite)** more readily — a critical factor in **slope stability, foundation weathering, and tunnel squeezing problems**.

---

## 2.4 Twinning — The Diagnostic Feature of Plagioclase

Twinning in plagioclase is **diagnostic under both hand-lens and petrographic-microscope** examination:

> [!TIP]
> **Three critical twin laws for KTU:**

1. **Albite Law (Polysynthetic Twinning):** Repeated, fine, parallel twin lamellae on the {010} composition plane. Visible as **fine striations** on {001} cleavage faces. This is the **most diagnostic** feature — it distinguishes plagioclase from orthoclase (which typically shows simple Carlsbad twins).
2. **Carlsbad Law:** Simple, penetration twin with composition plane {010}. Common in all feldspars.
3. **Pericline–Albite Combined Twinning:** Produces a **checkerboard or quadrille pattern** in cross-section, diagnostic of triclinic symmetry.

**Untwinned plagioclase** is **very rare** — its near-constant presence of **polysynthetic albite twinning** is one of the most reliable field identification features.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| General formula | $(\text{Na,Ca})(\text{Al,Si})_4\text{O}_8$ | Identifies plagioclase in chemical analysis |
| End-member albite | $\text{NaAlSi}_3\text{O}_8$ | Sodium-rich end |
| End-member anorthite | $\text{CaAl}_2\text{Si}_2\text{O}_8$ | Calcium-rich end |
| Composition notation | $\text{An}_{x}\text{Ab}_{100-x}$ | Standard reporting in petrography |
| Mole fraction of An | $\text{An}\% = \dfrac{n_{\text{CaO}}}{n_{\text{CaO}} + n_{\text{Na}_2\text{O}}} \times 100$ | Computed from XRF / wet chemistry |
| Coupled substitution | $\text{Na}^+ + \text{Si}^{4+} \leftrightarrow \text{Ca}^{2+} + \text{Al}^{3+}$ | Maintains charge neutrality |
| Density trend | $\rho(\text{An}_{100}) - \rho(\text{An}_0) = 0.16$ g/cm³ | Lab measurement of specific gravity → An% |
| Refractive index | $n_\alpha \approx 1.527 + 0.49 \times (\text{An}/100)$ | Optical estimate of composition |
| Igneous crystallization | **Continuous series** (Bowen) | Plagioclase crystallizes over a wide T range |
| Metamorphic indicator | An% **decreases** with **increasing metamorphic grade** in mafic rocks | Petrogenetic tool |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember for KTU:** Plagioclase forms a **COMPLETE, CONTINUOUS solid solution** at high temperatures (above ~ 700°C). It is one of the textbook examples of **isomorphous replacement** and is part of **Bowen's Continuous Reaction Series** (along with olivine).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Calculating Plagioclase Composition from Chemical Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context (KTU-style):** A chemical analysis of a plagioclase sample yields the following mole percentages of oxides: CaO = 6.75% and Na₂O = 8.86%. Compute the anorthite content (An%) of the plagioclase.

### Step 1 — Convert weight % to mole % (mole fraction)

> **Molar masses:**
> $M(\text{CaO}) = 40.08 + 16.00 = 56.08$ g/mol
> $M(\text{Na}_2\text{O}) = 2(22.99) + 16.00 = 61.98$ g/mol

Moles of CaO per 100 g:

$$ n(\text{CaO}) = \frac{6.75}{56.08} = 0.1204 \text{ mol} $$

Moles of Na₂O per 100 g:

$$ n(\text{Na}_2\text{O}) = \frac{8.86}{61.98} = 0.1430 \text{ mol} $$

### Step 2 — Convert Na₂O moles to Na-atom equivalents

> Each Na₂O molecule contains **2 Na atoms**, so:

$$ n(\text{Na-atoms}) = 2 \times 0.1430 = 0.2860 \text{ mol} $$

### Step 3 — Stoichiometry of Plagioclase

> In plagioclase, **1 mole of Ca²⁺ corresponds to 1 mole of anorthite (CaAl₂Si₂O₈)** unit, and **1 mole of Na⁺ corresponds to 1 mole of albite (NaAlSi₃O₈)** unit. Therefore:

$$ n(\text{An component}) = n(\text{CaO}) = 0.1204 \text{ mol} $$

$$ n(\text{Ab component}) = n(\text{Na atoms}) = 0.2860 \text{ mol} $$

### Step 4 — Compute An mole percentage

$$ \text{An}\% = \frac{n(\text{An})}{n(\text{An}) + n(\text{Ab})} \times 100 $$

$$ \text{An}\% = \frac{0.1204}{0.1204 + 0.2860} \times 100 $$

$$ \text{An}\% = \frac{0.1204}{0.4064} \times 100 $$

$$ \text{An}\% = 29.63\% $$

### Step 5 — Identify the mineral name

> Using the classification table from Section 2.1: An = **29.63%** falls in the **10 – 30% range** → therefore this plagioclase is **Oligoclase** (specifically **An₃₀**).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Estimating An% from Measured Specific Gravity

> [!TIP]
> **Empirical relationship** (commonly used in KTU lab examinations):

$$ \text{An}\% \approx \frac{\rho - 2.605}{0.16} \times 100 $$

where $\rho$ is the measured specific gravity of the plagioclase sample.

### Worked Example

**Given:** A plagioclase specimen has a measured specific gravity of **2.685**.

**Step 1:** Subtract the albite end-member density:

$$ \Delta \rho = 2.685 - 2.605 = 0.080 $$

**Step 2:** Divide by the density span of the series (0.16 g/cm³):

$$ \frac{\Delta \rho}{0.16} = \frac{0.080}{0.16} = 0.50 $$

**Step 3:** Convert to percentage:

$$ \text{An}\% = 0.50 \times 100 = 50\% $$

**Step 4:** Identify the mineral:

> An₅₀ falls right at the **Andesine–Labradorite boundary** — the sample is **andesine** (or low-labradorite).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Refractive Index Method (Michel-Lévy / Immersion Method)

The **mean refractive index (n)** of plagioclase varies **linearly** with composition:

$$ n \approx 1.527 + 0.49 \times \left(\frac{\text{An}\%}{100}\right) $$

### Worked Example

**Given:** A plagioclase grain in thin section shows a mean refractive index of **1.560** (measured by immersion).

**Step 1:** Substitute into the formula:

$$ 1.560 = 1.527 + 0.49 \times \left(\frac{\text{An}\%}{100}\right) $$

**Step 2:** Solve for An%:

$$ 0.033 = 0.49 \times \left(\frac{\text{An}\%}{100}\right) $$

$$ \frac{\text{An}\%}{100} = \frac{0.033}{0.49} = 0.0673 $$

**Step 3:**

$$ \text{An}\% \approx 6.7\% $$

**Step 4:** Identify the mineral:

> An₆.₇ ≈ **Albite** (An 0–10%).

---

## 3.4 KTU Analytical Matrix — Composition ↔ Properties ↔ Rock Type

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **three-way correlation matrix** is a **high-yield KTU exam tool**. Memorize the relationships, not just the numbers.

| An% | Plagioclase Name | Specific Gravity | n (mean) | Typical Host Rock | Igneous Series |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | Albite | 2.605 | 1.527 | Granite, pegmatite | **Felsic** |
| **20** | Oligoclase | 2.637 | 1.574 | Granodiorite | Felsic–intermediate |
| **50** | Andesine | 2.685 | 1.560 | Diorite, andesite | **Intermediate** |
| **70** | Labradorite | 2.717 | 1.570 | Gabbro, basalt | **Mafic** |
| **90** | Bytownite | 2.749 | 1.578 | Anorthosite | Mafic–ultramafic |
| **100** | Anorthite | 2.765 | 1.583 | Lunar basalt, contact rocks | **Ultramafic** |

> [!TIP]
> **Trend to remember:** As you go from **felsic → mafic → ultramafic** igneous rocks, the plagioclase composition shifts from **albite-rich → anorthite-rich**, and **specific gravity + refractive index both increase**. This is a direct consequence of the **larger mass and higher polarizability of Ca²⁺** compared to Na⁺.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Temperature of Crystallization (Approximate, from Composition in Igneous Rocks)

> [!NOTE]
> Plagioclase composition in **equilibrium igneous assemblages** is temperature-sensitive. A simplified linear approximation (suitable for KTU-level analysis):

$$ T(^{\circ}\text{C}) \approx 1100 - 4.5 \times \text{An}\% $$

### Worked Example

A basalt contains plagioclase of composition **An₆₅** (labradorite). Estimate its crystallization temperature.

$$ T \approx 1100 - 4.5 \times 65 $$

$$ T \approx 1100 - 292.5 $$

$$ T \approx 807.5^{\circ}\text{C} $$

This is consistent with the **typical basaltic magma crystallization range** (1000–1200°C), acknowledging that the first-crystallizing (most Ca-rich) plagioclase forms at the **liquidus** — actual equilibrium temperatures of later rim compositions would be lower.

> [!WARNING]
> This is a **simplified empirical approximation** for academic purposes only. In professional petrology, two-feldspar geothermometry or plagioclase–melt equilibrium models are used.

## 4.2 Coupled Substitution Mechanism — Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph albiteEnd["ALBITE UNIT NaAlSi3O8"]
        a1["Na cation site"]
        a2["Al in T1 tetrahedron"]
        a3["Si in T1 tetrahedron"]
    end
    subgraph anorthiteEnd["ANORTHITE UNIT CaAl2Si2O8"]
        b1["Ca cation site"]
        b2["Al in T1 tetrahedron"]
        b3["Al in T2 tetrahedron"]
    end
    a1 -. coupled replacement .-> b1
    a3 -. Al for Si .-> b3
    a2 -. retained .-> b2
    style albiteEnd fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#DAA520,stroke-width:2px
    style anorthiteEnd fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#9370DB,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** The replacement of one albite unit by one anorthite unit involves **two simultaneous substitutions** — Na⁺ → Ca²⁺ at the large cation site, and Si⁴⁺ → Al³⁺ at one of the tetrahedral sites. This is the **coupled substitution** that makes the plagioclase series a textbook example of **isomorphous replacement**.
