The **CO₃ groups in Layer 2 and Layer 4 are rotated 60° relative to each other** — this 60° rotation, repeated through the long $c$-axis, generates the **3-fold trigonal axis ($\bar{3}$)** and the **three equivalent cleavage planes**. --- ## 4.2 Calcite Cleavage Geometry ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Calcite Crystal
Trigonal System] --> B[Identify three
10-11 planes] B --> C[Plane P1
Leans right] B --> D[Plane P2
Leans left] B --> E[Plane P3
Front face] C --> F[Intersection angle
75 degrees acute] C --> G[Intersection angle
105 degrees obtuse] D --> F D --> G E --> F E --> G F --> H[Rhombohedral
Cleavage Fragment] G --> H H --> I[When struck:
3 matching planes
crack open] I --> J[Resulting fragment
is a perfect RHOMB
All faces identical] style A fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style H fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style J fill:#14532d,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff ``` --- ## 4.3 Calcite Weathering & Karst Cycle ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Atmospheric CO2
pCO2 = 10^-3.5 atm] --> B[Rainwater absorbs CO2
Forms weak H2CO3] B --> C[H2CO3 attacks
Calcite CaCO3] C --> D[CaCO3 + H2CO3
= Ca2+ + 2 HCO3-] D --> E[Ca-HCO3 solution
flowing through joints] E --> F[Dissolution enlarges
fractures into CAVES] F --> G[SINKHOLES form
at surface] F --> H[UNDERGROUND RIVERS
develop] H --> I[Eventually CaCO3
re-precipitates] I --> J[STALACTITES hang
from cave roof] I --> K[STALAGMITES rise
from cave floor] J --> L[Engineering hazard
Dam leakage] G --> L K --> L L --> M[Civil engineering
mitigation: grouting,
geo-membranes] style A fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style F fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style L fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style M fill:#14532d,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff ``` --- ## 4.4 Identification Decision Tree (Field/Lab) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Mineral Specimen
White, glassy, H=3] --> B{Tests with
cold 5 percent HCl?} B -->|Vigorous effervescence| C[Suspect Calcite or
Aragonite] B -->|No reaction| D{Tests with hot HCl?} D -->|Effervescence| E[Suspect Dolomite] D -->|No reaction| F[Not a carbonate] C --> G{Specific gravity?} G -->|2.72 to 2.94| H[Confirm CALCITE
CaCO3] G -->|2.94 to 2.95| I[Confirm ARAGONITE
orthorhombic CaCO3] H --> J[3 perfect cleavage
rhombs at 75/105 deg] J --> K[Strong birefringence
0.172 in thin section] K --> L[FINAL ID: CALCITE] style A fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style H fill:#14532d,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style L fill:#14532d,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff ``` --- ## 4.5 Calcite in Engineering Geology — Application Map ```mermaid flowchart LR A[CALCITE CaCO3] --> B[Geological Settings] A --> C[Engineering Implications] B --> B1[SEDIMENTARY
Limestone, Chalk] B --> B2[METAMORPHIC
Marble] B --> B3[HYDROTHERMAL
Veins in faults] B --> B4[BIOGENIC
Shells, coral, pearls] C --> C1[Dam foundations
Reservoir leakage] C --> C2[Tunnel excavation
Karst water inflow] C --> C3[Cement raw material
CaO source] C --> C4[Concrete aggregate
ACR alkali reaction] C --> C5[CO2 sequestration
Carbon capture] B1 --> C1 B1 --> C2 B2 --> C3 B2 --> C4 B3 --> C1 B3 --> C2 B4 --> C5 style A fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff style C fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, Module 2]** ### Q1. Define Calcite and state its distinguishing optical property. (3 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 — adapted]** **Model Answer:** Calcite is a **trigonal carbonate mineral** with the chemical formula **CaCO₃** (Calcium carbonate), belonging to the Calcite Group of anhydrous carbonates. It is the principal mineral of limestone, marble and chalk. Its **distinguishing optical property** is **extreme birefringence**: $\delta = n_o - n_e = 1.658 - 1.486 = 0.172$, the **highest among common rock-forming minerals**. It is **uniaxial negative** and produces brilliant high-order interference colours in thin section. A transparent variety (**Iceland Spar**) exhibits strong **double refraction** visible to the naked eye. | Parameter | Marks | Coverage | |---|---|---| | Definition + formula | 1 | Definition | | Crystal system + group | 1 | Crystallography | | Birefringence with value | 1 | Optical property | --- ### Q2. Briefly explain why calcite effervesces in cold dilute HCl while dolomite does not. (3 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2 — adapted]** **Model Answer:** When cold dilute (≈ 5%) HCl is applied: - **Calcite (CaCO₃)** reacts **instantly and vigorously**, releasing CO₂ gas: $$ CaCO_3 + 2HCl \rightarrow CaCl_2 + H_2O + CO_2 \uparrow $$ - **Dolomite CaMg(CO₃)₂** does **not** react with cold dilute HCl because the **Mg²⁺ ions in its structure bond the carbonate groups more strongly**, raising the activation energy of the acid attack. Only when the acid is **heated** or the dolomite is **powdered** (increasing surface area) does the reaction proceed: $$ CaMg(CO_3)_2 + 4HCl \rightarrow CaCl_2 + MgCl_2 + 2H_2O + 2CO_2 \uparrow $$ This differential reactivity is the **standard field test** to distinguish limestone (calcite-rich) from dolostone (dolomite-rich) during geological site investigation. | Parameter | Marks | Coverage | |---|---|---| | Calcite reaction | 1 | Chemistry | | Dolomite no-reason | 1 | Mineralogy | | Mg²⁺ reason + condition | 1 | Conceptual | --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern) > **Note:** As per KTU 2024 Scheme, Part B Module-internal-choice questions carry **14 marks** divided into sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks, OR three sub-parts (a) 5 + (b) 5 + (c) 4, depending on the question paper template. --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper, Module 2]** #### (a) Describe the crystallographic and physical properties of calcite in detail. (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** **1. Crystallographic Properties:** - **Crystal System:** Trigonal (a subsystem of the hexagonal system). - **Space Group:** $R\bar{3}c$. - **Axial ratio:** $a : c = 1 : 3.419$, with $a = 4.989$ Å, $c = 17.062$ Å. - **Symmetry elements:** One 3-fold rotoinversion axis ($\bar{3}$), three 2-fold axes perpendicular to it, three mirror planes, and a centre of symmetry — giving point-group symmetry $\bar{3}\tfrac{2}{m}$. - **Habit:** Common habits are **rhombohedral**, **scalenohedral (dog-tooth spar)**, **prismatic**, and **stalactitic**. - **Structure:** Calcite structure is a **distorted NaCl-type lattice** in which **Ca²⁺** ions alternate with planar **CO₃²⁻** groups, the latter being oriented perpendicular to the $c$-axis and **rotated 60° between successive layers**. **2. Physical Properties:** | Property | Value / Description | |---|---| | Hardness (Mohs) | **3** | | Specific Gravity | **2.72** | | Cleavage | **Perfect** on $\{10\bar{1}1\}$ in 3 directions at 75°/105° | | Fracture | Conchoidal (rarely seen due to perfect cleavage) | | Luster | Vitreous; sometimes pearly on cleavage | | Streak | **White** | | Colour | Colourless, white, grey, yellow, blue, pink | | Transparency | Transparent to translucent | | Birefringence | **0.172** (highest of common minerals) | | Optic Sign | **Uniaxial negative (−)** | | Refractive indices | $n_o = 1.658$, $n_e = 1.486$ | | Twinning | Common on $\{01\bar{1}2\}$ (glide twins) | | Tenacity | Brittle | | Magnetism | Non-magnetic | | Electrical | Non-conductive (good insulator) | | Luminescence | Sometimes fluorescent (red/orange) under UV | | Solubility | Slowly soluble in water with CO₂ | | Reaction with HCl | **Violent effervescence** in cold dilute acid | | Thermal behaviour | Decomposes at ≈ 900 °C to **CaO + CO₂** | | Sub-topic | Marks | |---|---| | Crystal system + structure | 2 | | Habit + symmetry | 1 | | Physical properties table | 3 | | Optical properties | 1 | --- #### (b) Explain the origin, mode of occurrence, and engineering significance of calcite. (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** **1. Origin and Mode of Occurrence:** Calcite is a **ubiquitous mineral** that forms in **five principal geological environments:** **(i) Sedimentary (most abundant):** - **Biogenic precipitation** by marine organisms — corals, molluscs, foraminifera build calcitic shells and skeletons that accumulate on the sea floor. - **Inorganic precipitation** from saturated water bodies — chemical limestone. - **Clastic accumulation** of pre-existing calcite fragments. - Resulting rocks: **limestone, chalk, travertine, tufa, coquina**. **(ii) Metamorphic:** - Recrystallisation of limestone under heat and pressure produces **marble**, a coarse-grained calcite rock. - At very high pressures, calcite may transform to **aragonite**, then back to calcite on decompression. **(iii) Hydrothermal / Vein:** - Calcite precipitates from **hot aqueous solutions** in fractures, faults, and vesicles. - Forms **calcite veins** — important markers of fluid flow in dam abutments and tunnel faces. - Common gangue mineral in **Pb–Zn–Cu ore deposits**. **(iv) Igneous (accessory):** - Occurs as a late-stage **accessory mineral** in carbonatites and some pegmatites. **(v) Weathering / Supergene:** - Calcite is precipitated as **caliche, cave dripstone (stalactites and stalagmites), and travertine** around hot springs. **2. Engineering Significance:** | Engineering Field | Role of Calcite | |---|---| | **Cement Industry** | Raw material for **Portland cement**; calcined to CaO at ≈ 900 °C | | **Building Stone** | Marble (metamorphic calcite) is a premium dimension stone | | **Aggregates** | Calcite-rich aggregates may undergo **Alkali–Carbonate Reaction (ACR)** with cement, causing concrete cracking | | **Tunnels** | Limestone (calcite rock) → **karst water inflow**, cavity collapse, TBM jamming | | **Dams** | Calcite-filled fractures = **permeable seepage paths** under reservoirs | | **Foundations** | Solution cavities in calcite = **sinkhole subsidence** under buildings | | **Soil Mechanics** | Weathered calcite = **expansive marl** behaviour; lime stabilisation of clay soils | | **Hydrogeology** | Aquifer in karst limestone; high transmissivity but unpredictable | | **Carbon Sequestration** | Calcite precipitation as a **permanent CO₂ storage** mechanism | | **Petroleum Geology** | Calcite cement in reservoir sandstones reduces porosity; secondary porosity by calcite dissolution enhances oil recovery | | Sub-topic | Marks | |---|---| | Modes of occurrence (5 environments) | 3 | | Engineering significance table | 3 | | Synthesis | 1 | --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper, Module 2]** #### (a) Discuss the chemical weathering of calcite and the formation of karst topography. (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** **1. Chemistry of Calcite Weathering:** Calcite is **insoluble in pure water** but dissolves readily when the water contains dissolved **carbon dioxide (CO₂)** derived from soil air and atmospheric sources. The dissolution proceeds through the following sequence of equilibria: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{(a)} \quad & CO_2(g) + H_2O \rightleftharpoons H_2CO_3(aq) \\ \text{(b)} \quad & H_2CO_3 \rightleftharpoons H^+ + HCO_3^{-} \\ \text{(c)} \quad & CaCO_3 + H^+ \rightleftharpoons Ca^{2+} + HCO_3^{-} \end{aligned} $$ **Overall reaction:** $$ CaCO_3 + CO_2 + H_2O \rightleftharpoons Ca^{2+} + 2\,HCO_3^{-} $$ The reaction is **reversible** — when the dissolved CO₂ escapes (e.g., water emerging from a cave), the reaction proceeds in reverse, precipitating calcite as **travertine, tufa, stalactites, and stalagmites**. **2. Karst Topography Development:** **Karst** is a distinctive landscape developed on **soluble carbonate rocks** (limestone, dolomite, gypsum) in humid climates. The following sequence describes its evolution: | Stage | Process | Geomorphic Expression | |---|---|---| | 1 | Rainwater absorbs CO₂ → weak carbonic acid | Initial surface dissolution | | 2 | Acid attacks joints, bedding planes | **Limestone pavement** (clints and grykes) | | 3 | Enlargement along fractures | **Sinkholes / dolines** (circular depressions) | | 4 | Underground dissolution | **Caves** (e.g., Mammoth Cave, Borra Caves) | | 5 | Cave roof collapse | **Uvalas, poljes** (large depressions) | | 6 | Re-precipitation of calcite | **Stalactites** (ceiling) + **Stalagmites** (floor) → Columns | | 7 | Continued dissolution | **Disappearing streams, springs** | **3. Indian Examples:** Borra Caves (Andhra Pradesh), Edakkal Caves (Kerala), Mawsmai Cave (Meghalaya), Kutumsar Cave (Chhattisgarh). | Sub-topic | Marks | |---|---| | Chemistry with equations | 2 | | Karst stages table | 3 | | Indian examples + synthesis | 2 | --- #### (b) Describe the role of calcite in (i) cement manufacture, and (ii) alkali–carbonate reaction in concrete. (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** **(i) Calcite in Cement Manufacture (3.5 Marks):** Calcite, in the form of **limestone (CaCO₃ > 80 %)**, is the **primary source of lime (CaO)** in the manufacture of **Portland cement**. The process involves: **Step 1 — Quarrying & Crushing:** Limestone (calcite) is quarried, crushed, and ground to fine powder. **Step 2 — Calcination in Rotary Kiln (≈ 900 – 1000 °C):** $$ CaCO_3 \xrightarrow{\Delta} CaO + CO_2 \uparrow $$ This step liberates ≈ 224 m³ of CO₂ per tonne of pure limestone (as derived in §3.4). **Step 3 — Clinker Formation:** The CaO is mixed with **silica (SiO₂), alumina (Al₂O₃), and iron oxide (Fe₂O₃)** in correct proportions and heated in a kiln to ≈ 1450 °C to form **clinker minerals** — alite (C₃S), belite (C₂S), aluminate (C₃A), and ferrite (C₄AF). **Step 4 — Grinding:** Clinker is ground with ≈ 3–5 % gypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O) to produce **Portland cement**. **Quality specifications for cement-grade limestone:** | Parameter | Required Value | |---|---| | CaCO₃ | > 80 % (preferably > 85 %) | | MgCO₃ | < 5 % | | SiO₂ | < 5 % | | Al₂O₃ + Fe₂O₃ | < 3 % | | LOI | 40 – 44 % | **(ii) Alkali–Carbonate Reaction (ACR) in Concrete (3.5 Marks):** **Definition:** ACR is a deleterious chemical reaction between the **alkali hydroxides (Na⁺, K⁺, OH⁻) in cement pore fluid** and certain **calcite-rich (carbonate) aggregates** that contain **reactive dolomite**. **Mechanism:** 1. Alkali pore solution attacks reactive **dolomite crystals** within the aggregate. 2. **Dedolomitisation** reaction occurs: $$ CaMg(CO_3)_2 + 2\,NaOH \rightarrow CaCO_3 + Mg(OH)_2 + Na_2CO_3 $$ 3. The **Mg(OH₂) brucite** and **secondary calcite** form with **expansion**, generating **internal tensile stresses** in the concrete. 4. **Map cracking** (pattern cracking) develops on the surface — also called **"concrete cancer"**. **Preventive measures:** - Avoid reactive carbonate aggregates. - Use **low-alkali cement** (Na₂O + 0.658 K₂O < 0.6 %). - Limit reactive aggregate content to < 15 %. - Use **mineral admixtures** like fly ash, slag, silica fume. **Indian Context:** ACR has been documented in **Himachal Pradesh, Kashmir, and parts of Tamil Nadu** where dolomitic limestones are abundant. | Sub-topic | Marks | |---|---| | Cement steps + equations | 2 | | Limestone quality specs | 1.5 | | ACR mechanism + prevention | 3.5 | --- ## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / COMMON PITFALLS > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Lose Marks in Calcite Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing Calcite with Dolomite:** Many students state the hardness of calcite as 3.5–4. Always remember — **calcite = 3, dolomite = 3.5–4**. The HCl test is the clincher. > > 2. **Wrong Crystal System:** Calcite is **trigonal**, NOT hexagonal. Although trigonal is a subsystem of the hexagonal family, examiners expect the word **"trigonal"** in crystallography questions. > > 3. **Mixing up Optic Sign:** Calcite is **uniaxial NEGATIVE** (o-ray slower than e-ray). Students often write "positive" by reflex. Recall: $n_o > n_e$ ⇒ negative. > > 4. **Forgetting the CO₂ Release:** When asked about cement manufacture, students must write the **calcination reaction** and the **temperature**. Marks are reserved specifically for this. > > 5. **Numerical Errors in % Composition:** 56.03 % CaO and 43.97 % CO₂ — many students write 40 % / 60 % by mistake. Memorise the derivation (Derivation 3.1). > > 6. **Birefringence Sign Convention:** $\delta$ is always **positive** (defined as $n_{max} - n_{min}$). The value is 0.172, not −0.172, even though calcite is optically negative. > > 7. **Cement Composition:** Don't confuse clinker minerals with raw mix. **Raw mix = CaO + SiO₂ + Al₂O₃ + Fe₂O₃** (from limestone + clay). **Clinker = C₃S + C₂S + C₃A + C₄AF**. > > 8. **Karst Stages:** Examiners want the **sequence** (pavement → dolines → caves → poljes). Random listing = 0 marks. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Calcite (Module 2, PECET416)** - **Formula:** $\text{CaCO}_3$ — **56.03 % CaO + 43.97 % CO₂** - **Crystal System:** **Trigonal**, Space group $R\bar{3}c$, axial ratio $a:c = 1:3.419$ - **Hardness:** **3** (scratched by copper coin; cannot scratch glass) - **Specific Gravity:** **2.72** - **Cleavage:** **3 perfect** on $\{10\bar{1}1\}$ at **75°/105°** → rhombs - **Habit:** Rhombohedral, scalenohedral (dog-tooth), prismatic, stalactitic - **Twinning:** Common on $\{01\bar{1}2\}$ — marker of tectonic deformation - **Luster:** Vitreous; **Streak:** White - **Reaction:** **Violent effervescence in cold 5 % HCl** (diagnostic vs. dolomite) - **Birefringence:** **$\delta = 0.172$** — highest of common rock-forming minerals - **Optic Sign:** **Uniaxial NEGATIVE** (since $n_o = 1.658 > n_e = 1.486$) - **Polymorphs:** Aragonite (orthorhombic) + Vaterite (hexagonal, rare) - **Decomposition:** $CaCO_3 \xrightarrow{900°C} CaO + CO_2 \uparrow$ (calcination) - **Solubility:** ≈ **14 mg/L Ca²⁺** in CO₂-charged water at 25 °C → **karst** - **Key Equations:** - $\delta = n_o - n_e = 0.172$ - $K_{sp} = [Ca^{2+}][CO_3^{2-}] = 10^{-8.48}$ - $V_{CO_2} \approx 224 \;\text{m}^3$ per tonne of pure CaCO₃ - **Engineering Uses:** Cement raw mix, building stone (marble), soil lime stabilisation, CO₂ sequestration - **Engineering Hazards:** Karst sinkholes, dam-reservoir leakage, alkali–carbonate reaction in concrete, tunnel water inflow - **Key Field Tests:** (1) Cold dilute HCl effervescence; (2) Rhomb shape on hammering; (3) Hardness 3; (4) White streak - **Indian Examples:** Borra Caves (AP), Edakkal Caves (Kerala), Mawsmai Cave (Meghalaya), Kolar marble (Rajasthan) - **Calcite Group Series:** Calcite — Magnesite — Siderite — Rhodochrosite — Smithsonite (isostructural, with Ca²⁺ progressively replaced by Mg²⁺, Fe²⁺, Mn²⁺, Zn²⁺) - **Cement Raw Mix Rule:** Limestone (CaCO₃) + Clay (SiO₂ + Al₂O₃ + Fe₂O₃) → **Clinker** at 1450 °C → Ground with gypsum → **Portland cement** - **ACR Preventive Rule:** Use **low-alkali cement** (Na₂O + 0.658 K₂O < 0.6 %) to avoid alkali–carbonate reaction with reactive dolomitic aggregates. > **Final Exam Tip (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Always end any calcite answer with at least **one engineering application** — it secures the higher-order Bloom's marks (Apply / Analyse / Evaluate tier).

topics.title: Gypsum. Petrology - Igneous Rocks - Chemical and Mineralogical Classification

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET416 - ENGINEERING GEOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Gypsum. Petrology - Igneous Rocks - Chemical and Mineralogical Classification # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 GYPSUM ### Formal Academic Definition **Gypsum** is a soft, hydrous **calcium sulfate** mineral with the chemical formula: $$\text{CaSO}_4 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ It belongs to the **evaporite mineral group** and is one of the most abundant sulfate minerals found in the Earth's crust. It crystallizes in the **monoclinic crystal system** and is a sedimentary mineral primarily formed through the **evaporation of saline lake and sea waters** under arid climatic conditions. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Gypsum is classified as a non-metallic industrial mineral and is studied under Engineering Geology for its direct applications in cement manufacture, plaster production, and as a soil stabilizer in civil engineering projects. ### Intuitive Analogy Think of gypsum as **"nature's baking soda."** When seawater or lake water evaporates in a hot desert (think Rann of Kutch in Gujarat), the dissolved salts precipitate out in a definite order. The last to settle are the most soluble — and **calcium sulfate (gypsum) is one of the earliest evaporites to precipitate** because of its lower solubility compared to halite (NaCl) and sylvite (KCl). Just as how salt crystallizes at the bottom of a glass of seawater left in the sun, gypsum forms thick, layered deposits called **"evaporite sequences."** ### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Hardness (Mohs Scale):** 2 (can be scratched by a fingernail) - **Specific Gravity:** 2.32 g/cm³ - **Molecular Weight:** 172.17 g/mol - **Solubility in Water:** 2.0 g/L at 20°C - **Crystal System:** Monoclinic - **Cleavage:** Perfect on {010}, distinct on {100} and {011} - **Luster:** Subvitreous to silky/pearly - **Streak:** White - **Color:** Colorless, white, yellowish, pinkish, or brownish (due to impurities) - **Tenacity:** Flexible but inelastic (slightly sectile) ### Three Recognized Varieties (KTU High-Yield) | Variety | Description | Engineering Use | |---|---|---| | **Selenite** | Transparent, well-developed tabular or prismatic crystals | Specimen/decorative stone | | **Satin Spar** | Fibrous, silky-lustered form | Ornamental carving | | **Alabaster** | Fine-grained, massive, translucent form | Sculptures, plaster | > [!NOTE] > **Plaster of Paris (POP) Connection:** When gypsum is heated to ~120–190°C, it loses 3/4 of its water of crystallization and converts to **calcium sulfate hemihydrate (CaSO₄·½H₂O)**, commonly known as **Plaster of Paris**. When mixed with water, it reverts back to gypsum and hardens — this reversible reaction is the foundation of all gypsum-based construction materials. --- ## 1.2 IGNEOUS ROCKS — OVERVIEW ### Formal Academic Definition **Igneous rocks** (from Latin *ignis* = fire) are the **primary rocks** of the Earth's crust, formed by the **cooling, crystallization, and solidification of molten magma (intrusive) or lava (extrusive)**. They constitute approximately **65% of the Earth's crust** by volume, though they are largely covered by sedimentary rocks at the surface. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module 2 Focus:** The classification of igneous rocks is approached through **two parallel, complementary schemes:** > 1. **Chemical Classification** — based on the percentage of **silica (SiO₂)** content > 2. **Mineralogical Classification** — based on the **modal mineral composition** (visible minerals under petrographic microscope) ### Intuitive Analogy Imagine a **kitchen recipe** for igneous rocks: - **Chemical Classification** is like checking the **main ingredient** (flour content in a cake) — whether it is silica-rich or silica-poor. - **Mineralogical Classification** is like looking at the **final dish** — counting the actual nuts, raisins, and chocolate chips (i.e., the minerals quartz, feldspar, mica) visible in the cooled product. Both methods lead to the same "cake," but they look at it from different angles — one by recipe, the other by visible ingredients. > [!NOTE] > **Why Two Classifications?** > - **Chemical classification** is preferred in field studies and rapid logging (geochemical data from XRF). > - **Mineralogical classification** is the **IUGS (International Union of Geological Sciences) standard** used worldwide, based on the actual mineral percentages observed in thin section under a polarizing microscope. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 GYPSUM — Detailed Theoretical Analysis ### 2.1.1 Chemistry & Formation The theoretical chemical composition of pure gypsum is: - **CaO (Calcium Oxide):** 32.6% - **SO₃ (Sulfur Trioxide):** 46.5% - **H₂O (Water of Crystallization):** 20.9% #### Stepwise Formation Logic: 1. **Source:** Calcium ions (Ca²⁺) from weathering of calcium-bearing rocks (e.g., limestone, pyrite oxidation). 2. **Sulfate source:** From oxidation of pyrite (FeS₂) or dissolution of evaporite sulfate rocks. 3. **Transport:** These ions reach closed/semi-closed marine basins, lagoons, or playa lakes. 4. **Concentration:** Solar evaporation concentrates dissolved salts. 5. **Precipitation Sequence (Solubility-controlled):** - First: **Calcite (CaCO₃)**, then **Gypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O)**, then **Halite (NaCl)**, then **Sylvite (KCl)**. The chemical reaction of gypsum formation: $$\text{Ca}^{2+} + \text{SO}_4^{2-} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CaSO}_4 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ ### 2.1.2 Engineering Properties of Gypsum | Property | Value/Description | Engineering Significance | |---|---|---| | Hardness | **2 on Mohs scale** | Easily powdered → low abrasion on machinery | | Specific Gravity | **2.32** | Lightweight aggregate potential | | Solubility | Slightly soluble in water | Affects tunnel & underground water seepage | | Fire Resistance | Non-combustible; releases water at high T | Used as fireproofing material | | Setting Time (POP) | 8–15 min (rapid) | Critical in casting/moulding applications | ### 2.1.3 Why "How" and "Why" Gypsum Matters to Engineers - **Why?** Gypsum controls the **flash setting of cement** — added at 2–5% to retard the reaction of tricalcium aluminate (C₃A). - **How?** Without gypsum, cement would set in minutes, making it unusable for concrete pouring. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Practical Insight:** Gypsum karst (dissolution cavities) in foundation rocks can lead to sudden **sinkhole formation**, a critical concern in dam and tunnel engineering. --- ## 2.2 IGNEOUS ROCKS — CHEMICAL CLASSIFICATION (Deep Analysis) ### 2.2.1 Basis of Classification The chemical classification is based primarily on the **silica (SiO₂) content** of the rock, as analyzed through whole-rock geochemistry (X-Ray Fluorescence — XRF). ### Stepwise Logic of Chemical Classification: 1. **Magma Composition** is governed by SiO₂ percentage. 2. Higher SiO₂ → **more polymerized** melt → **higher viscosity** → **slower flow** → **explosive volcanism** (e.g., rhyolitic). 3. Lower SiO₂ → **less polymerized** melt → **lower viscosity** → **effusive volcanism** (e.g., basaltic). ### 2.2.2 The Four Chemical Divisions | Class | SiO₂ Content | Key Characteristics | Plutonic (Intrusive) | Volcanic (Extrusive) | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Acidic** | **> 65%** | High viscosity, light-colored (felsic), excess quartz | **Granite** | **Rhyolite** | | **Intermediate** | **65 – 52%** | Moderate viscosity, salt-and-pepper appearance | **Diorite / Syenite** | **Andesite / Trachyte** | | **Basic** | **52 – 45%** | Low viscosity, dark-colored (mafic) | **Gabbro** | **Basalt** | | **Ultrabasic** | **< 45%** | Very fluid, extremely dark (ultramafic) | **Peridotite / Dunite** | **Komatiite** (rare) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Implication:** Acidic rocks (granite) are **stronger, more resistant to weathering**, and preferred as foundation/decorative stones. Basic rocks (basalt) are **denser** and used as road aggregates and railway ballast. ### 2.2.3 Color Index (Mafic Index) — Linked Concept The **Color Index (CI)** is a derived field parameter that complements the chemical classification: $$\text{Color Index (\%)} = \frac{\text{Volume \% of dark (mafic) minerals}}{\text{Total volume of rock}} \times 100$$ | Rock Type | Color Index | Feldsic/Mafic Ratio | |---|---|---| | Granite (Acidic) | < 20% | Felsic-dominant | | Diorite (Intermediate) | 20 – 40% | Mixed | | Gabbro (Basic) | 40 – 70% | Mafic-dominant | | Peridotite (Ultrabasic) | > 70% | Ultramafic | --- ## 2.3 IGNEOUS ROCKS — MINERALOGICAL CLASSIFICATION (IUGS Standard) ### 2.3.1 The QAPF Diagram (Streckeisen Classification) The **IUGS (International Union of Geological Sciences)** standard classification is based on the **modal mineral composition** (actual volume % of minerals) plotted on a **double triangle** (QAP + F). **Q = Quartz** **A = Alkali Feldspar** (orthoclase, microcline, albite ≤ An₅) **P = Plagioclase** (An₅ to An₁₀₀) **F = Feldspathoids** (nepheline, leucite, sodalite) > [!NOTE] > For rocks **saturated with silica (quartz-bearing)**, use the **QAP triangle**. > For rocks **undersaturated with silica (feldspathoid-bearing)**, use the **FAP triangle**. ### 2.3.2 KTU High-Yield Mineralogical Classification Table | Rock Name (Plutonic) | Texture | Essential Minerals | Accessory Minerals | Tectonic Setting | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Granite** | Phaneritic (coarse) | Quartz + Alkali Felspar + Plagioclase | Biotite, Hornblende, Muscovite | Continental crust, subduction zones | | **Granodiorite** | Phaneritic | Quartz + Plagioclase (dominant) + Alkali Fs | Biotite, Hornblende | Continental arcs | | **Tonalite** | Phaneritic | Quartz + Plagioclase | Biotite, Hornblende | Continental arcs | | **Syenite** | Phaneritic | Alkali Feldspar dominant | Plagioclase < 35%, minor mafics | Rift zones | | **Diorite** | Phaneritic | Plagioclase (intermediate) + Hornblende | Biotite, minor quartz | Continental arcs | | **Gabbro** | Phaneritic | Plagioclase (Ca-rich) + Pyroxene | Olivine, Hornblende | Oceanic crust, layered intrusions | | **Peridotite** | Coarse-granular | Olivine + Pyroxene | Chromite, Garnet | Upper mantle, ophiolites | | **Dunite** | Coarse-granular | Olivine (>90%) | Chromite | Mantle xenoliths | | **Pyroxenite** | Coarse-granular | Pyroxene (>90%) | Olivine | Layered intrusions | ### 2.3.3 Volcanic Equivalents (KTU Board-Favorite Comparison) | Plutonic (Intrusive) | Volcanic (Extrusive) | Distinguishing Feature | |---|---|---| | Granite | Rhyolite | Rhyolite is fine-grained, often with glassy matrix | | Diorite | Andesite | Andesite named from Andes Mountains | | Gabbro | Basalt | Basalt = most abundant volcanic rock on Earth | | Peridotite | Komatiite | Komatiite is Archean (>2.5 Ga), high-Mg | --- ## 2.4 KTU FORMULA SHEET / CHEAT SHEET (Markdown Table) | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Value | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Gypsum chemical formula | $\text{CaSO}_4 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | — | | 2 | Gypsum Hardness (Mohs) | $\text{H} = 2$ | Dimensionless | | 3 | Gypsum Specific Gravity | $\text{G} = 2.32$ | $\text{g/cm}^3$ | | 4 | Gypsum → POP (calcination) | $\text{CaSO}_4 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{120-190°C} \text{CaSO}_4 \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O} + \tfrac{3}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O}\uparrow$ | — | | 5 | POP → Gypsum (rehydration) | $\text{CaSO}_4 \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O} + \tfrac{3}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CaSO}_4 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | Sets in ~10 min | | 6 | Chemical Class boundary (Acidic) | $\text{SiO}_2 > 65\%$ | wt.% | | 7 | Chemical Class boundary (Intermediate) | $65\% \geq \text{SiO}_2 \geq 52\%$ | wt.% | | 8 | Chemical Class boundary (Basic) | $52\% > \text{SiO}_2 \geq 45\%$ | wt.% | | 9 | Chemical Class boundary (Ultrabasic) | $\text{SiO}_2 < 45\%$ | wt.% | | 10 | Color Index | $\text{CI} = \dfrac{\text{Mafic Mineral \%}}{\text{Total Mineral \%}} \times 100$ | % | | 11 | QAPF Modal Sum Rule | $\text{Q} + \text{A} + \text{P} = 100\%$ (saturated rocks) | % | | 12 | Nepheline Syenite criterion | $\text{F} > 10\% \text{ AND Q} = 0\%$ | Modal % | # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 GYPSUM — Analytical Derivations ### 3.1.1 Derivation of % Water Content in Pure Gypsum Starting with the chemical formula $\text{CaSO}_4 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$, we calculate the theoretical water percentage by mass. **Given:** - Atomic/Molecular masses: $\text{Ca} = 40$, $\text{S} = 32$, $\text{O} = 16$, $\text{H} = 1$ **Step 1:** Compute molecular weight of $\text{CaSO}_4$ (anhydrite equivalent): $$\begin{aligned} \text{MW}(\text{CaSO}_4) &= 40 + 32 + (4 \times 16) \\ &= 40 + 32 + 64 \\ &= 136 \text{ g/mol} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2:** Compute mass of water of crystallization: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Mass of } 2\text{H}_2\text{O} &= 2 \times (2 \times 1 + 16) \\ &= 2 \times 18 \\ &= 36 \text{ g/mol} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3:** Total molecular weight of gypsum: $$\begin{aligned} \text{MW}(\text{CaSO}_4 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O}) &= 136 + 36 \\ &= 172 \text{ g/mol} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4:** Theoretical % water content: $$\begin{aligned} \% \text{H}_2\text{O} &= \frac{\text{Mass of water}}{\text{Total MW}} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{36}{172} \times 100 \\ &= 20.93\% \end{aligned}$$ This matches the standard textbook value of **20.9%**, confirming the formula's stoichiometric accuracy. ### 3.1.2 Derivation of Plaster of Paris (POP) Composition When gypsum loses 1.5 molecules of water to form POP: **Step 1:** Mass lost as water vapor: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Mass of } 1.5 \text{ H}_2\text{O} &= 1.5 \times 18 = 27 \text{ g} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2:** Mass of remaining hemihydrate: $$\begin{aligned} \text{MW of POP} &= 172 - 27 = 145 \text{ g/mol} \\ &\text{i.e., } \text{CaSO}_4 \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O} = 145 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3:** Verify the hemihydrate formula: $$\begin{aligned} \text{CaSO}_4 \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O} &= 136 + 9 = 145 \text{ g/mol} \quad \checkmark \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4:** Water-to-POP ratio for setting (engineering standard): $$\begin{aligned} \text{Water:POP ratio (by mass)} &= \frac{27 \text{ (added)}}{145 \text{ (POP)}} \approx 0.186 \text{ or } \sim 18.6\% \end{aligned}$$ > [!NOTE] > In practice, civil engineers use a slightly higher water-to-POP ratio (typically 0.5–0.7) to achieve a workable paste, but the **stoichiometric minimum is 18.6%** — the rest evaporates or remains as uncombined pore water. --- ## 3.2 IGNEOUS ROCKS — ANALYTICAL CLASSIFICATION MATRIX ### 3.2.1 Worked Example: Modal Analysis → Rock Identification **Given:** A coarse-grained igneous rock shows the following minerals (by volume) under a petrographic microscope: - Quartz: 25% - Alkali Feldspar: 35% - Plagioclase: 30% - Biotite: 7% - Hornblende: 3% **Step 1:** Normalize Q-A-P to 100% (exclude accessories like biotite and hornblende): $$\begin{aligned} \text{Q} + \text{A} + \text{P} &= 25 + 35 + 30 = 90\% \\ \text{Normalized Q} &= \frac{25}{90} \times 100 = 27.78\% \\ \text{Normalized A} &= \frac{35}{90} \times 100 = 38.89\% \\ \text{Normalized P} &= \frac{30}{90} \times 100 = 33.33\% \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2:** Plot on the QAP triangle (Q = 27.78%, A = 38.89%, P = 33.33%) — falls within the **GRANITE** field of the IUGS QAPF diagram. **Step 3:** Verification using chemical classification — typical granite SiO₂ ≈ 72% → **Acidic** ✓ **Conclusion:** The rock is a **GRANITE (Acidic, Plutonic)**. ### 3.2.2 Stepwise Identification Algorithm for Igneous Rocks (KTU Board Pattern) **Step 1:** Check texture — is it coarse (phaneritic) or fine (aphanitic)? - Coarse → **Plutonic** path - Fine/Glassy → **Volcanic** path **Step 2:** For plutonic rocks, count Q-A-P-F modal %. **Step 3:** For volcanic rocks, use the TAS (Total Alkali-Silica) diagram if chemical data is available, or use phenocryst assemblage in porphyritic varieties. **Step 4:** Apply the QAPF triangle rules: - Q > 20% → **Granitoid family** (Granite/Granodiorite/Tonalite) - A > P, Q < 20% → **Syenite** family - A ≈ P (within 10%), Q < 20% → **Monzonite** - P > A, Q < 20% → **Diorite/Gabbro** family - F > 10% → **Foid-bearing** family (e.g., Nepheline Syenite) - M (Mafics) > 90% → **Ultramafic** family (Peridotite, Dunite, Pyroxenite) ### 3.2.3 Worked Example: Calculating Color Index **Given:** A diorite sample contains 55% plagioclase, 5% quartz, 5% alkali feldspar, 25% hornblende, and 10% biotite. **Step 1:** Identify mafic minerals (dark-colored): Hornblende + Biotite = 25 + 10 = 35% **Step 2:** Calculate Color Index: $$\begin{aligned} \text{CI} &= \frac{\text{Mafic \%}}{\text{Total Mineral \%}} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{35}{100} \times 100 = 35\% \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3:** Cross-check classification: - CI = 35% → **Intermediate** ✓ (matches diorite's expected range of 20–40%) - SiO₂ expected ≈ 57% → **Intermediate chemical class** ✓ - Mineralogically: P > A, Q < 20% → **Diorite** ✓ All three independent methods converge on the same identification: **DIORITE**. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Chemical Classification Flowchart for Igneous Rocks ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Igneous Rock Sample] --> B{Texture Identification} B -->|Coarse-grained
Phaneritic| C[Plutonic Suite] B -->|Fine-grained
Aphanitic| D[Volcanic Suite] B -->|Glassy/Porphyritic| E[Special Texture Path] C --> F{Measure SiO2 %} D --> F F -->|SiO2 > 65%| G1[ACIDIC] F -->|SiO2 = 52 to 65%| G2[INTERMEDIATE] F -->|SiO2 = 45 to 52%| G3[BASIC] F -->|SiO2 < 45%| G4[ULTRABASIC] G1 --> H1[Granite / Rhyolite] G2 --> H2[Diorite-Syenite / Andesite-Trachyte] G3 --> H3[Gabbro / Basalt] G4 --> H4[Peridotite / Komatiite] H1 --> I1[Quartz-rich

Olivine-pyroxene rich] style A fill:#E1F5FF,stroke:#0066CC style G1 fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#FF8C00 style G2 fill:#FFD1DC,stroke:#C71585 style G3 fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#696969 style G4 fill:#2F4F4F,stroke:#000,color:#FFF ``` ## 4.2 IUGS QAPF Modal Classification Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Igneous Rock Modal Analysis] --> B{Quartz Present?} B -->|Yes, more than 20%| C[QAP Triangle - Granitoids] B -->|No or less than 20%| D{Feldspathoids Present?} D -->|Yes, more than 10%| E[FAP Triangle - Foid-bearing] D -->|No or less than 10%| F[AP Binary - Feldspar Rocks] C --> C1[Quartz more than 60% A] C1 --> C2[Tonalite field] C --> C3[A more than 90% of A+P] C3 --> C4[Alkali Granite field] C --> C5[P more than 90% of A+P] C5 --> C6[Quartz Diorite field] F --> F1[A more than 90% of A+P] F1 --> F2[Syenite field] F --> F3[P more than 90% of A+P] F3 --> F4[Gabbro-Diorite field] F --> F5[A and P roughly equal] F5 --> F6[Monzonite field] E --> E1[F more than 60%] E1 --> E2[Foidolite field] E --> E3[A dominant with F] E3 --> E4[Nepheline Syenite field] style A fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#8B4513 style B fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#DAA520 style D fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#DAA520 style C2 fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#8B0000 style C4 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B style C6 fill:#F5F5DC,stroke:#556B2F style F2 fill:#FAFAD2,stroke:#B8860B style F4 fill:#DCDCDC,stroke:#2F4F4F style E4 fill:#FFEBCD,stroke:#8B008B ``` ## 4.3 Gypsum — Process Flow: From Mineral to Plaster of Paris ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Natural Gypsum
CaSO4.2H2O
Extracted from Mines] --> A2[Crushing and Grinding] A2 --> A3{Calculation Temperature} A3 -->|120 to 190 degree C| A4[Plaster of Paris
CaSO4.0.5H2O
Beta Hemihydrate] A3 -->|Above 190 degree C| A5[Anhydrite CaSO4
Dead Burnt Gypsum] A4 --> A6[Mixing with Water] A6 --> A7[Setting and Hardening] A7 --> A8[Reformed Gypsum
CaSO4.2H2O
Set Plaster] A8 --> A9[Engineering Applications] A9 --> A9a[Cement Retarder] A9 --> A9b[POP Plaster and Boards] A9 --> A9c[Soil Stabilization] A9 --> A9d[Decorative Alabaster] style A1 fill:#F5DEB3,stroke:#8B4513 style A4 fill:#FFEFD5,stroke:#FF8C00 style A8 fill:#FAFAD2,stroke:#B8860B style A9 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B ``` ## 4.4 Igneous Rock Identification Decision Tree (Mineralogy-Based) ```mermaid flowchart TD M1[Step 1: Identify Texture] --> M2{Coarse or Fine?} M2 -->|Coarse| M3[Plutonic Rock] M2 -->|Fine| M4[Volcanic Rock] M3 --> M5[Step 2: Count Quartz and Feldspathoids] M5 --> M6{Quartz more than 20%?} M6 -->|Yes| M7[Granite / Granodiorite / Tonalite] M6 -->|No| M8{Feldspathoids more than 10%?} M8 -->|Yes| M9[Nepheline Syenite / Foid Syenite] M8 -->|No| M10{Plagioclase Dominant?} M10 -->|Yes| M11[Plagioclase type?] M11 -->|Ca-rich An more than 50| M12[Gabbro] M11 -->|Na-rich An less than 50| M13[Diorite] M10 -->|No Alkali Fs dominant| M14[Syenite] M10 -->|Mixed| M15[Monzonite] M4 --> M16[Step 2V: Use Phenocrysts or TAS Diagram] M16 --> M17[Corresponding Volcanic Equivalent] M17 --> M18[Basalt Andesite Trachyte Rhyolite] M12 --> Z[Step 3: Engineering Classification] M13 --> Z M14 --> Z M7 --> Z M9 --> Z M18 --> Z Z --> Z1[Strong Foundation Stone] Z --> Z2[Aggregate for Concrete] Z --> Z3[Decorative Stone] Z --> Z4[Road Metal] style M1 fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082 style M7 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513 style M12 fill:#A9A9A9,stroke:#000 style M9 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#800080 style Z fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400 ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definition) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Define gypsum. List its three important varieties and give one identifying property for each.** **Model Answer:** **Gypsum Definition:** Gypsum is a soft, hydrous calcium sulfate mineral with the chemical formula $\text{CaSO}_4 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$, formed primarily as an evaporite from the evaporation of saline waters. It has a Mohs hardness of **2** and specific gravity of **2.32**. **Three Varieties:** 1. **Selenite** — transparent, well-formed crystals (often tabular or prismatic). 2. **Satin Spar** — fibrous variety with silky luster. 3. **Alabaster** — fine-grained, massive, translucent form used for carving. *(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for the three varieties with properties)* ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Distinguish between acidic and ultrabasic igneous rocks. Give one example of each.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Acidic Igneous Rock | Ultrabasic Igneous Rock | |---|---|---| | Silica content | **> 65%** SiO₂ | **< 45%** SiO₂ | | Color | Light (felsic) | Very dark (ultramafic) | | Quartz | Abundant | Absent or rare | | Density | Lower (~2.65 g/cm³) | Higher (~3.2 g/cm³) | | Viscosity of magma | High (explosive eruptions) | Low (effusive flow) | | Example (Plutonic) | **Granite** | **Peridotite** | | Example (Volcanic) | **Rhyolite** | **Komatiite** | *(3 marks)* --- ## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > Per KTU 2024 ESE Pattern, Module 2 contains internal choice questions of 14 marks with sub-parts typically of (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students are expected to answer **ONE full question** (either A or B). ### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2]** **(a) Explain the chemical classification of igneous rocks in detail with examples of each class. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Introduction (1 mark):** The chemical classification of igneous rocks is based on the **silica (SiO₂) content** as determined by whole-rock chemical analysis. The system was developed in the early 20th century and is widely used in petrology for both intrusive and extrusive rocks. **Classification Table (4 marks):** | Class | SiO₂ Range | Key Features | Plutonic Example | Volcanic Example | |---|---|---|---|---| | Acidic | > 65% | Excess quartz; light-colored (felsic); high viscosity magma | **Granite** | **Rhyolite** | | Intermediate | 65 – 52% | Little or no quartz; mixed felsic-mafic minerals | **Diorite / Syenite** | **Andesite / Trachyte** | | Basic | 52 – 45% | No quartz; Ca-plagioclase + pyroxene dominant | **Gabbro** | **Basalt** | | Ultrabasic | < 45% | No feldspar; olivine + pyroxene only | **Peridotite / Dunite** | **Komatiite** (rare) | **Engineering Significance (2 marks):** - Acidic rocks (granite) — preferred for foundations, monuments, and decorative use. - Basic rocks (basalt) — preferred for road metal, railway ballast, and concrete aggregates. - Ultrabasic rocks (peridotite) — host rocks for **chromite, diamond, and platinum** deposits. --- **(b) Describe the mineralogical (QAPF) classification of igneous rocks as recommended by IUGS. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Basis (1 mark):** The **IUGS (International Union of Geological Sciences)** classification, also called the **Streckeisen Classification**, classifies igneous rocks based on the **modal mineral composition** (actual volume percentages of minerals visible in thin section under a petrographic microscope). **Modal Parameters (2 marks):** - **Q = Quartz** (and other SiO₂ polymorphs) - **A = Alkali Feldspar** (orthoclase, microcline, albite ≤ An₅) - **P = Plagioclase Feldspar** (An₅ to An₁₀₀) - **F = Feldspathoids** (nepheline, leucite, sodalite) For QAP classification (quartz-bearing/saturated rocks): $\text{Q} + \text{A} + \text{P} = 100\%$ For FAP classification (quartz-absent/undersaturated): $\text{F} + \text{A} + \text{P} = 100\%$ **Key Classification Rules (2 marks):** 1. Q > 20% → Granite family (Granite, Granodiorite, Tonalite) 2. A > 90% of (A+P) → Alkali Syenite 3. P > 90% of (A+P) → Gabbro / Diorite (depending on An content) 4. F > 10% with no Q → Foid-bearing syenite / Nepheline Syenite 5. Mafic minerals > 90% → Ultramafic (Peridotite, Dunite, Pyroxenite) **IUGS Rock Names with Modal Ranges (2 marks):** | Rock | Q% | A% of (A+P) | Mafic% | |---|---|---|---| | Granite | 20–60 | 65–90 | < 20 | | Granodiorite | 20–60 | 35–65 | < 20 | | Tonalite | 20–60 | 10–35 | < 20 | | Syenite | 0–5 | 90–100 | < 20 | | Diorite | 0–5 | 10–35 | 20–40 | | Gabbro | 0–5 | 0–10 | 40–70 | | Peridotite | 0 | 0 | 70–100 | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (QAPF):** > - ❌ Do NOT confuse **modal %** (actual counted minerals) with **normative %** (calculated from chemical analysis using CIPW norm). The IUGS QAPF system is **strictly modal-based**. > - ❌ Do NOT plot volcanic rocks on the QAPF plutonic diagram — use the **TAS diagram** for volcanic rocks. > - ❌ Students often forget to **normalize** Q, A, P to 100% (excluding accessories like biotite, hornblende, magnetite). --- ### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]** **(a) Describe the mineral gypsum in detail. Include its properties, varieties, formation, and engineering applications. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Definition & Chemistry (1 mark):** Gypsum is a soft, hydrous **calcium sulfate** mineral with formula $\text{CaSO}_4 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$, formed as an evaporite mineral. It contains **20.9% water of crystallization** by weight. **Physical Properties (2 marks):** - Hardness: **2 (Mohs)** — can be scratched by fingernail - Specific Gravity: **2.32** - Crystal System: **Monoclinic** - Cleavage: Perfect on {010}, distinct on {100} and {011} - Luster: Subvitreous to silky; **Streak: White** - Color: Colorless, white, gray, yellowish, pink - Tenacity: Slightly sectile / flexible but inelastic - Solubility: 2.0 g/L in water at 20°C **Varieties (1.5 marks):** 1. **Selenite** — large, transparent, well-formed crystals 2. **Satin Spar** — fibrous variety with silky luster 3. **Alabaster** — fine-grained, massive, translucent 4. **Rock Gypsum** — massive, impure form (most common in nature) **Formation (1 mark):** Gypsum forms in sedimentary environments through evaporation of saline waters in lagoons, playas, and restricted marine basins. The precipitation sequence is: Calcite → Gypsum → Halite → Sylvite (in order of increasing solubility). **Engineering Applications (1.5 marks):** - **Cement industry:** 2–5% gypsum added to retard setting of cement (controls flash set of C₃A). - **Plaster of Paris (POP):** Calcined at 120–190°C to produce hemihydrate plaster. - **Soil stabilization:** Used in agriculture to improve alkaline soils. - **Drywall/Plasterboard:** Gypsum panels for fire-resistant construction. - **Decorative use:** Alabaster sculptures and ornaments. - **Gypsum karst hazards:** Sinkhole formation in foundation engineering. --- **(b) Explain the mineralogical classification of igneous rocks. Describe the granite, gabbro, and peridotite families with their characteristic minerals. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Introduction (1 mark):** The mineralogical classification identifies igneous rocks based on the **types and proportions of minerals** they contain. This system is the **IUGS standard (QAPF)** for naming rocks, requiring microscopic modal analysis in thin section. **Basis of Classification (1 mark):** - **Light-colored (felsic) minerals:** Quartz, Feldspars, Muscovite - **Dark-colored (mafic) minerals:** Pyroxene, Hornblende, Biotite, Olivine - **Color Index (CI):** Volume % of mafic minerals in the rock **Granite Family (1.5 marks):** - **Type:** Acidic, Plutonic - **Essential minerals:** Quartz (>20%), Alkali Feldspar, Plagioclase - **Accessory minerals:** Biotite, Hornblende, Muscovite - **Color Index:** < 20% (felsic-dominant) - **Occurrence:** Continental crust, batholiths, plutons - **Engineering use:** Monument stone, building stone, foundation rock **Gabbro Family (2 marks):** - **Type:** Basic, Plutonic - **Essential minerals:** Ca-rich Plagioclase (labradorite-bytownite), Pyroxene (augite) - **Accessory minerals:** Olivine, Hornblende, Magnetite, Ilmenite - **Color Index:** 40–70% (mafic-dominant) - **Texture:** Phaneritic, often equigranular - **Occurrence:** Oceanic crust (Layer 3), layered intrusions (e.g., Bushveld Complex) - **Engineering use:** Crushed stone aggregate, road metal, dimension stone (polished gabbro = "black granite") **Peridotite Family (1.5 marks):** - **Type:** Ultrabasic, Ultramafic - **Essential minerals:** Olivine + Pyroxene (orthopyroxene and clinopyroxene) - **Accessory minerals:** Chromite, Garnet (pyrope), Spinel - **Color Index:** > 90% (ultramafic) - **Subtypes:** - **Dunite:** > 90% olivine - **Lherzolite:** Olivine + orthopyroxene + clinopyroxene - **Harzburgite:** Olivine + orthopyroxene - **Occurrence:** Upper mantle, ophiolite sequences, kimberlite pipes - **Engineering/Economic significance:** Host rock for **diamond, chromite, nickel, and platinum** deposits > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Mineralogy):** > - ❌ Confusing **gabbro** (basic, plutonic, Ca-plagioclase) with **diorite** (intermediate, plutonic, Na-plagioclase) — both are dark, but their **plagioclase composition** is the key differentiator. > - ❌ Writing **"peridotite is a volcanic rock"** — it is **plutonic/ultramafic**; its volcanic equivalent (extremely rare) is **komatiite**. > - ❌ Omitting accessory minerals — examiners award marks for listing biotite, hornblende, etc., in granite descriptions. > - ❌ Stating SiO₂ as 90% for granite — typical granite is **~72% SiO₂**, not 90%. --- ## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST ### 🟦 GYPSUM — Key Points - ✅ Chemical formula: $\text{CaSO}_4 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ (hydrous calcium sulfate) - ✅ Composition: CaO = 32.6%, SO₃ = 46.5%, H₂O = 20.9% - ✅ Hardness = 2 (Mohs); Specific Gravity = 2.32 - ✅ Crystal System = Monoclinic; Cleavage = Perfect on {010} - ✅ Three varieties: **Selenite** (transparent), **Satin Spar** (fibrous), **Alabaster** (massive) - ✅ Forms as **evaporite** in arid sedimentary basins - ✅ Calcined (120–190°C) to produce **Plaster of Paris (POP)** — $\text{CaSO}_4 \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O}$ - ✅ Used as **cement retarder (2–5%)** in civil engineering - ✅ **Gypsum karst** can cause sinkholes in dam/tunnel foundations ### 🟧 IGNEOUS ROCKS — Chemical Classification - ✅ Based on **SiO₂ percentage** (whole-rock chemistry) - ✅ **Acidic** (>65%): Granite, Rhyolite - ✅ **Intermediate** (65–52%): Diorite/Syenite, Andesite/Trachyte - ✅ **Basic** (52–45%): Gabbro, Basalt - ✅ **Ultrabasic** (<45%): Peridotite, Komatiite - ✅ **Color Index** = (Mafic Mineral % / Total Mineral %) × 100 ### 🟥 IGNEOUS ROCKS — Mineralogical (IUGS QAPF) Classification - ✅ Based on **modal mineral composition** (Q, A, P, F percentages) - ✅ **Q** = Quartz; **A** = Alkali Feldspar; **P** = Plagioclase; **F** = Feldspathoids - ✅ **QAP Triangle** for silica-saturated (quartz-bearing) rocks - ✅ **FAP Triangle** for silica-undersaturated (feldspathoid-bearing) rocks - ✅ **Granite**: Q > 20%, A > P; accessory biotite/hornblende - ✅ **Diorite**: P > A (intermediate composition plagioclase) - ✅ **Gabbro**: P > A (Ca-rich plagioclase + pyroxene) - ✅ **Peridotite/Dunite**: M (Mafics) > 90%; olivine + pyroxene - ✅ **Volcanic equivalents** differ in texture, not mineralogy: Granite ↔ Rhyolite; Gabbro ↔ Basalt; Diorite ↔ Andesite - ✅ Engineering uses: **Granite** → foundation/decoration; **Basalt** → road aggregate; **Gabbro** → "black granite" dimension stone > [!IMPORTANT] > **One-Line Memory Trick for the Exam:** > *"Gypsum = Good Old CaSO₄·2H₂O"* | *"Acidic → Acid (lots of SiO₂); Ultrabasic → Almost no SiO₂"* | *"QAPF: Quartz, Alkali-fspar, Plagioclase, Feldspathoid — remember as **Q-A-P-F = Quality-Assessment-Program-For igneous rocks**."*

topics.title: Structures and Textures. Sedimentary Rocks - Types Based on Mode of Formation and Structures. Metamorphic Rocks - Structures Only. Megascopic Study of Granite
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET416 - ENGINEERING GEOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Structures and Textures. Sedimentary Rocks - Types Based on Mode of Formation and Structures. Metamorphic Rocks - Structures Only. Megascopic Study of Granite

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Rock Texture — The "Recipe" of a Rock

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Texture** is the *geometrical* aspect of the rock fabric — it describes the **size, shape, arrangement, and mutual relationship of the mineral grains** that make up a rock, observed at hand-specimen or microscopic scale.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, texture is a **primary feature** formed at the time of rock crystallization (igneous), deposition (sedimentary), or recrystallization (metamorphic).

### Conceptual Analogy — "Texture is the Rice Dish"
Think of a rock like a plate of **biryani**:
- The **size** of the rice grains = *grain size* (fine, medium, coarse)
- The **shape** of the rice grains = *grain form* (rounded, angular, flaky)
- The **grain-to-grain relationship** = *fabric* (whether the pieces are tightly packed, randomly oriented, or aligned in layers)
- The **overall feel on the tongue** = the rock's *texture*

Two biryanis can have the same ingredients but completely different textures — just as two granites can have the same minerals yet look vastly different.

## 1.2 Rock Structure — The "Architecture" of a Rock

> [!NOTE]
> **Structure** refers to the *larger, megascopic features* of a rock mass that result from **geological processes acting during or after** its formation. Structures are typically visible at outcrop scale (centimeters to meters).

### Conceptual Analogy — "Structure is the Building's Architecture"
If a rock were a **building**:
- **Texture** = the type of bricks, cement, and tiles used (small-scale, internal)
- **Structure** = the floor plan, balconies, pillars, and the cracks on the wall (large-scale, external)

A building can be made of the same bricks but have completely different architecture — and vice versa.

## 1.3 Why This Distinction Matters in Engineering Geology

For a civil engineer, the **structure** of a rock often controls:
- **Stability of slopes** in tunnels and open excavations
- **Direction of sliding** in landslides
- **Anisotropy of strength** (a foliated rock is strong *across* foliation but weak *along* it)
- **Permeability** and groundwater flow paths

The **texture** controls:
- **Strength and durability** of building stone
- **Weathering behavior** (coarse-grained rocks weather faster due to higher surface area)
- **Polishing suitability** (fine-grained, equigranular stones polish better)
- **Foundation bearing capacity**

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Valuation Tip:** Examiners frequently allot 2–3 marks for a *clean distinction* between texture and structure. Memorize: *Texture = small-scale, mineral-grain-level; Structure = large-scale, rock-mass-level.*

## 1.4 Sedimentary Rocks — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sedimentary rocks** are secondary rocks formed at the Earth's surface (low P, low T) by the **accumulation, compaction, and cementation (lithification)** of pre-existing rock fragments, mineral grains, organic matter, or chemical precipitates.

Average coverage of Earth's surface: **~75%** (only ~5% by volume of the crust, but extremely important for civil engineering because most foundations, tunnels, and reservoirs are built on/in them).

## 1.5 Metamorphic Rocks — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Metamorphic rocks** are formed when pre-existing igneous, sedimentary, or other metamorphic rocks are subjected to **elevated temperature (T) and/or pressure (P)** *without melting*, leading to **recrystallization, mineralogical change, and textural reorganization**.

Key parameter ranges for metamorphism:
- **Temperature:** ~200 °C to ~800 °C
- **Pressure:** ~1 kbar to ~10 kbar
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Analytical Matrix

## 2.1 Master Classification of Rock Textures

| **Texture Family** | **Sub-Texture** | **Defining Feature** | **Typical Rock Example** | **Engineering Significance** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Phaneritic** (visible grains) | Coarse (> 5 mm) | All grains visible to naked eye | Granite, Gabbro | Excellent dimensional stone; high strength |
| | Medium (1–5 mm) | Grains clearly visible | Dolerite, Diorite | Good for aggregate |
| | Fine (< 1 mm) | Grains barely visible | Aphanitic basalts | Tough, used as road metal |
| **Aphanitic** (invisible grains) | Glassy | No crystals, glassy | Obsidian, Pumice | Brittle, not used as aggregate |
| **Porphyritic** | — | Large *phenocrysts* in fine *groundmass* | Porphyritic granite | Two-stage cooling; variable strength |
| **Equigranular** | — | All grains roughly equal size | Dunite, Syenite | Homogeneous, isotropic strength |
| **Inequigranular** | — | Marked size contrast | Pegmatite | Coarse crystals create cleavage planes |
| **Clastic (Sed.)** | — | Fragments cemented in matrix | Sandstone, Breccia | Porosity, permeability, weak cement = problem |
| **Crystalline (Met.)** | Granoblastic | Equant, polygonal grains | Marble, Quartzite | High strength, low anisotropy |
| | Lepidoblastic | Flaky minerals aligned | Schist | **Strong anisotropy** — slope failure risk |
| | Nematoblastic | Needle-like minerals aligned | Amphibolite | Anisotropic strength |

## 2.2 Sedimentary Rocks — Classification Based on Mode of Formation

### A. Mechanically Formed (Clastic / Detrital) Rocks
Formed by **mechanical disintegration** of pre-existing rocks followed by **transport, deposition, and lithification**.

- **RUDACEOUS (Rudytes)** — grain size > **2 mm**
  - *Gravels* (unconsolidated) → *Conglomerate* (rounded fragments) or *Breccia* (angular fragments)
- **ARENACEOUS (Arenites)** — grain size **0.0625 mm to 2 mm**
  - *Sand* (loose) → *Sandstone* (cemented). Varieties: Quartzitic, Ferruginous, Calcareous, Argillaceous sandstones
- **ARGILLACEOUS (Lutites)** — grain size < **0.0625 mm**
  - *Clay / Silt* (loose) → *Shale* (fissile) or *Mudstone* (non-fissile)

### B. Chemically Formed (Non-Clastic / Orthochemical) Rocks
Formed by **precipitation from aqueous solutions** (chemical sedimentation).

- **Calcareous** — Limestone (CaCO₃), Travertine, Tufa
- **Siliceous** — Chert, Flint, Jasper (SiO₂)
- **Ferruginous** — Ironstones, Bog iron ore (Fe-oxides/hydroxides)
- **Carbonaceous** — Coal (from plant matter under pressure)
- **Evaporites** — Rock Salt (NaCl), Gypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O), Anhydrite (CaSO₄)
- **Phosphatic** — Phosphorite, Guano deposits

### C. Organically Formed (Biogenic / Biochemical) Rocks
Formed by **accumulation of organic remains** (shells, skeletons, plant debris) often with associated chemical precipitation.

- **Calcareous** — Fossiliferous Limestone, Chalk (coccolithophores), Coral Limestone
- **Siliceous** — Diatomaceous Earth (diatoms), Radiolarian Ooze
- **Carbonaceous** — Coal, Peat, Lignite, Bituminous Coal, Anthracite
- **Phosphatic** — Bone beds, Guano

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic for Sedimentary Types:** **"MAO"** — *Mechanical, Accumulation (chemical), Organic.* Examiners love asking students to give 2 examples under each.

## 2.3 Sedimentary Structures — Master List

| **Category** | **Structure** | **Description** | **Engineering Importance** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical (Physical)** | Stratification / Bedding | Layers of differing composition/grain size | Planes of weakness → sliding surfaces |
| | Lamination | Very thin (< 1 cm) layers | Tendency to split |
| | Cross-bedding | Inclined laminations within a bed | Indicates paleocurrent; anisotropy |
| | Ripple Marks | Wave/current-induced surface undulations | Marks bedding plane orientation |
| | Mud Cracks | Polygonal desiccation cracks | Top-of-bed indicator; permeability |
| | Rain Prints | Small impact craters | Top-of-bed indicator |
| | Graded Bedding | Gradual size change vertically | Right-way-up indicator |
| **Chemical** | Concretions | Spherical / nodular masses (e.g., flint) | Localized hardness contrasts |
| | Nodules | Irregular mineral segregations | Engineering inhomogeneity |
| | Geodes | Hollow, crystal-lined cavities | Borehole obstruction potential |
| | Stylolites | Suture-like seams from dissolution | Planes of weakness |
| **Organic** | Fossils | Remains or traces of organisms | Stratigraphic & age indicators |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** A "fissile" shale splits along *lamination planes*. A "non-fissile" mudstone of identical composition does NOT. This distinction is a **favourite 3-mark question.**

## 2.4 Metamorphic Rocks — Structures Only (KTU Module 2 Scope)

### A. Foliated Structures (Planar / Linear fabric — *directed stress*)

- **Slaty Cleavage** — finest, planar; rocks split into smooth sheets. Rock: **Slate**
- **Phyllitic Structure** — silky sheen, crenulated sheets. Rock: **Phyllite**
- **Schistosity** — parallel arrangement of platy/prismatic minerals (mica, hornblende). Rock: **Schist**
- **Gneissose Structure (Gneissic Banding)** — alternating light (felsic) and dark (mafic) mineral bands. Rock: **Gneiss**

### B. Non-Foliated (Massive / Granulose) Structures — *uniform stress / contact metamorphism*

- **Granular / Granoblastic Structure** — equidimensional, interlocking grains. Rock: **Marble, Quartzite**
- **Porphyroblastic Structure** — large crystals (*porphyroblasts*) in finer groundmass. Rock: **Some schists, gneisses**
- **Cataclastic Structure** — crushed, granulated, mortar-like. Rock: **Mylonite, Fault Gouge**
- **Augen Structure** — large lenticular feldspar "eyes" wrapped by foliation. Rock: **Augen Gneiss**
- **Hornfelsic Structure** — fine, dense, sharp, splintery. Rock: **Hornfels** (contact metamorphism)

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Engineering Geology Module 2

> [!NOTE]
> Unlike calculus-based subjects, Engineering Geology Module 2 uses *identification matrices* rather than numerical formulas. The "formulas" are essentially **identification keys** and **property relationships.**

| **Parameter / Key** | **Relationship / Definition** | **Units / Note** |
|---|---|---|
| Wentworth Grain-Size Scale (clastic) | Gravel > 2 mm > Sand > 0.0625 mm > Silt > Clay | mm |
| Fissility of Shale | Fissile if $\text{Lamina thickness} < 1 \text{ cm}$ | cm |
| Specific Gravity of Granite | $G_s \approx 2.65$ to $2.75$ | dimensionless |
| UCS of Granite (typical) | $\sigma_c \approx 100$ to $250 \text{ MPa}$ | MPa |
| Porosity of Sandstone | $n \approx 5\%$ to $30\%$ | % |
| Porosity of Shale | $n \approx 10\%$ to $40\%$ (but very low permeability) | % |
| Porosity of Crystalline Rocks | $n < 2\%$ (joints/fractures dominate flow) | % |
| Anisotropy Ratio (foliated) | $R = \dfrac{UCS_{\parallel}}{UCS_{\perp}} \approx 0.4$ to $0.7$ | dimensionless |
| Darcy's Law (flow in porous sedimentary) | $Q = k \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{dh}{dl}$ | m³/s |
| Calcium Carbonate Test | Limestone effervesces vigorously with dilute HCl | qualitative |

**Note on Darcy's Law:** Used in the context of *sedimentary rocks with high primary porosity* (sand, gravel, sandstone). For crystalline igneous/metamorphic rocks, secondary porosity from joints and fractures dominates.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Tunnelling in Schist:** The *foliation planes* act as sliding surfaces. Tunnel alignment is chosen **perpendicular to the dominant foliation dip** to minimize wedge failure.
2. **Dam Foundation on Sandstone:** *Cross-bedding* and *clay partings* within sandstone cause *seepage* problems; *grouting* is required along bedding planes.
3. **Building Stone Selection:** *Equigranular, holocrystalline* granite is preferred over *porphyritic* varieties for dimension stone because the latter fails preferentially along phenocryst boundaries.
4. **Slope Stability in Shale:** *Mud cracks, lamination, and fissility* all combine to make shale a *highly anisotropic* and problematic material in cuts.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Identification Procedures & Analytical Matrices

> [!NOTE]
> Module 2 of Engineering Geology is fundamentally an *identification* module. The "derivation" equivalent is the **step-by-step megascopic identification procedure** for any given rock in hand specimen. Below is the exhaustive KTU-recommended analytical pathway.

## 3.1 Universal Rock Identification Algorithm (KTU Board Pattern)

**Step 1 — Determine the Rock Family (3 options):**
- Is it crystalline with interlocking grains? → Could be **Igneous or Metamorphic**
- Is it layered or fossiliferous? → Likely **Sedimentary**
- Does it have foliation? → **Metamorphic**

**Step 2 — Sedimentary Sub-Classification:**
- Can you see distinct grains/fragments with cement? → **Clastic**
- Does it fizz vigorously with HCl? → **Calcareous (chemical or organic)**
- Is it black, lightweight, with plant remains? → **Carbonaceous (Coal family)**
- Does it have a salty/bitter taste? → **Evaporite**

**Step 3 — Metamorphic Sub-Classification:**
- Is foliation present?
  - **Yes** → Foliated (Slate / Phyllite / Schist / Gneiss)
  - **No** → Non-foliated (Marble / Quartzite / Hornfels)
- If foliated, what is the *grain size* and *sheen*?
  - Very fine, dull, smooth → **Slate**
  - Very fine, silky sheen → **Phyllite**
  - Visible flakes of mica → **Schist**
  - Banded light/dark → **Gneiss**

## 3.2 Detailed Megascopic Identification Matrix — Sedimentary Rocks

| **Step** | **Observation** | **Inference** | **Next Step** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Visible rock fragments > 2 mm, rounded | Gravel → **Conglomerate** | Check cement type (siliceous = strong) |
| 2 | Visible rock fragments > 2 mm, angular | Gravel → **Breccia** | Confirm angularity |
| 3 | Sand-sized grains, gritty feel | **Sandstone** | Check cement (siliceous/calcareous/ferruginous/argillaceous) |
| 4 | Very fine, smooth, plastic feel when wet | **Clay / Mudstone / Shale** | Test fissility: splits in sheets? → Shale |
| 5 | Reacts vigorously with cold dilute HCl | **Limestone** | Check fossils → organic origin |
| 6 | Reacts weakly only with warm HCl | **Dolomite** | — |
| 7 | Hard, dense, conchoidal fracture, brown/black | **Chert / Flint** | Scratch test — Chert scratches steel |
| 8 | Reddish-brown, heavy, rust-colored | **Ironstone / Laterite** | Specific gravity test |
| 9 | Black, burns with smoke, low density | **Coal family** | Streak, hardness to differentiate peat → anthracite |
| 10 | Tastes salty | **Rock Salt (Halite)** | Solubility test |
| 11 | Crystalline, soft (fingernail scratches) | **Gypsum** | Confirm with solubility in HCl |
| 12 | Contains visible fossils/shells | **Fossiliferous Limestone** | Classify as organic |

## 3.3 Detailed Megascopic Identification Matrix — Metamorphic Rocks (Structures Focus)

| **Observed Structure** | **Mineralogy** | **Rock Name** | **Parent Rock** | **Metamorphic Grade** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Slaty cleavage (smooth sheets) | Microscopic micas, chlorite | **Slate** | Shale / Claystone | Low |
| Phyllitic (silky sheen) | Fine sericite, chlorite, mica | **Phyllite** | Shale / Claystone | Low–Medium |
| Schistosity (visible mica flakes) | Mica, garnet, kyanite, staurolite | **Schist** | Shale, basalt, etc. | Medium |
| Gneissose banding | Quartz, feldspar, mica alternating bands | **Gneiss** | Granite, shale | High |
| Granular (no foliation) | Calcite | **Marble** | Limestone | Any (contact/regional) |
| Granular (no foliation) | Quartz | **Quartzite** | Sandstone | Any |
| Porphyroblastic | Large garnet / staurolite / andalusite in matrix | **Porphyroblastic Schist / Gneiss** | Shale | Medium–High |
| Augen structure | Lenticular feldspar "eyes" | **Augen Gneiss** | Granite / Gneiss | High (deformation) |
| Cataclastic (crushed) | Mortar texture, granulated | **Mylonite** | Any (fault zones) | Dynamic metamorphism |
| Hornfelsic (fine, dense, splintery) | Microscopic, equigranular | **Hornfels** | Shale, basalt | Contact (high T, low P) |

## 3.4 Megascopic Identification of Granite — Complete KTU-Style Procedure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Granite is the most frequently asked igneous rock in KTU Engineering Geology. The following exhaustive procedure carries **direct board-exam value**.

### Step 1 — Confirm Igneous Origin
- The rock is **crystalline**, with **interlocking grains** (no layers, no fossils, no foliation visible in a typical fresh specimen).

### Step 2 — Confirm Plutonic (Intrusive) Origin
- All mineral grains are **visible to the naked eye** → *phaneritic texture*.
- This indicates **slow cooling at depth** → *intrusive / plutonic*.

### Step 3 — Identify the Essential Minerals

| **Mineral** | **Megascopic Property** | **Approx. %** | **Role in Identification** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Quartz | Glassy, grey, no cleavage, conchoidal fracture | 20–40% | Distinguishes granite from gabbro/diorite |
| Orthoclase Feldspar | Pink / white, two cleavages at 90°, pearly lustre | 30–60% | Causes *pink coloration* if K-rich |
| Plagioclase Feldspar | White/grey, striations on cleavage faces | 10–30% | Distinguished by striations |
| Biotite Mica | Black, shiny flakes, perfect basal cleavage | 5–15% | Distinguishes from syenite |
| Muscovite Mica | Silvery, transparent flakes | Up to 5% | Optional accessory |
| Hornblende | Black, prismatic, 60°/120° cleavage | 0–10% | When dominant → diorite |

### Step 4 — Textural Description
- **Crystallization:** Holocrystalline (entirely crystalline)
- **Grain size:** Coarse-grained (> 5 mm; *pegmatitic* if > 20 mm)
- **Fabric:** Equigranular (most granites) or slightly porphyritic
- **Structure:** Typically **massive**, often **jointed** in the field

### Step 5 — Distinguishing Granite from Similar Rocks

| **Property** | **Granite** | **Syenite** | **Diorite** | **Gabbro** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Quartz | **Present (20–40%)** | Absent / < 5% | Absent / < 5% | Absent |
| Dominant Feldspar | Orthoclase (pink) | Orthoclase (pink) | Plagioclase (white) | Plagioclase (grey) |
| Color | Light grey, pink | Pink to grey | Salt-and-pepper | Dark grey to black |
| Specific Gravity | $G_s \approx 2.65$ | $G_s \approx 2.75$ | $G_s \approx 2.85$ | $G_s \approx 3.00$ |

### Step 6 — Engineering Properties of Granite

- **Compressive Strength:** $\sigma_c \approx 100$ to $250 \text{ MPa}$
- **Specific Gravity:** $G_s \approx 2.65$ to $2.75$
- **Porosity:** $n < 1\%$ (secondary porosity via joints only)
- **Durability:** High — resistant to weathering and chemical attack
- **Workability:** Excellent for dressing, polishing; difficult to quarry by hand
- **Uses:** Building stone, monuments, bridge piers, aggregate, kerb stones, cladding, flooring
- **Avoid in:** Highly acidic environments (feldspar decomposes); coastal regions with salt spray (efflorescence)

### Step 7 — Field Structures in Granite
- **Massive structure** in fresh outcrops
- **Jointing** — most common structure; typically in three sets (sheeting, cross-joints, longitudinal)
- **Exfoliation / Sheeting** — curved sheets parallel to topographic surface (due to pressure release on erosion)
- **Blocky disintegration** — produces corestones and spheroidal weathering
- Sometimes **gneissose banding** at margins of the pluton (due to flow during emplacement)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** A "pink granite" gets its color from the **orthoclase feldspar**, NOT from any iron oxide. A common error students make is attributing pink color to iron-oxide staining.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
