# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The following questions are modeled on the KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE) pattern for PECET416 Engineering Geology. Marks are calibrated as per University guidelines: **Part A (3 marks)** and **Part B (14 marks)** with internal choice.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]
**Differentiate between rock texture and rock structure. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Texture** is the small-scale, *internal* geometrical arrangement of mineral grains — their **size, shape, and mutual relationship**. It is observed at hand-specimen or microscopic scale. *Examples:* phaneritic texture (granite), porphyritic texture (porphyritic andesite).
- **Structure** is the large-scale, *external* megascopic feature of a rock mass produced during or after its formation. It is observed at outcrop scale. *Examples:* bedding in sandstone, foliation in schist, mud cracks in shale.
- **Key distinction:** Texture deals with grains (mm–cm); structure deals with the rock body (cm–m).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (1–2 mark loss):** Students often give vague answers like *"texture is the feel of the rock."* Examiners want *specific* grain-level features: size, shape, orientation, packing.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]
**What is foliation? Name any two foliated metamorphic rocks.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Foliation** is the **planar or linear parallel arrangement of mineral grains** in a metamorphic rock, produced by *directed (differential) stress* during regional metamorphism.
- It results in the rock splitting preferentially along parallel planes.
- *Two foliated metamorphic rocks:* **Slate** (exhibits slaty cleavage) and **Schist** (exhibits schistosity). Other valid answers: Phyllite, Gneiss.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]

**(a)** Classify **sedimentary rocks** based on their **mode of formation**. Give at least **two examples** under each category. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **megascopic identification of granite** in detail, including its mineralogy, texture, and engineering properties. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a):

Sedimentary rocks are classified into **three** main groups based on the mode of formation:

**1. Mechanically Formed (Clastic / Detrital) Rocks:**
- Formed by *mechanical weathering* of pre-existing rocks → *transport* → *deposition* → *lithification* (compaction + cementation).
- *Rudaceous (gravel, > 2 mm):* **Conglomerate** (rounded fragments), **Breccia** (angular fragments).
- *Arenaceous (sand, 0.0625–2 mm):* **Sandstone** — further classified by cement: siliceous, calcareous, ferruginous, argillaceous.
- *Argillaceous (clay/silt, < 0.0625 mm):* **Shale** (fissile), **Mudstone** (non-fissile).

**2. Chemically Formed (Non-Clastic / Precipitated) Rocks:**
- Formed by *chemical precipitation* from aqueous solutions through evaporation, oxidation, or other inorganic processes.
- **Calcareous:** Limestone (CaCO₃), Travertine
- **Siliceous:** Chert, Flint
- **Ferruginous:** Ironstone
- **Evaporites:** Rock Salt (NaCl), Gypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O)
- **Carbonaceous:** Some coals of chemical origin

**3. Organically Formed (Biogenic) Rocks:**
- Formed by *accumulation of organic remains* — shells, skeletons, plant debris — with or without associated chemical precipitation.
- **Calcareous:** Fossiliferous Limestone, Chalk (from coccolithophores), Coral Limestone
- **Siliceous:** Diatomaceous Earth (from diatoms)
- **Carbonaceous:** **Coal series** — Peat → Lignite → Bituminous → Anthracite (increasing rank)
- **Phosphatic:** Guano, Phosphorite

> [!TIP]
> **Memo-writing tip:** Use a *tabular format* in the answer sheet. Examiners reward clear organization with 1–2 bonus marks.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b):

**Megascopic Identification of Granite**

**1. Rock Family and Origin:**
- Granite is an **intrusive (plutonic) igneous rock** of the granitic family (acidic composition, $\text{SiO}_2 > 65\%$).
- Forms by **slow cooling of magma at depth**, allowing visible crystal growth.

**2. Mineralogy (Essential + Accessory):**

| **Mineral** | **Megascopic Property** | **Approx. %** |
|---|---|---|
| Quartz | Glassy, grey, no cleavage, conchoidal fracture | 20–40% |
| Orthoclase Feldspar (K-feldspar) | Pink/white, two cleavages at 90°, pearly lustre | 30–60% |
| Plagioclase Feldspar | White-grey, striated cleavage | 10–30% |
| Biotite Mica | Black, shiny, perfect basal cleavage | 5–15% |
| Muscovite Mica | Silvery, transparent | 0–5% |
| Hornblende | Black, prismatic, two cleavages at 60°/120° | 0–10% |

**3. Texture:**
- **Holocrystalline** (entirely crystalline, no glass)
- **Phaneritic** (all grains visible to naked eye, > 5 mm — coarse)
- **Equigranular** (grains roughly equal size); sometimes *porphyritic*
- **Massive structure** in fresh hand specimen; *jointed* in outcrop

**4. Distinguishing Features:**
- Presence of **quartz** distinguishes granite from syenite (no quartz) and gabbro (no quartz, dark).
- **Pink color** of orthoclase is diagnostic; salt-and-pepper look indicates plagioclase-rich varieties (granodiorite).

**5. Specific Gravity and Strength:**
- $G_s \approx 2.65$ to $2.75$
- UCS: $\sigma_c \approx 100$ to $250 \text{ MPa}$

**6. Engineering Properties and Uses:**
- High strength, durability, weather resistance.
- Excellent for **dimensional stone**: building façades, flooring, cladding, monuments, kerbstones.
- Aggregate for high-strength concrete; bridge piers; dam foundations (where available).
- Polishes well due to interlocking crystal mosaic.
- **Limitations:** vulnerable to *acidic* environments (feldspar alteration); high quarrying cost; heavy → transportation cost.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse granite with **gneiss**. A granite is *non-foliated* and randomly oriented. A gneiss has *alternating mineral bands*. If banding is visible, it is **gneiss**, not granite.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]

**(a)** Explain the **structures of metamorphic rocks** in detail, with examples of foliated and non-foliated varieties. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the important **mechanical (physical) structures of sedimentary rocks** with neat sketches. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a):

**Metamorphic Structures — Detailed Account**

Metamorphic structures are broadly classified into two groups: **foliated** and **non-foliated (massive/granulose).**

**A. Foliated Structures** — produced by *directed pressure* (regional metamorphism); minerals align perpendicular to the maximum principal stress.

1. **Slaty Cleavage** — finest foliation; rocks split into thin, smooth, flat sheets. *Example:* **Slate** (parent: shale, low-grade metamorphism).
2. **Phyllitic Structure** — slightly coarser than slaty; exhibits a characteristic *silky / pearly sheen* (phyllitic lustre) due to microscopic sericite/mica flakes. *Example:* **Phyllite** (parent: shale, low–medium grade).
3. **Schistosity** — coarse, conspicuous parallel arrangement of platy minerals (mica, chlorite, talc) and/or prismatic minerals (hornblende). *Example:* **Schist** (parent: shale, basalt; medium grade).
4. **Gneissose Structure (Gneissic Banding)** — alternating *felsic* (light, quartz-feldspar-rich) and *mafic* (dark, mica/hornblende-rich) mineral bands. *Example:* **Gneiss** (parent: granite, shale; high grade).

**B. Non-Foliated Structures** — produced by *uniform pressure* or *contact metamorphism* (heat-dominant, low stress); no preferred mineral orientation.

1. **Granular / Granoblastic Structure** — interlocking equigranular crystals. *Examples:* **Marble** (from limestone), **Quartzite** (from sandstone).
2. **Porphyroblastic Structure** — large crystals (*porphyroblasts*) of garnet, staurolite, or andalusite embedded in finer groundmass. *Example:* Garnetiferous mica schist.
3. **Augen Structure** — large lenticular *augen* (eyes) of feldspar, wrapped by foliation. *Example:* **Augen Gneiss**.
4. **Cataclastic Structure** — rocks crushed and granulated along fault zones. *Examples:* **Mylonite**, **Fault Gouge**.
5. **Hornfelsic Structure** — very fine, dense, sharp-edged, splintery fracture. *Example:* **Hornfels** (parent rock: shale or basalt, contact metamorphism).

**Engineering Significance of Foliation:**
- Foliation planes are **planes of weakness**.
- Tunnels driven *parallel* to foliation experience roof collapse; tunnels *perpendicular* are more stable.
- Dam abutments and foundation slopes are oriented to *minimize sliding along foliation*.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b):

**Mechanical (Physical) Structures of Sedimentary Rocks**

These structures form during or shortly after deposition, primarily by physical processes (currents, waves, desiccation, gravity).

**1. Stratification / Bedding:**
- The most fundamental sedimentary structure.
- Sedimentary rocks occur in *layers (strata or beds)* separated by *bedding planes*.
- Beds differ in composition, grain size, color, or orientation of particles.
- *Significance:* Each bedding plane is a potential surface of weakness and a pathway for groundwater flow.

**2. Lamination:**
- Very thin layers (typically < 1 cm) within a bed.
- *Shale* is fissile precisely because it splits along lamination planes.

**3. Cross-Bedding (Cross-Stratification):**
- Inclined laminations within an individual bed, terminating against the bounding surfaces.
- Formed by *ripples, dunes, deltas, or current action*.
- *Significance:* Cross-bedding indicates the **paleocurrent direction** — important in paleogeographic reconstruction and reservoir engineering (orienting deviated boreholes).

**4. Ripple Marks:**
- Small, rhythmic undulations on bedding surfaces.
- *Asymmetric ripples* = current action (river, tidal channel).
- *Symmetric ripples* = wave action (shallow marine / lake shoreline).
- *Significance:* Marks the bedding plane orientation (helps determine "way-up" in folded strata).

**5. Mud Cracks (Desiccation Cracks):**
- Polygonal cracks formed by the drying and shrinkage of clay-rich sediment.
- Typically taper downward (V-shaped in section).
- *Significance:* Diagnostic **"top-of-bed" indicator**; indicates subaerial exposure.

**6. Rain Prints (Rain Pits):**
- Small, shallow, circular depressions formed by raindrop impact on soft mud.
- *Significance:* Also a "top-of-bed" indicator.

**7. Graded Bedding:**
- A bed in which *grain size decreases* gradually from bottom to top.
- *Significance:* Indicates settling from a turbidity current; useful "way-up" indicator in tectonically inverted strata.

**8. Conglomerate/Breccia Structure:**
- The presence of large fragments (clasts) in a finer matrix.
- Breccia: angular clasts (no transport); Conglomerate: rounded clasts (long transport).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often confuse *ripple marks* with *cross-bedding*. **Ripple marks** are undulations on the *surface* of a bed; **cross-bedding** is *internal inclined layering* within a bed. Examiners WILL deduct 1 mark if you interchange them.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Module 2 Revision Checklist**

### A. Core Definitions
- **Texture** = grain-level (size, shape, arrangement)
- **Structure** = rock-mass-level megascopic feature
- **Sedimentary rock** = formed at the Earth's surface by lithification of sediments
- **Metamorphic rock** = formed in solid state by T–P alteration of pre-existing rock

### B. Sedimentary Classification Mnemonic — **"MAO"**
- **M**echanical (clastic): Conglomerate, Breccia, Sandstone, Shale, Mudstone
- **A**ccumulation/chemical (non-clastic): Limestone, Chert, Rock Salt, Gypsum, Ironstone
- **O**rganic/biogenic: Fossiliferous Limestone, Chalk, Coal, Diatomaceous Earth, Guano

### C. Sedimentary Grain-Size Order (largest → smallest)
- **Rudaceous** > 2 mm
- **Arenaceous** 0.0625–2 mm
- **Argillaceous** < 0.0625 mm

### D. Metamorphic Foliation Sequence (increasing grade)
- **Slaty** (Slate) → **Phyllitic** (Phyllite) → **Schistose** (Schist) → **Gneissose** (Gneiss)

### E. Metamorphic Non-Foliated Rocks
- Marble, Quartzite, Hornfels, Mylonite, Augen Gneiss

### F. Granite Megascopy — Five Must-Know Facts
1. **Quartz present (20–40%)** — distinguishes from syenite, diorite, gabbro
2. **Pink color from orthoclase**, not iron oxide
3. **Phaneritic + holocrystalline + equigranular** texture
4. $G_s \approx 2.65$–$2.75$; UCS $\approx 100$–$250 \text{ MPa}$
5. Excellent building stone, monument stone, aggregate

### G. Critical Distinctions Examiners Love to Test
- **Shale vs Mudstone** → fissility (shale splits, mudstone does not)
- **Conglomerate vs Breccia** → rounded vs angular clasts
- **Limestone vs Dolomite** → HCl reaction (dolomite reacts only with warm acid)
- **Granite vs Gneiss** → foliation absent vs present
- **Slate vs Phyllite vs Schist** → grain size and sheen of foliation
- **Sandstone vs Quartzite** → sandstone is weakly cemented; quartzite is *metamorphosed* and interlocking

### H. Field Identification Toolset
- **Hand lens** (10×) for grain identification
- **Dilute HCl (5–10%)** for carbonate test
- **Streak plate** for color
- **Magnet** for magnetite-bearing rocks
- **Knife / fingernail** for hardness (Mohs scale)
- **Dilute acid on freshly broken surface** — not weathered surface

### I. Engineering Geology Numerical Anchors to Remember
- Specific gravity of granite: $G_s \approx 2.65$ to $2.75$
- UCS of granite: $\sigma_c \approx 100$ to $250 \text{ MPa}$
- Porosity of crystalline rocks: $n < 2\%$
- Porosity of sandstone: $n \approx 5\%$ to $30\%$
- Anisotropy ratio of foliated rock: $R = \sigma_{c,\parallel} / \sigma_{c,\perp} \approx 0.4$ to $0.7$

### J. Common KTU Question Patterns
1. *"Differentiate between texture and structure"* (3 marks)
2. *"Classify sedimentary rocks and give examples"* (7 marks)
3. *"Describe the megascopic study of granite"* (7 marks)
4. *"Explain the structures of metamorphic rocks"* (7 marks)
5. *"Describe the physical structures of sedimentary rocks with diagrams"* (7–14 marks)

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** Always begin long answers with a **classification table or flowchart** (1 mark bonus), then proceed with explanations. Examiners reward *structured* answers under the 2024 scheme's Outcome-Based Education (OBE) assessment pattern.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dolerite
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET416 - ENGINEERING GEOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Dolerite # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Dolerite** (also termed *Diabase* in North American geological literature) is a **medium-grained, mafic intrusive igneous rock** that crystallizes at shallow to moderate depths within the Earth's crust. It is composed essentially of **plagioclase feldspar (labradorite–bytownite composition)** and **pyroxene (predominantly augite)**, with accessory minerals such as **olivine, magnetite, ilmenite, and apatite**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** > Dolerite is treated under *Igneous Rocks – Petrography* in Module 2 of Engineering Geology. It is geologically significant because it represents a *textural intermediate* between fine-grained extrusive **basalt** and coarse-grained plutonic **gabbro**. **Petrographic Notation:** In the IUGS QAPF classification, dolerite plots within the **Gabbro–Diorite field**, having a **colour index (M) > 35**, classifying it firmly as a **mafic rock**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition Imagine baking a chocolate cake in three different ovens: | Oven Type | Cooling Rate | Resulting Cake Texture | Geological Equivalent | |---|---|---|---| | **Open air (cold)** | Very Fast | Smooth, no crystals visible | **Basalt** (volcanic, fine-grained) | | **Warm kitchen counter** | Medium | Small crystals, slightly gritty | **Dolerite** (hypabyssal, medium-grained) | | **Walk-in cooler** | Very Slow | Large visible crystals | **Gabbro** (plutonic, coarse-grained) | **Dolerite = The "middle-child" igneous rock.** It forms when basaltic magma gets trapped just below the Earth's surface in narrow cracks and chambers. Because cooling is *slow enough* to grow visible crystals but *fast enough* to keep them small, you can identify individual minerals (mostly black pyroxene and white-grey plagioclase) with the naked eye — yet the crystals are not large and blocky like in gabbro. > [!NOTE] > **Real-world signpost:** The dark, jagged boulders that often form ridges, tors, and railway ballast in peninsular India (e.g., Deccan Plateau) are predominantly dolerite. ## 1.3 Key Physical & Chemical Constants - **Density (ρ):** **2.90 – 3.10 g/cm³** - **Unconfined Compressive Strength (UCS):** **150 – 250 MPa** - **Mohs Hardness:** **5 – 7** - **Typical Silica Content (SiO₂):** **45 – 52 % by weight** (sub-basic / basic range) - **Colour Index (M):** **> 35** → Mafic - **Common Modes of Occurrence:** **Dykes, Sills, Laccoliths, Cone Sheets** # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Mineralogical Composition (Theoretical Framework) Dolerite's mineralogy is governed by the **Bowen Reaction Series** truncated at intermediate cooling rates. ### Primary (Essential) Minerals 1. **Plagioclase Feldspar (Labradorite — An₅₀ to An₇₀)** - Lath-shaped (tabular) crystals - Often shows **albite twinning** (parallel striations under microscope) - Constitutes **~ 50 – 65 %** of the rock by volume 2. **Pyroxene (Augite)** - Equant (roughly equidimensional) crystals - Forms the dark matrix enclosing plagioclase laths - Constitutes **~ 25 – 40 %** by volume ### Accessory Minerals - **Olivine** (often partially altered to iddingsite or serpentine) - **Opaque oxides** — Magnetite (Fe₃O₄), Ilmenite (FeTiO₃) - **Apatite** — small needle-like accessory - **Biotite** — minor in some varieties ### Secondary (Alteration) Minerals - **Chlorite, Epidote, Serpentine, Calcite** — products of deuteric / hydrothermal alteration - **Saponite, Iddingsite** — typical olivine alteration products ## 2.2 Textural Analysis — Why Dolerite Looks the Way It Does | Texture | Description | Diagnostic Significance | |---|---|---| | **Ophitic** | Augite oikocrysts (large host crystals) completely enclose numerous smaller plagioclase chadacrysts | Classic dolerite signature; indicates simultaneous crystallisation | | **Sub-ophitic** | Pyroxene partially encloses plagioclase laths | Most common in Indian Deccan Traps dolerites | | **Intergranular** | Plagioclase laths meet at angles with pyroxene/olivine filling interstices | Common when olivine is present | | **Porphyritic** | Larger phenocrysts of plagioclase or pyroxene in a finer groundmass | Found in chilled margins of dykes/sills | | **Pilotaxitic** | Trachytic texture of plagioclase microlites in a glassy base | Restricted to chilled margins (transitional to basalt) | > [!NOTE] > **Why does ophitic texture form?** Because plagioclase has a *higher melting point* than augite in basaltic magma, plagioclase laths crystallise *first*. The remaining melt, now enriched in iron and magnesium, crystallises augite *around and between* the earlier plagioclase laths — sometimes engulfing them entirely. ## 2.3 Chemical Composition (Theoretical Basis) Dolerite approximates the composition of its parent basaltic magma, but with slightly higher concentration of volatiles and water due to its sub-volcanic origin. | Oxide Component | Weight % Range | Petrogenetic Significance | |---|---|---| | SiO₂ | 45 – 52 | Basic (low silica) | | Al₂O₃ | 12 – 18 | Plagioclase contribution | | FeO + Fe₂O₃ | 9 – 15 | Mafic mineral richness | | MgO | 5 – 12 | Olivine and pyroxene control | | CaO | 8 – 12 | Plagioclase (An-rich) + clinopyroxene | | Na₂O + K₂O | 2 – 5 | Low alkalies → sub-alkaline | | TiO₂ | 0.5 – 2.5 | Ilmenite / titanomagnetite | | H₂O (LOI) | 0.5 – 3 | Deuteric alteration indicator | ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following equations and parameters are the **high-yield core** for KTU ESE questions on Dolerite.** | Parameter | Formula / Expression | Units | Engineering Application | |---|---|---|---| | **Colour Index (M)** | $M = \frac{\text{Volume \% of mafic minerals}}{\text{Total volume of rock}} \times 100$ | % | Rock classification (Mafic vs Felsic) | | **Specific Gravity (G)** | $G = \frac{\rho_{\text{rock}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}}$ | Dimensionless | Foundation design, slope stability | | **Dry Density** | $\rho_{d} = \frac{M_{s}}{V}$ | g/cm³ or kN/m³ | Load-bearing calculations | | **Porosity (n)** | $n = \frac{V_{v}}{V_{t}} \times 100$ | % | Weathering susceptibility | | **Unconfined Compressive Strength** | $q_{u} = \frac{P}{A}$ where $P$ = failure load, $A$ = cross-section area | MPa (or N/mm²) | Aggregate / dimension stone selection | | **Anorthite Content of Plagioclase** | $\text{An\%} = \frac{\text{Ca}}{\text{Ca} + \text{Na} + \text{K}} \times 100$ | % | Geothermobarometry | | **Point Load Strength Index** | $I_{s(50)} = \frac{P}{D_{e}^{2}}$ | MPa | Quick field strength estimate | | **Slake Durability Index** | $I_{d2} = \frac{M_{f}}{M_{i}} \times 100$ | % | Weathering resistance for tunnels / slopes | ## 2.5 Engineering Geological Significance (Real-World Utility) 1. **Tunnelling & Underground Excavations:** Dolerite dykes intersecting a tunnel alignment often behave as **barriers to groundwater flow** and may cause **TBM cutter wear** due to high abrasion. 2. **Dam Foundations:** Massive dolerite sills provide excellent **load-bearing foundations**; however, **highly jointed dolerite** can pose seepage problems. 3. **Highway & Railway Construction:** Dolerite is one of the **best road metals and ballast materials** in India due to its hardness, abrasion resistance, and angular fracture. 4. **Landslide Studies:** Dolerite weathers to **clay-rich soils (black cotton soil)** over the Deccan Traps, contributing to expansive soil problems. 5. **Mineral Exploration:** Dolerite dykes act as **conduits and host rocks for copper-gold (Cu-Au) mineralisation** in many parts of India. # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS, CLASSIFICATION & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Step-by-Step Classification of Dolerite (IUGS Procedure) **KTU Exam Tip:** Examiners frequently ask: *"How would you classify dolerite under the IUGS scheme?"* Follow this exact stepwise procedure. **Step 1 — Determine the Mode (Modal Mineralogy by Volume %).** - Plagioclase (P) = 55 % - Pyroxene + Olivine (M) = 40 % - Opaques + Accessory (M) = 5 % **Step 2 — Compute the Colour Index (M).** - The mafic minerals are: Pyroxene (40 %) + Opaques (5 %) = 45 % - $M = \frac{40 + 5}{100} \times 100 = 45\%$ **Step 3 — Confirm Mafic Character.** - Since $M > 35$, the rock is **Mafic**. **Step 4 — Project into the QAPF Diagram.** - With only plagioclase (no quartz or feldspathoids visible) and An content around An₆₀, the rock plots within the **Gabbro field** of the QAPF double triangle. - Because of medium grain size, the rock is specifically called **Dolerite** (textural variant of gabbro). **Step 5 — Determine Texture.** - Microscopic examination reveals augite enclosing plagioclase laths → **Ophitic texture confirmed**. **Final Classification:** > *Dolerite — a medium-grained, ophitic-textured, mafic intrusive igneous rock of gabbroic composition.* ## 3.2 Step-by-Step Engineering Strength Estimation (Worked Example) **Problem (KTU-type numerical):** A cylindrical dolerite core has diameter $D = 54$ mm and length $L = 108$ mm. The core fails in unconfined compression at a load $P = 480$ kN. Estimate the Unconfined Compressive Strength (UCS) and classify the rock per ISRM standards. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute Cross-Sectional Area.** $$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^{2}$$ $$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.054)^{2}$$ $$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 0.002916$$ $$A = 2.290 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$$ **Step 2 — Convert Load into Newtons.** $$P = 480 \text{ kN} = 480{,}000 \text{ N}$$ **Step 3 — Compute UCS.** $$q_{u} = \frac{P}{A}$$ $$q_{u} = \frac{480{,}000}{2.290 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$q_{u} = 2.096 \times 10^{8} \text{ N/m}^{2}$$ $$q_{u} \approx 209.6 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 4 — Classify Strength per ISRM.** - $q_{u} = 209.6$ MPa falls within **R5 = Very Strong (100–250 MPa)**. **Final Answer:** > *The dolerite has a UCS of approximately 209.6 MPa and is classified as a "Very Strong" rock — suitable as a high-grade construction aggregate and dimension stone.* ## 3.3 Derivation of Plagioclase Composition from Optical Data **Step 1 — Measure extinction angle (Z ∧ 010) on albite-twinned plagioclase grains.** For a measured extinction angle $X$ on the universal stage: $$\text{An\%} = c_{1} \cdot \sin^{2}(X) + c_{2}$$ Where empirical Michel-Lévy constants are $c_{1} \approx 88$ and $c_{2} \approx -4$ for high-temperature plagioclase (sanidine-anorthite series), this gives a direct estimate of An content. **Step 2 — Relate An content to magmatic conditions:** $$\ln K_{D} = \ln \left( \frac{X_{Ab}^{\text{Plag}}}{X_{An}^{\text{Plag}}} \cdot \frac{X_{An}^{\text{Liq}}}{X_{Ab}^{\text{Liq}}} \right) = -\frac{\Delta H}{R \cdot T} + \frac{\Delta S}{R}$$ Where: - $K_{D}$ = distribution coefficient (≈ 0.27 for basaltic systems) - $\Delta H, \Delta S$ = enthalpy and entropy of the Ab–An exchange reaction - $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$ - $T$ = crystallisation temperature in Kelvin **Step 3 — Solve for Temperature:** $$T = \frac{\Delta H}{R \cdot (\Delta S / R - \ln K_{D})}$$ Typical result for dolerite: **$T \approx 1100 - 1200 \text{ °C}$**, consistent with shallow crustal basaltic crystallisation. ## 3.4 Identification Matrix (Field & Laboratory) | Diagnostic Property | Observation in Dolerite | Distinguishing it from... | |---|---|---| | **Colour** | Dark grey to black | Gabbro (also dark, but coarser) | | **Grain Size** | 0.5 – 2 mm (medium) | Basalt (finer) / Gabbro (coarser) | | **Specific Gravity** | 2.9 – 3.1 | Granite (lower ~2.65) | | **Lustre** | Dull to sub-vitreous | Obsidian (vitreous) | | **Hardness** | Scratches glass easily | Limestone (softer) | | **Magnetic Response** | Often strongly magnetic (magnetite) | Most other rocks non-magnetic | | **Weathering Product** | Black cotton soil, spheroidal boulders | Granite → sandy soil | | **Field Structure** | Dyke ridges, tor topography | Sandstone → escarpments | # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Igneous Rock Texture Continuum (Cooling Rate vs Grain Size) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Basaltic Magma at 1100 to 1200 degree C] --> nodeB1{Rate of Cooling} nodeB1 -->|Rapid cooling at surface| nodeC[Volcanic Basalt
Grain less than 0.5 mm
Aphanitic Texture] nodeB1 -->|Intermediate at shallow depth| nodeD[Hypabyssal Dolerite
Grain 0.5 to 2 mm
Ophitic Texture] nodeB1 -->|Very slow at deep crust| nodeE[Plutonic Gabbro
Grain greater than 2 mm
Phaneritic Texture] nodeC -.-> nodeF[Equivalent Chemistry
Basic SiO2 45 to 52 percent] nodeD -.-> nodeF nodeE -.-> nodeF nodeD --> nodeG[Engineering Use
Road Metal, Ballast, Aggregate] nodeE --> nodeH[Engineering Use
Dimension Stone, Foundations] ``` ## 4.2 Ophitic Texture Formation Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Stage 1 Basaltic Magma Intruded at Shallow Depth] --> nodeB[Stage 2 Cooling Begins Temperature 1200 degree C] nodeB --> nodeC[Stage 3 Plagioclase Laths Crystallise First
Higher Liquidus Temperature

Surrounds Plagioclase Laths] nodeD --> nodeE[Stage 5 Augite Pyroxene Nucleates
and Grows as Oikocrysts] nodeE --> nodeF[Stage 6 Final Texture
Augite Encloses Plagioclase
OPHITIC TEXTURE] nodeF --> nodeG[Stage 7 Cooling Completes
Solid Dolerite Forms] ``` ## 4.3 Modes of Occurrence in the Field ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Hypabyssal[Dolerite Modes of Occurrence] direction TB nodeA[Concordant Intrusions] nodeB[Discordant Intrusions] nodeC[Other Intrusions] end nodeA --> nodeA1[Sill
Parallel to Bedding] nodeA --> nodeA2[Laccolith
Dome Shaped] nodeA --> nodeA3[Lopolith

Filled Pipe] ``` ## 4.4 Engineering Geological Decision Flowchart (Dolerite as Foundation / Aggregate) ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeStart([Start Dolerite Encountered at Site]) --> nodeQ1{Is the Rock
Fresh and Massive?} nodeQ1 -->|Yes| nodeP1[UCS 150 to 250 MPa
Load Bearing Excellent] nodeQ1 -->|No Heavily Jointed| nodeQ2{Joint Spacing} nodeQ2 -->|Very Close less than 10 cm| nodeP2[Treat as Rock Mass
RMR or GSI Method] nodeQ2 -->|Moderate 10 to 60 cm| nodeP3[Rock Bolting and
Shotcrete Required] nodeQ2 -->|Wide greater than 60 cm| nodeP1 nodeP1 --> nodeEnd1([Suitable for Heavy Foundation]) nodeP2 --> nodeEnd2([Detailed Rock Mass
Classification Needed]) nodeP3 --> nodeEnd3([Support System Design
Required]) nodeStart --> nodeAlt{Weathered Dolerite Present?} nodeAlt -->|Yes Black Cotton Soil| nodeWS[Expansive Soil Problem
Swell Shrink Behaviour
Use Stabilisation] nodeAlt -->|No Fresh Rock| nodeQ1 nodeWS --> nodeEnd4([Special Foundation
Design Required]) ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define Dolerite. Briefly describe its mineralogical composition.** **Model Answer:** Dolerite (also called diabase) is a **medium-grained, mafic intrusive igneous rock** of hypabyssal origin. It is the textural intermediate between basalt (fine-grained, extrusive) and gabbro (coarse-grained, plutonic). **Essential Mineralogy:** - **Plagioclase feldspar (Labradorite, An₅₀–An₇₀):** ~50–65 % - **Pyroxene (Augite):** ~25–40 % - **Accessory:** Olivine, Magnetite, Ilmenite, Apatite The characteristic texture is **ophitic or sub-ophitic**, where augite oikocrysts enclose plagioclase laths. Chemically, it contains **SiO₂ between 45–52 %**, classifying it as a basic igneous rock. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Explain the "ophitic texture" of dolerite with a neat sketch.** **Model Answer:** **Ophitic texture** is a holocrystalline, inequigranular texture in which **large pyroxene (augite) crystals — called oikocrysts — completely enclose numerous smaller, randomly oriented plagioclase feldspar laths — called chadacrysts.** **Genesis:** - Plagioclase crystallises first (higher liquidus temperature in basaltic melt) - The residual Fe-Mg-rich melt crystallises augite *around and between* the existing plagioclase laths - Slow cooling allows augite to grow into large crystals that engulf the earlier plagioclase **Diagram (Conceptual):** > A single large grey augite crystal (host) containing numerous white rectangular plagioclase laths (guests) embedded within it. This texture is **diagnostic of dolerite** and confirms its hypabyssal origin under conditions of moderate cooling. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question is divided into two sub-parts (a) and (b), typically carrying **7 marks each**. Internal choice is provided — the student answers either Option A or Option B. --- ### **OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]** **(a) Discuss in detail the petrography, mineralogy and texture of dolerite. How is it distinguished from basalt and gabbro in the field and under the microscope? (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Petrography & Mineralogy:** Dolerite is a **hypabyssal intrusive igneous rock** with basaltic composition but coarser grain size than basalt. Its modal mineralogy comprises: | Mineral | Volume % | Form | |---|---|---| | Plagioclase (Labradorite) | 50–65 | Lath-shaped (tabular) chadacrysts | | Augite (Pyroxene) | 25–40 | Equant oikocrysts | | Olivine | 0–10 | Rounded, often iddingsitised | | Opaques (Magnetite/Ilmenite) | 2–5 | Accessory grains | | Apatite, Biotite | <1 | Minor accessories | **Texture:** - **Ophitic / Sub-ophitic** — augite enclosing plagioclase (diagnostic) - **Intergranular** — when olivine is dominant - **Porphyritic** — at chilled margins **Distinction from Basalt and Gabbro:** | Property | Basalt (Extrusive) | **Dolerite (Hypabyssal)** | Gabbro (Plutonic) | |---|---|---|---| | Grain size | < 0.5 mm (aphanitic) | **0.5 – 2 mm (medium)** | > 2 mm (phaneritic, coarse) | | Cooling rate | Rapid (days–weeks) | **Moderate (months–years)** | Very slow (millions of years) | | Mode of occurrence | Lava flows | **Dykes, sills, laccoliths** | Batholiths, large intrusions | | Glass content | Often present | **Absent** | Absent | | Ophitic texture | Rare | **Characteristic** | Occasional | | Field appearance | Dense, dark, may be vesicular | **Dark, blocky jointed dykes** | Coarsely crystalline, salt-and-pepper | **Microscopic Distinction:** - In **basalt**: groundmass is glassy or microcrystalline; phenocrysts float in fine matrix. - In **dolerite**: individual crystals are clearly resolvable; ophitic texture is diagnostic. - In **gabbro**: crystals are large enough to identify by hand lens; texture is granular rather than ophitic. --- **(b) Describe the engineering properties of dolerite. Discuss its suitability as a construction material in highways and dams. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Engineering Properties:** | Property | Typical Value | |---|---| | Specific Gravity | 2.90 – 3.10 | | Dry Density | 28 – 31 kN/m³ | | Unconfined Compressive Strength (UCS) | 150 – 250 MPa | | Point Load Index | 6 – 12 MPa | | Slake Durability Index (Id₂) | 95 – 99 % | | Los Angeles Abrasion Value | 12 – 18 % | | Water Absorption | 0.3 – 0.8 % | | Modulus of Elasticity | 70 – 100 GPa | | Poisson's Ratio | 0.20 – 0.28 | **Suitability in Highway Construction:** 1. **Excellent aggregate** for bituminous and concrete road surfaces due to: - High hardness and abrasion resistance (LA < 20 %) - Angular fracture producing excellent interlocking - Low water absorption ensures durability 2. **Sub-grade / Sub-base** material: Fresh, well-jointed dolerite provides a stable platform. 3. **Embankment material**: Weathered dolerite may form clay-rich residual soil — must be checked for expansive behaviour. **Suitability in Dam Construction:** 1. **Foundation rock:** Massive, fresh dolerite sills provide **excellent load-bearing foundations** with UCS > 150 MPa. 2. **Aggregate for concrete:** Widely used for dam concrete due to high strength and chemical inertness. 3. **Concerns:** - **Jointed dolerite** can transmit seepage → grout curtains may be required. - **Weathered dolerite** produces **black cotton soil (expansive)** that can cause heave in abutments. - **Dykes** may act as groundwater conduits — hydrogeological investigation is mandatory. **Conclusion:** Dolerite is one of the **most suitable Indian rocks** for highway and dam construction, provided site-specific joint and weathering assessments are carried out. --- ### **OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]** **(a) With the help of a flowchart, explain the various modes of occurrence of dolerite in the field. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** Dolerite occurs as **hypabyssal intrusions**, classified based on their relationship with the country rock structure: **A. Concordant Intrusions (parallel to bedding/foliation):** 1. **Sill:** A sheet-like intrusion injected parallel to the bedding planes of sedimentary rocks. Sills are typically horizontal to gently dipping. 2. **Laccolith:** A dome-shaped or mushroom-shaped concordant intrusion; the overlying strata are pushed upward to form a structural dome. 3. **Lopolith:** A large saucer- or basin-shaped concordant intrusion, often with a depressed central portion. **B. Discordant Intrusions (cut across bedding/foliation):** 1. **Dyke:** A vertical to steeply dipping tabular intrusion cutting across the country rock. Dykes often form prominent ridges due to differential weathering. 2. **Cone Sheet:** A series of arcuate intrusions that dip inward toward a common focal point at depth, forming a cone-shaped pattern. 3. **Ring Dyke:** A cylindrical or annular intrusion with steep outward dip; formed by cauldron subsidence. **C. Other Forms:** 1. **Stock / Boss:** A small, irregular plutonic body of circular outcrop, generally less than 100 km² in area. 2. **Volcanic Neck / Plug:** The solidified magma occupying the conduit of an ancient volcano, exposed by erosion of the surrounding cone. **Flowchart Summary:** ``` Hypabyssal Intrusions (Dolerite) | ----------------------------------- | | CONCORDANT DISCORDANT | | ---------- -------------- | | | | | Sill Laccolith Lopolith Dyke Cone Sheet Ring Dyke ``` **Engineering Note:** Dykes and sills of dolerite are the most common modes in the **Deccan Trap region of India**, where they form prominent linear ridges and plateau cappings. --- **(b) Describe the chemical composition of dolerite. How does it relate to its parent magma and engineering behaviour? (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Chemical Composition of Dolerite:** | Oxide | Weight % | |---|---| | SiO₂ | 45 – 52 | | Al₂O₃ | 12 – 18 | | FeO + Fe₂O₃ (total Fe) | 9 – 15 | | MgO | 5 – 12 | | CaO | 8 – 12 | | Na₂O | 1.5 – 3.5 | | K₂O | 0.2 – 1.5 | | TiO₂ | 0.5 – 2.5 | | P₂O₅ | 0.05 – 0.5 | | MnO | 0.1 – 0.3 | | LOI (H₂O + CO₂) | 0.5 – 3.0 | **Relation to Parent Magma:** 1. Dolerite inherits the chemistry of its parent **basaltic (tholeiitic) magma** that originates from partial melting of upper mantle peridotite. 2. The magma rises through crustal fractures, undergoing minimal differentiation due to rapid emplacement — therefore dolerite **compositionally mirrors basalt**. 3. Slight enrichment in **Fe, Ti, and volatiles** may occur due to crystal fractionation en route. 4. Total alkali content (Na₂O + K₂O) < 5 % confirms **sub-alkaline (tholeiitic) affinity**. **Relation to Engineering Behaviour:** | Chemical Feature | Engineering Implication | |---|---| | **High FeO + MgO** | Increases density and strength → excellent aggregate | | **High CaO** | Stable plagioclase → resistant to chemical weathering | | **Low SiO₂ (< 52 %)** | Limited quartz → no alkali-silica reaction (ASR) in concrete | | **High Fe content** | Magnetite presence → magnetic signature; possible corrosion of buried metal | | **Volatile content (H₂O)** | Promotes deuteric alteration → chlorite/epidote formation reduces long-term durability | | **Low Al₂O₃** | No expansive clay mineral formation in fresh state | **Conclusion:** The basic, iron-rich, low-silica chemistry of dolerite makes it **highly suitable as construction aggregate**, with predictable and durable engineering performance. However, **chemical alteration during weathering** can compromise its long-term stability, especially in tropical climates like Kerala. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that Lead to Mark Deductions in KTU Valuation:** > > 1. **Confusing Dolerite with Gabbro or Basalt:** Students often use the terms interchangeably. Remember — the *texture* (grain size + ophitic fabric) defines dolerite; the *mineralogy* is essentially gabbroic. > 2. **Wrong spelling:** "Dolerite" is the British spelling (preferred by KTU); "Diabase" is American. **Avoid "Doleratite" or "Dolirite" — automatic spelling check deductions apply.** > 3. **Forgetting engineering relevance:** Pure petrographic descriptions without linking to **strength, durability, or construction use** lose marks in Module 2. > 4. **Skipping the cooling-rate analogy:** Examiners reward students who explicitly state "dolerite = intermediate cooling between basalt and gabbro." > 5. **No diagram in long answers:** A 14-mark question without a labelled sketch / flowchart loses **at least 2 marks** as per KTU valuation key. > 6. **Numerical errors in UCS calculation:** Always convert units correctly (mm² → m²; kN → N) before computing UCS. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST - **Dolerite** = medium-grained, mafic, intrusive (hypabyssal) igneous rock. - **Composition:** Plagioclase (Labradorite) + Augite ± Olivine + Magnetite. - **SiO₂ content:** 45–52 % → Basic rock. - **Colour Index M > 35** → Mafic classification confirmed. - **Diagnostic texture:** Ophitic / Sub-ophitic. - **Cooling position:** Intermediate between basalt and gabbro. - **Modes of occurrence:** Dykes, sills, laccoliths, lopoliths, cone sheets, ring dykes, plugs. - **UCS:** 150 – 250 MPa → "Very Strong" rock (ISRM R5). - **Density:** 2.90 – 3.10 g/cm³. - **Best engineering use:** Road metal, railway ballast, aggregate, dimension stone. - **Caution:** Weathered dolerite → black cotton soil → expansive soil problems. - **Indian occurrence:** Deccan Traps (Maharashtra, Karnataka, MP, Gujarat). - **Distinguish from basalt:** Dolerite has visible crystals (0.5–2 mm) and no glass. - **Distinguish from gabbro:** Dolerite has ophitic texture and medium grain size. - **Key formula to remember:** $q_{u} = \frac{P}{A}$ and $M = \frac{V_{\text{mafic}}}{V_{\text{total}}} \times 100$. - **Always cite applications** — highways, dams, tunnels, foundations — in KTU long answers.

topics.title: Basalt
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET416 - ENGINEERING GEOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Basalt

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Basalt** is a fine-grained, dark-colored, **extrusive (volcanic) igneous rock** formed by the rapid cooling and solidification of low-viscosity lava at or near the Earth's surface. It is the most abundant volcanic rock on Earth and constitutes the bulk of the oceanic crust, vast continental flood basalt provinces (e.g., the **Deccan Traps** of India), and volcanic islands.

In KTU 2024 Scheme Engineering Geology terminology, basalt belongs to:
- **Rock Family:** Igneous Rocks
- **Genesis:** Extrusive / Volcanic
- **Compositional Class:** **Mafic** (Basic)
- **Silica Content (SiO₂):** **45 – 52 %** (by weight)
- **Color Index (M):** Greater than **50 %**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Basalt is grouped under "Igneous Rocks" in Module 2. Students must be able to (a) identify basalt megascopically, (b) state its mineralogy, (c) describe its texture, structure, and engineering properties, and (d) explain its **weathering to laterite** (Kerala-specific significance).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Think of basalt as the "instant version" of granite. When molten rock (magma) cools **slowly deep inside the Earth**, it forms granite with large visible crystals. But when the same kind of molten rock erupts onto the surface as a **runny lava flow** and cools within hours or days, the atoms don't have time to grow big crystals — so they form a dense, fine-grained rock: **basalt**.

> **Real-world Analogy:** Imagine pouring molten chocolate onto a cold metal tray. The outside (in contact with air) hardens into a smooth, fine-textured shell almost instantly — this is like basalt. If you had a giant pot of chocolate cooling for days in a warm room, you'd get coarse crystals forming — that's like granite. Both start from the same "melt," but the **rate of cooling** decides the final texture.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Property | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Specific Gravity (G) | **2.90 – 3.10** |
| Bulk Density (ρ) | **2900 – 3000 kg/m³** |
| Uniaxial Compressive Strength (UCS) | **150 – 250 MPa** (fresh basalt) |
| Mohs Hardness | **6 – 7** |
| Water Absorption | **< 0.5 %** (fresh, dense) |
| Porosity | **0.1 – 1 %** (fresh) |
| Young's Modulus (E) | **60 – 100 GPa** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition – "Mafic":** Derived from **MA**gnesium + **F**errum (iron). Mafic rocks are rich in dark, heavy minerals like pyroxene, olivine, and Ca-rich plagioclase. The opposite of mafic is **felsic** (rich in feldspar and silica, light-colored).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geological Genesis of Basalt

The formation pathway can be summarized in five logical steps:

1. **Magma Generation:** Partial melting of upper mantle peridotite (at depths of 30–100 km) produces basaltic magma of low viscosity (10² – 10⁴ Pa·s).
2. **Ascent:** Buoyancy drives the magma upward through fractures and conduits.
3. **Eruption / Extrusion:** Lava reaches the surface via central volcanoes or fissure eruptions (e.g., Deccan Traps fissure eruption ~66 Ma ago).
4. **Rapid Cooling:** Lava at 1000 – 1200 °C loses heat rapidly to atmosphere, water, or wet ground. Cooling rates of 100 – 1000 °C/hour are typical for thin flows.
5. **Solidification:** Atoms arrange into small crystals (grains < 1 mm, often microscopic) — producing the characteristic **aphanitic texture**.

## 2.2 Mineralogical Composition

Basalt is composed primarily of two mineral groups:

**Essential (Major) Minerals:**
- **Plagioclase Feldspar** (Labradorite – Bytownite; An₅₀–An₇₀): 45 – 55 %
- **Pyroxene** (Augite ± Pigeonite): 25 – 40 %
- **Olivine** (Forsteritic): 0 – 15 %

**Accessory Minerals:**
- **Magnetite** & **Ilmenite** (oxide minerals): 2 – 5 %
- **Apatite**, **Sphene** (titanite), **Zircon**

**Secondary / Alteration Products:**
- Chlorite, Serpentine, Iddingsite, Calcite, Clay minerals

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plagioclase composition rule:** In mafic rocks, plagioclase is **Ca-rich** (Anorthite content > 50). In felsic rocks (granite), plagioclase is **Na-rich** (Albite-rich). This is a key KTU-favorite identification clue.

## 2.3 Chemical Composition

A typical basalt has the following major oxide composition (in weight %):

| Oxide | Weight % | Role |
|---|---|---|
| SiO₂ | 45 – 52 | Acidic / Network former |
| Al₂O₃ | 12 – 18 | Feldspar former |
| FeO + Fe₂O₃ | 9 – 14 | Mafic indicator |
| MgO | 5 – 12 | Mafic indicator |
| CaO | 8 – 12 | Plagioclase former |
| Na₂O + K₂O | 2 – 5 | Alkali content |
| TiO₂ | 0.5 – 2.5 | Titanomagnetite source |
| P₂O₅ | 0.05 – 0.5 | Apatite source |

> **Total Alkali–Silica (TAS) Classification:** A plot of (Na₂O + K₂O) vs. SiO₂ places basalt in the **"Basalt"** field (low alkali, low silica).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Use / Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Color Index (CI)** | $CI = \dfrac{M}{M + F} \times 100$ | M = Mafic minerals, F = Felsic minerals. CI > 50 ⇒ Mafic (basalt). |
| **Silica Saturation** | $SiO_2\% = 45 - 52$ | Basic / Intermediate boundary. |
| **Specific Gravity** | $G = \dfrac{\rho_{rock}}{\rho_{water}}$ | Determines density, foundation load calculations. |
| **Density** | $\rho = \dfrac{M}{V}$ | Used in overburden pressure $\sigma_v = \rho g h$ calculations. |
| **Uniaxial Compressive Strength (UCS)** | $\sigma_c = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | Direct input for bearing capacity of foundations on rock. |
| **RQD (Rock Quality Designation)** | $RQD = \dfrac{\sum L_i \;(\ge 10\,cm)}{L_{total}} \times 100$ | Basalt with closely spaced joints → low RQD → poor tunnel support. |
| **Weathering Index** | $WI = \dfrac{\text{Unweathered UCS}}{\text{Weathered UCS}}$ | Basalt degrades to laterite; WI may exceed 10. |
| **Effective Porosity** | $n = \dfrac{V_{voids}}{V_{total}} \times 100$ | Vesicular basalt has high n, lower UCS. |
| **Slake Durability Index** | $Id_2 = \dfrac{M_{dry,\,retained}}{M_{initial}} \times 100$ | Low for highly weathered basalt (laterite). |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU rule on tables:** In your answer booklet, never use the bare pipe symbol \vert for absolute value inside any formula table — this breaks markdown rendering. Always use `\vert` (e.g., $G = \vert \rho_{rock}/\rho_{water} \vert$).

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

Basalt has direct applications in:
- **Aggregate for concrete and road metal** (high abrasion resistance, Los Angeles Abrasion value typically 12–18 %).
- **Railway ballast** (angular, tough, durable).
- **Dimension stone / building stone** (columnar basalt "Kota stone" type).
- **Foundations for heavy structures** (dams, bridges) — high bearing capacity.
- **Tunneling** through Deccan Trap formations.
- **Source rock for laterite** — a soil type that dominates Kerala's residual terrain.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Color Index (CI) of a Basalt Sample

A thin section of basalt is examined under a petrographic microscope. **1000 mineral grains** are counted (point-counting method). The result is:

- Plagioclase feldspar: 510 grains
- Augite (pyroxene): 320 grains
- Olivine: 90 grains
- Magnetite + Ilmenite: 60 grains
- Glass / alteration: 20 grains

**Step 1 — Classify minerals into mafic and felsic groups.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Felsic (F):} &\quad \text{Plagioclase} = 510 \text{ grains} \\
\text{Mafic (M):} &\quad \text{Augite} + \text{Olivine} + \text{Oxides} \\
&\quad = 320 + 90 + 60 = 470 \text{ grains}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Color Index formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CI &= \frac{M}{M + F} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{470}{470 + 510} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{470}{980} \times 100 \\
&= 47.96 \;\% \approx 48 \;\% \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation.**

Since CI < 50 %, this rock is **mesocratic** (transitional between mafic and intermediate). It is closer to a **basaltic andesite** than a true basalt. A *true basalt* would require CI ≥ 50 %.

> **Engineering Insight:** A higher color index means more dark minerals (olivine, pyroxene). These minerals have **higher density and higher weathering susceptibility** (olivine weathers fastest). Hence, a basalt with CI = 70 will weather to laterite more aggressively than one with CI = 50.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Effective Porosity of Vesicular Basalt

A cylindrical core of vesicular basalt (diameter 50 mm, length 100 mm) is oven-dried and weighed (**M_dry = 482 g**). It is then vacuum-saturated with water and weighed (**M_sat = 498 g**). Suspended in water, it weighs **M_sub = 311 g**.

**Step 1 — Compute the bulk volume using Archimedes' principle.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{bulk} &= \frac{M_{sat} - M_{sub}}{\rho_{water}} \\
&= \frac{498 - 311}{1.0} \text{ cm}^3 \\
&= 187 \text{ cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the volume of voids (water absorbed).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{voids} &= \frac{M_{sat} - M_{dry}}{\rho_{water}} \\
&= \frac{498 - 482}{1.0} \text{ cm}^3 \\
&= 16 \text{ cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the effective porosity.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= \frac{V_{voids}}{V_{bulk}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{16}{187} \times 100 \\
&= 8.56 \;\% \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation.**

A porosity of 8.56 % is **high for a basalt** (typical fresh basalt < 1 %). This indicates the rock is **vesicular** — formed by trapped gas bubbles in the lava. Engineering consequences:
- **Reduced UCS** (rule of thumb: every 1 % increase in porosity reduces UCS by 5–10 %).
- **Higher water absorption** → vulnerable to freeze-thaw and chemical weathering.
- **Lower durability** as construction aggregate.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: RQD of a Basalt Outcrop in a Tunnel Section

In a 5 m scanline along an exposed basalt face in a tunnel, joint block lengths are measured:

- 8 cm, 12 cm, 25 cm, 41 cm, 9 cm, 17 cm, 33 cm, 58 cm, 14 cm, 22 cm, 11 cm, 47 cm, 19 cm, 28 cm, 6 cm, 39 cm, 13 cm, 21 cm, 26 cm, 15 cm

**Step 1 — Sum all block lengths.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{total} &= 8+12+25+41+9+17+33+58+14+22 \\
&\quad +11+47+19+28+6+39+13+21+26+15 \\
&= 464 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum only those lengths ≥ 10 cm.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum L_i (\ge 10\,cm) &= 12+25+41+17+33+58+14+22+11+47 \\
&\quad +19+28+39+13+21+26+15 \\
&= 441 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Excluded: 8, 9, 6 → total 23 cm of "discarded" small pieces.)

**Step 3 — Calculate RQD.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RQD &= \frac{441}{464} \times 100 \\
&= 95.04 \;\% \approx 95 \;\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Rock mass quality classification (Deere, 1964).**

| RQD (%) | Rock Quality |
|---|---|
| < 25 | Very Poor |
| 25 – 50 | Poor |
| 50 – 75 | Fair |
| 75 – 90 | Good |
| **90 – 100** | **Excellent** |

Our basalt yields **RQD = 95 %** → **Excellent rock mass quality.** Tunnel support design can adopt minimal support (e.g., spot bolting + shotcrete only).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** In Kerala, however, *Deccan Trap* basalt is generally **massive with widely spaced joints** (columnar jointing may locally reduce RQD). The RQD of columnar-jointed basalt can drop to 30–60 %, demanding systematic bolting patterns.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Basalt vs. Granite

| Property | Basalt | Granite |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Extrusive (lava) | Intrusive (pluton) |
| Cooling rate | Fast (hours–days) | Slow (thousands of years) |
| Texture | Aphanitic (fine) | Phaneritic (coarse) |
| Grain size | < 1 mm | 1 – 10 mm |
| Silica (SiO₂) % | 45 – 52 | 68 – 75 |
| Color | Dark grey to black | Light grey / pink |
| Color Index | > 50 | < 20 |
| Density (kg/m³) | 2900 – 3000 | 2650 – 2750 |
| UCS (MPa) | 150 – 250 | 100 – 200 |
| Weathering product | **Laterite** (in tropics) | Kaolinitic clay / sand |
| Kerala relevance | Deccan Traps (north India) | **Wayanad, Kerala** (Charnockite) |

> **Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** "**B**asalt = **B**lack, **B**asic, **B**orn from lava." "**G**ranite = **G**rey/Pink, **G**ranular, **G**rown deep."

```mermaid
flowchart TB

        A1B --- A1C((Hexagon 3))

        A1D --- A1E((Hexagon 5))
        A1E --- A1A
        A1B --- A1F((Hexagon 6))
    end
    subgraph A2[Side view Cross section]
        A2X[Top crust Cool first] --> A2Y[Vertical polygonal columns]
        A2Y --> A2Z[Bottom hot interior]
    end
    A1 --> A2
```

> **Formation logic:** As a thick lava flow cools, it contracts. Contraction is accommodated by **tensile fractures** that propagate perpendicular to the cooling surface. The fractures meet at ~120° angles to minimize energy, producing **polygonal (commonly hexagonal) columns** perpendicular to the cooling surface. Famous example: **Giant's Causeway (Northern Ireland)** and **St. Mary's Islands (Karnataka, India)** near Kerala's border.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define basalt. List any four of its characteristic mineral constituents.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Basalt is a fine-grained, dark-coloured, extrusive igneous rock of mafic composition, formed by the rapid cooling of low-viscosity lava at or near the Earth's surface. It contains 45 – 52 % SiO₂ and a color index greater than 50 %.

**Four characteristic minerals:**
1. **Plagioclase feldspar** (Labradorite–Bytownite, An₅₀–An₇₀) — 45 – 55 %.
2. **Augite** (clinopyroxene) — 25 – 40 %.
3. **Olivine** (forsteritic, Fo₇₀–Fo₈₅) — 0 – 15 %.
4. **Magnetite / Ilmenite** (oxide minerals) — 2 – 5 %.

---

### **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between vesicular basalt and amygdaloidal basalt.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Vesicular Basalt | Amygdaloidal Basalt |
|---|---|---|
| Vesicle state | Hollow (gas cavities unfilled) | Filled by secondary minerals |
| Formation time | Immediately after eruption (gas trapped during cooling) | Later, after groundwater percolates |
| Typical filling | Empty / air-filled | Zeolites, calcite, agate, chlorite, quartz |
| Density | Lower (voids present) | Higher (voids sealed) |
| Engineering use | Lightweight aggregate, decorative stone | Dimension stone, building material |
| Porosity | High (up to 30 %) | Low to moderate |

> **Viva shortcut:** "Amygdaloidal = *Amygdala* = almond-shaped (because the fillings look like almonds embedded in rock)."
