# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**Define a fold. List any four geometric elements of a fold.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** A fold is a wave-like undulation in rock strata produced by ductile deformation under tectonic stress. It represents the plastic bending of originally flat-lying sedimentary, metamorphic, or igneous layers.

> **Four Geometric Elements (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
> 1. **Hinge Line** — Line joining points of maximum curvature
> 2. **Limbs** — The straight flanks on either side of the hinge
> 3. **Axial Plane** — Imaginary plane bisecting the fold through successive hinges
> 4. **Plunge** — Angle the hinge line makes with the horizontal

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]

**Distinguish between an anticline and a syncline with respect to (a) core rocks, (b) limb dip direction, and (c) age sequence.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Anticline (1.5 marks) | Syncline (1.5 marks) |
|---------|----------------------|----------------------|
| **(a) Core rocks** | Oldest rocks occupy the core | Youngest rocks occupy the core |
| **(b) Limb dip direction** | Limbs dip **away** from the hinge | Limbs dip **towards** the hinge |
| **(c) Age sequence** | Rocks get **younger** moving from core outward | Rocks get **older** moving from core outward |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Following the **KTU 2024 ESE Module Pattern**, Part B carries 14 marks and provides **internal choice**. You must attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified]

**(a)** With neat sketches, classify folds based on the **attitude of the axial plane**. Discuss the engineering significance of each type with respect to **tunnel alignment** and **dam foundation**.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> **Introduction (1 mark):** Folds are classified based on axial plane attitude into the following types:

> **Classification with Sketches (4 marks — 0.5 mark per type):**
>
> 1. **Symmetrical (Upright) Fold:** Axial plane is vertical; both limbs dip at equal angles in opposite directions.
> 2. **Asymmetrical Fold:** Axial plane is inclined; limbs dip at unequal angles in opposite directions.
> 3. **Overturned Fold:** Axial plane is steeply inclined; both limbs dip in the **same direction**; one limb is inverted.
> 4. **Recumbent Fold:** Axial plane is horizontal or near-horizontal (dip < 10°); indicates extreme compression.
> 5. **Isoclinal Fold:** Both limbs are parallel and dip in the same direction at equal angles.
> 6. **Chevron Fold:** Sharp angular hinge with long straight limbs.
> 7. **Fan Fold:** Axial plane fans out with reversed dips.
> 8. **Box Fold:** Rectangular profile with flat crest and trough.

> **Engineering Significance (2 marks):**
>
> | Fold Type | Tunnel Alignment | Dam Foundation |
> |-----------|------------------|----------------|
> | Symmetrical | Manageable; moderate stress | Acceptable with care |
> | Asymmetrical | Variable stress; need reinforcement | Moderate stability |
> | Overturned | High stress at inverted limb; risk of squeezing | **Avoid**; unstable |
> | Recumbent | **Avoid**; highly deformed rocks; high squeezing risk | **Unsuitable**; complex geology |
> | Isoclinal | High stress concentration | Variable bearing |
> | Chevron | Brittle failure at hinges; rock burst risk | Differential settlement |
> | Fan/Box | Complex stress pattern | Foundation heterogeneity |

---

**(b)** A fold limb has a **true dip of 50° towards S30°E**. Calculate the apparent dip observed in a vertical cross-section trending **(i) N-S** and **(ii) E-W**. Comment on the engineering significance of the apparent dip in road cut design.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> **Given Data (0.5 mark):**
> - True dip $\alpha = 50°$
> - True dip direction = S30°E (= 150° azimuth from N)
> - Required: Apparent dip in N-S section and E-W section

> **Step 1: Calculate Angle β for N-S Section (1 mark)**
> The N-S section trend = 0° (or 180°). True dip direction = 150°.
> $$\beta_{NS} = \vert 150° - 180° \vert = 30°$$

> **Step 2: Apparent Dip in N-S Section (1 mark)**
> $$\tan \alpha'_{NS} = \tan(50°) \times \cos(30°)$$
> $$\tan \alpha'_{NS} = 1.1918 \times 0.8660$$
> $$\tan \alpha'_{NS} = 1.0320$$
> $$\alpha'_{NS} = \tan^{-1}(1.0320) = 45.91°$$

> **Step 3: Calculate Angle β for E-W Section (0.5 mark)**
> E-W section trend = 90° (or 270°). True dip direction = 150°.
> $$\beta_{EW} = \vert 150° - 90° \vert = 60°$$

> **Step 4: Apparent Dip in E-W Section (1 mark)**
> $$\tan \alpha'_{EW} = \tan(50°) \times \cos(60°)$$
> $$\tan \alpha'_{EW} = 1.1918 \times 0.5000$$
> $$\tan \alpha'_{EW} = 0.5959$$
> $$\alpha'_{EW} = \tan^{-1}(0.5959) = 30.78°$$

> **Step 5: Engineering Significance in Road Cut Design (2 marks)**
>
> - The **true dip (50°)** is the **steepest possible** inclination; the **apparent dip in any other direction is always less than or equal to the true dip**.
> - For a road cut aligned **N-S**, the slope will appear at **45.91°** — closer to the true dip; this is **near-maximum instability**.
> - For a road cut aligned **E-W**, the slope will appear at **30.78°** — much gentler; this is the **more stable direction**.
> - **Recommendation:** Align the road cut **E-W** to reduce slope angle, minimize rockfall, and improve stability.
> - The difference in apparent dip in two perpendicular sections is the basis for **3-point problem** in structural geology.

> **Final Answer (0.5 mark):**
> - Apparent dip in N-S section = **45.91°**
> - Apparent dip in E-W section = **30.78°**
> - Road cut should be aligned **E-W** for better stability.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified]

**(a)** Discuss the **engineering significance of folds** with respect to:
   (i) Tunnel alignment
   (ii) Dam foundation
   (iii) Reservoir and groundwater
   (iv) Highway and railway cuttings

**(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> **(i) Tunnel Alignment (2 marks):**
> - **Anticlinal hinges** are zones of **maximum tensile stress** and **fracturing** → **avoid** tunnel alignment along the hinge.
> - **Synclinal troughs** experience **compressive stress**; tunnels are stable but prone to **water seepage and inflow**.
> - In **plunging folds**, tunnel alignment should be **perpendicular to the plunge direction** to minimize length through deformed rock.
> - **Recumbent and isoclinal folds** with associated **cleavage and schistosity** cause **squeezing ground conditions** and **rock burst hazards**.
> - In **nappe and thrust-fold belts**, tunnels may encounter **rock mixing (mélange)** → unpredictable ground behavior.

> **(ii) Dam Foundation (2 marks):**
> - **Synclinal valleys** are **ideal dam sites** (e.g., Bhakra Nangal, Idukki) because:
>   - Rocks dip **into the abutments** → no sliding tendency.
>   - **V-shaped cross-section** provides natural grip and resistance to horizontal thrust.
> - **Anticlinal ridges** are **unsuitable** for dams because:
>   - **Tension fractures** at the hinge cause **seepage and leakage**.
>   - Rocks dip **away from abutments** → sliding planes parallel to bedding.
> - **Overturned and recumbent folds** → highly variable bearing capacity, require **detailed subsurface investigation**.

> **(iii) Reservoir and Groundwater (1.5 marks):**
> - **Anticlinal crests** form **good aquifer recharge zones**; **synclinal troughs** form **good groundwater storage zones**.
> - In **reservoirs**, folded strata may cause **anisotropic seepage**; fractured hinges lead to **leakage from reservoirs** (e.g., Idukki reservoir, Kerala).
> - **Plunging synclines** form **artesian basins** for groundwater exploitation.

> **(iv) Highway and Railway Cuttings (1.5 marks):**
> - In **plunging folds**, road cuts must account for the **changing dip direction** along the alignment.
> - **Overturned limbs** cause **slope failure** along the bedding plane → require **rock bolting, benching, and drainage**.
> - **Fold hinges in cuttings** lead to **differential weathering** and **unstable slopes**.
> - In **recumbent fold terrain**, **sliding on foliation planes** is a major hazard.

---

**(b)** Two vertical cross-sections of a fold limb in a tunnel excavation give apparent dips of **25° in a section bearing N30°E–S30°W** and **18° in a section bearing N70°E–S70°W**. Calculate the **true dip** of the fold limb and the **azimuth of the true dip direction**.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> **Given Data (0.5 mark):**
> - Apparent dip $\alpha_1 = 25°$ in section bearing N30°E (azimuth = 30°)
> - Apparent dip $\alpha_2 = 18°$ in section bearing N70°E (azimuth = 70°)
> - Angle between the two sections: $\Delta\theta = 70° - 30° = 40°$

> **Step 1: General Formula for True Dip (1 mark)**
> Using the apparent dip formula:
> $$\tan \alpha_1 = \tan \alpha \cdot \cos \beta_1$$
> $$\tan \alpha_2 = \tan \alpha \cdot \cos \beta_2$$
> where $\beta_1$ and $\beta_2$ are the angles between the true dip direction and the two section directions.

> **Step 2: Set Up Equations (1 mark)**
> Let $\theta$ be the azimuth of the true dip direction. Then:
> $$\beta_1 = \theta - 30°, \quad \beta_2 = \theta - 70°$$
> $$\tan 25° = \tan \alpha \cdot \cos(\theta - 30°) \quad \text{...(i)}$$
> $$\tan 18° = \tan \alpha \cdot \cos(\theta - 70°) \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

> **Step 3: Divide Equation (i) by (ii) (1 mark)**
> $$\dfrac{\tan 25°}{\tan 18°} = \dfrac{\cos(\theta - 30°)}{\cos(\theta - 70°)}$$
> $$\dfrac{0.4663}{0.3249} = 1.4352 = \dfrac{\cos(\theta - 30°)}{\cos(\theta - 70°)}$$

> **Step 4: Solve Numerically (2 marks)**
> Let $u = \theta - 50°$ (midpoint), then $\theta - 30° = u + 20°$ and $\theta - 70° = u - 20°$.
> $$1.4352 = \dfrac{\cos(u + 20°)}{\cos(u - 20°)}$$
> $$1.4352 \cdot \cos(u - 20°) = \cos(u + 20°)$$
>
> Using the cosine addition formulas and solving iteratively (or graphically):
> $$u \approx 48.3°$$
> Therefore, $\theta = 50° + 48.3° = 98.3° \approx 98°$
> i.e., true dip direction is **S 8.3°E** (since azimuth 98° = E 8°S).

> **Step 5: Compute True Dip (1 mark)**
> $$\tan \alpha = \dfrac{\tan 25°}{\cos(98° - 30°)} = \dfrac{0.4663}{\cos(68°)} = \dfrac{0.4663}{0.3746} = 1.2448$$
> $$\alpha = \tan^{-1}(1.2448) = 51.20°$$

> **Final Answer (0.5 mark):**
> - **True dip = 51.2°**
> - **True dip direction = S 8°E (Azimuth 98°)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (Read Carefully):**
>
> 1. **Confusing Anticline with Antiform (–2 marks typical penalty):** Always specify whether the classification is **age-based (anticline/syncline)** or **geometry-based (antiform/synform)**. In overturned folds, the geometry may be anticlinal but the age sequence may be synclinal.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Units in Numerical Problems (–1 mark):** Always write units (degrees, meters) in the final answer. Dip angle is dimensionless but conventionally reported in degrees.
>
> 3. **Wrong Apparent Dip Direction (–2 marks):** Apparent dip is **always less than or equal to the true dip**. If you get a value greater than the true dip, you have made an arithmetic error.
>
> 4. **Omitting the Plunge Direction in Plunging Folds (–1 mark):** When describing plunging folds, ALWAYS mention the **direction** of plunge (e.g., "plunging 30° towards N45°E"), not just the plunge angle.
>
> 5. **Confusing Symmetrical with Isoclinal (–1 mark):** Symmetrical folds have a **vertical axial plane** with limbs dipping in **opposite directions** at **equal angles**. Isoclinal folds have **parallel limbs** dipping in the **same direction** at equal angles.
>
> 6. **Skipping the Sketch in Classification Questions (–2 marks):** KTU examiners **deduct 2 marks** if you classify folds without neat, labeled sketches. Always draw the diagrams.
>
> 7. **Mixing Up the Apparent Dip Formula:** The correct formula is $\tan \alpha' = \tan \alpha \cdot \cos \beta$, NOT $\tan \alpha' = \cos \alpha \cdot \tan \beta$. The cosine is multiplied with **tangent of true dip**, not the other way around.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam:**

### **A. Definition Block**
- ☐ Fold = wave-like bend in strata due to ductile deformation
- ☐ Caused by compressional, tensional, or shear stress
- ☐ Rocks behave plastically due to high P–T and slow strain rate
- ☐ Examples: Himalayas, Alps, Aravallis

### **B. Geometric Elements Block**
- ☐ Hinge line, Limbs, Axial plane, Plunge, Dip, Strike, Interlimb angle, Wavelength, Amplitude

### **C. Classification Block**
- ☐ **By Axial Plane:** Symmetrical, Asymmetrical, Overturned, Recumbent, Isoclinal, Chevron, Fan, Box
- ☐ **By Plunge:** Non-plunging (0°), Plunging (10°–80°), Reclined (60°–90° in axial plane), Vertical (90°)
- ☐ **By Shape:** Anticline (oldest core), Syncline (youngest core), Monocline, Antiform, Synform
- ☐ **By Tightness:** Gentle (180°–120°), Open (120°–70°), Close (70°–30°), Tight (30°–0°), Isoclinal (0°)

### **D. Formula Block**
- ☐ $\tan \alpha' = \tan \alpha \cdot \cos \beta$ (Apparent Dip)
- ☐ $\tan \alpha = \sqrt{\tan^2 \alpha_1 + \tan^2 \alpha_2}$ (True dip from 2 perpendicular apparent dips)
- ☐ $W = t / \sin \alpha$ (Outcrop width on horizontal ground)
- ☐ $t_v = t / \cos \alpha$ (Vertical thickness)
- ☐ $t_h = t \cdot \tan \alpha$ (Horizontal thickness)

### **E. Engineering Significance Block**
- ☐ **Tunnels:** AVOID anticlinal hinges (tensile stress, fractures); use synclinal troughs with dewatering
- ☐ **Dams:** PREFER synclinal valleys (compression, V-shape); AVOID anticlinal ridges (tension, leakage)
- ☐ **Reservoirs:** Anticlinal crests = recharge zones; synclinal troughs = storage zones
- ☐ **Roads/Railways:** Plunging folds cause variable dips; overturned limbs = sliding planes
- ☐ **Foundations:** Recumbent folds = unpredictable bearing; require pile foundations

### **F. Quick Memory Tricks**
- ☐ **"Anticline = Away"** (limbs dip away from hinge)
- ☐ **"Syncline = Same/Sink"** (limbs dip into the hinge, like a sink)
- ☐ **"Old is Gold in Anticline"** (oldest rocks in core)
- ☐ **"Young in Syncline"** (youngest rocks in core)
- ☐ **"Apparent < True"** (apparent dip is always less than or equal to true dip)
- ☐ **"Synclines for Dams, Anticlines for Trouble"**

---

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Brief Classification and Engineering Significance of Folds**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech PECET416 - Engineering Geology Syllabus*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Faults and Joints. Engineering Geology - Significance of Geological Investigations for Civil Engineering Projects. Geological Part of Site Investigation for the Construction of Dams and Reservoirs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET416 - ENGINEERING GEOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Faults and Joints. Engineering Geology - Significance of Geological Investigations for Civil Engineering Projects. Geological Part of Site Investigation for the Construction of Dams and Reservoirs # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Faults – Formal Definition A **fault** is a planar fracture or discontinuity in a rock mass along which there has been **significant relative displacement** of the two blocks (called the hanging wall and footwall) on either side of the fracture surface. The displacement is governed by tectonic stress regimes (compressional, tensional, or shear) acting over geological time. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):** A fault is a fracture surface in the Earth's crust along which the rock masses on either side have moved relative to each other, parallel to the fracture surface. ### Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition) Imagine a **deck of cards lying on a table** that you push sideways with your palm. The cards do not all slide as one — instead, they form a stepped pattern where individual cards shift relative to their neighbours along *planes of slip*. A fault is essentially this: when huge tectonic forces squeeze, pull, or shear the Earth's crust, the rock cannot bend plastically everywhere, so it *breaks* and *slides* along discrete surfaces. > [!NOTE] > A fracture with **no appreciable displacement** is called a **joint**, not a fault. Movement is the defining discriminator — this is a frequent KTU exam pitfall. --- ## 1.2 Joints – Formal Definition A **joint** is a fracture or crack in a rock mass across which there has been **no visible relative movement** parallel to the fracture surface. Joints are surfaces of mechanical weakness produced by tensile stresses, cooling, unloading (pressure release), or tectonic activity. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition:** A joint is a plane of structural weakness along which no relative displacement of the rock has taken place. Joints occur in sets with characteristic orientations and spacings. ### Conceptual Analogy Think of **dried mud at the bottom of a dried-up pond**. The mud cracks into polygonal blocks — but the blocks have not moved relative to each other. The cracks (joints) formed because the mud shrunk. Similarly, igneous rocks develop columnar joints as they cool; sedimentary rocks develop joints as they are uplifted and unloaded. --- ## 1.3 Engineering Geology – Significance **Engineering Geology** is the applied science of applying geological knowledge to civil engineering practice. It is the bridge between earth sciences and construction engineering. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition:** Engineering geology is the application of geological data, techniques, and principles to the study of rock and soil, surface and subsurface materials and structures, for the safe and economical design, construction, and maintenance of civil engineering works. ### Significance for Civil Engineering Projects - **Tunnels:** Joint and fault orientations determine rock burst risk, overbreak, and support design. - **Dams & Reservoirs:** Foundation stability, leakage, groutability, and abutment integrity depend on geological structure. - **Bridges & Highways:** Bedrock level, weathering profile, and slope stability are critical. - **Buildings:** Bearing capacity and settlement are governed by subsurface geology. > [!NOTE] > The motto of engineering geology as taught at KTU: *"Predict the geology, design for the geology, monitor the geology."* --- ## 1.4 Geological Part of Site Investigation for Dams and Reservoirs A **dam** is a permanent hydraulic structure that impounds water, transferring the load to the foundation rock. Geological investigation ensures: 1. The **foundation rock** has sufficient bearing capacity. 2. The **reservoir basin** is watertight (no leakage through karst, permeable joints, faults). 3. The **abutments** are stable. 4. The **downstream** area is safe from slope failure. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Highlight:** Site investigation for a dam proceeds in **stages**: Reconnaissance → Detailed surface mapping → Subsurface exploration (drill holes, drifts, adits) → In-situ testing → Grouting trials → Continuous monitoring during construction. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of a Fault — Structural Elements A fault is described by **eight fundamental geometric elements**: 1. **Fault plane / Fault surface:** The fracture surface along which movement occurs. 2. **Strike (α):** The compass direction of the horizontal line on the fault plane (N–S, E–W, NE–SW, etc.). 3. **Dip (β):** The angle the fault plane makes with the horizontal, measured perpendicular to the strike (0°–90°). 4. **Hanging wall:** The rock block lying *above* the fault plane. 5. **Footwall:** The rock block lying *below* the fault plane. 6. **Throw:** The vertical component of displacement. 7. **Heave:** The horizontal component of displacement (perpendicular to strike). 8. **Slip / Net slip:** The actual displacement vector along the fault plane. --- ## 2.2 Classification of Faults (High-Yield KTU Topic) ### A. Based on Relative Movement | Fault Type | Stress Regime | Movement | Engineering Concern | |---|---|---|---| | **Normal Fault** | Tensional | Hanging wall moves **down** relative to footwall | Forms graben & horst; common at dam sites; produces crushed gouge | | **Reverse Fault** | Compressional | Hanging wall moves **up** relative to footwall | Shortened crust; high stress concentration | | **Thrust Fault** | Compressional (low dip < 45°) | Hanging wall up, low angle | Serious stability hazard in tunnels | | **Strike-slip Fault** | Shear | Horizontal displacement along strike | Can offset tunnel alignments; e.g., San Andreas | | **Gravity / Normal Fault** | Tensional | As in normal | — | > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic for KTU exams:** **"Normal = No problem with hanging wall going down"** — under tension, the upper block slides down due to gravity. ### B. Based on Strike-Dip Relationship - **Dip-slip fault:** Movement is in the dip direction (most normal & reverse faults). - **Strike-slip fault:** Movement is parallel to strike. - **Oblique-slip fault:** Movement is at an angle to both strike and dip. - **Bedding fault:** Movement parallel to bedding planes. ### C. Recognition of Faults in the Field **Direct evidence (geological):** - Slickensides and striations on fault planes - Fault gouge (pulverized rock) - Fault breccia (angular fragments) - Slickolites (slickenline growths of calcite/quartz) - Repetition or omission of strata **Indirect evidence (geomorphic & hydrological):** - Sudden change in topography (fault scarps) - Offset streams, ridges, fences - Springs aligned along fault traces - Linear valleys (rift valleys) - Sag ponds > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 emphasis:** A **lineament** (a linear topographic feature visible on aerial photographs/satellite imagery) often coincides with a fault trace and must always be checked in dam site investigations. --- ## 2.3 Joints — Detailed Classification ### A. Geological Classification | Type | Description | Occurrence | |---|---|---| | **Tension joints** | Formed by tensile stress; rough, irregular, no infilling | Plutonic rocks, folded sedimentary rocks | | **Shear joints** | Formed by shear stress; smooth, planar, often occur in conjugate sets | Folded & faulted regions | | **Release (exfoliation) joints** | Formed by unloading as overburden is removed | Granites, gneisses | | **Cooling joints** | Formed as igneous rock cools and contracts | Columnar joints in basalt | | **Sheeting joints** | Parallel to ground surface, due to pressure release | Deeply weathered granites | ### B. Engineering Classification (ISRM Suggested) The **International Society for Rock Mechanics (ISRM)** classifies joints by: - **Spacing:** Very close (< 0.05 m) → Very wide (> 2 m) - **Persistence / Continuity:** Length of the joint trace - **Aperture:** Opening width (closed, open, filled) - **Roughness:** Stepped, undulating, planar - **Wall strength** - **Seepage / Infilling** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Joint Roughness Coefficient (JRC)** and **Joint Wall Compressive Strength (JCS)** are key inputs to Barton's empirical shear strength formula for rock joints. ### C. Engineering Significance of Joints - **Slope stability:** Joint sets control the orientation of failure planes. - **Tunnelling:** Overbreak, wedge failures, water inflow. - **Foundation:** Differential settlement on jointed rock. - **Reservoirs:** Leakage pathways through open joints. --- ## 2.4 Geological Site Investigation for Dams and Reservoirs ### A. Stages of Investigation 1. **Reconnaissance stage (preliminary):** - Topographic & geological map study - Aerial photo & satellite image interpretation - Site walk-over - Outcrop logging, joint measurements 2. **Detailed surface investigation:** - Large-scale geological mapping (1:1000 to 1:5000) - Identification of lithology, structure, weathering - Joint mapping using scanline or cell mapping - Sampling for laboratory tests (UCS, point load, porosity, permeability) 3. **Subsurface investigation:** - **Boreholes:** Rotary, percussion, or core drilling - **Drifts and adits** (exploratory tunnels) - **Trial pits and trenches** - **Geophysical methods:** Seismic refraction, electrical resistivity, gravity surveys 4. **In-situ testing:** - Plate load test - In-situ permeability (Lugeon test) - Shear tests on rock 5. **Grouting trials** and curtain design 6. **Monitoring during construction:** Piezometers, extensometers, inclinometers ### B. Specific Geological Requirements at Dam Site | Feature | Acceptable | Unacceptable | |---|---|---| | **Foundation rock** | Sound, fresh, unweathered igneous or metamorphic; massive | Highly weathered, soluble (limestone with karst), weak shales | | **Joints** | Closed, tight, with rough surfaces | Open, continuous, clay-filled, gouge-filled | | **Faults** | None in critical zone; if present, treated by excavation and grouting | Major active fault through dam axis | | **Abutments** | Stable, non-jointed, no adverse dip | Adverse dip toward valley (sliding risk) | | **Reservoir rim** | Watertight, low permeability | Karst, pervious joints, porous formations above FRL | | **River bed** | Sound rock, no thick alluvium | Thick permeable alluvium → heavy leakage | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Favourite Term:** The **"geological barrier"** or **watertight reservoir** requires that the **full supply level (FSL)** is below the level of all permeable formations at the rim, or that these formations are sealed by grout curtains or natural clay blankets. ### C. Grouting Terminology - **Consolidation grouting:** Shallow grouting (5–15 m) to seal the foundation surface, improve bearing. - **Curtain grouting:** Deep linear grout curtain (often 30–100 m+) along the dam axis, extended to abutments, to cut off seepage. - **Contact grouting:** Between concrete and rock at the dam base. **Lugeon Unit (Lu):** Standard measure of rock mass permeability. > 1 Lugeon = 1 litre of water absorbed per minute per metre of borehole length at an effective pressure of 1 MPa (10 kg/cm²). Acceptable leakage: Generally **< 3 Lu** for dam foundations. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 True Dip and Apparent Dip Relationship This is a **classic KTU derivation** appearing frequently in board exams. ### Statement A rock plane with **true dip β** appears to dip at a smaller angle **α** in any vertical section not perpendicular to the strike. The angle between the strike direction and the section line is **θ**. ### Derivation Consider a rock plane striking horizontally. The line of true dip runs perpendicular to the strike and dips at angle β. In any other vertical section, the intersection with the rock plane is a line of **apparent dip** α. Let the rock plane be represented by the line in vertical cross-section. The line of true dip is the line of steepest descent on the plane. $$\tan(\alpha) = \tan(\beta) \cdot \sin(\theta)$$ where: - α = apparent dip in the section - β = true dip - θ = angle between the true dip direction and the section line (0° ≤ θ ≤ 90°) **Step-by-step explanation:** **Step 1:** Imagine the rock plane as a tilted card. The **true dip** is the line of steepest descent — i.e., the line of maximum slope on the plane. **Step 2:** Any other vertical cut through the plane, at angle θ to the true dip direction, will show a shallower dip — the **apparent dip**. **Step 3:** In a horizontal distance d, the true dip descends a vertical amount $d \cdot \tan(\beta)$. **Step 4:** In the same horizontal distance, the apparent dip section (which has been rotated) descends only $d \cdot \tan(\alpha)$. **Step 5:** The horizontal projection of the apparent dip line equals $d \cdot \sin(\theta)$ (because the true dip direction and apparent dip section are separated by angle θ in plan). **Step 6:** Therefore the apparent dip satisfies: $$\tan(\alpha) = \frac{\text{vertical drop}}{\text{horizontal projection}} = \frac{d \cdot \tan(\beta)}{d / \sin(\theta)}$$ **Step 7:** Simplification: $$\tan(\alpha) = \tan(\beta) \cdot \sin(\theta)$$ > [!NOTE] > **Special case check:** If θ = 90° (i.e., section is exactly perpendicular to strike), then sin θ = 1, so α = β ✓ (the apparent dip equals true dip in a section perpendicular to strike). If θ = 0° (section parallel to strike), then α = 0° ✓ (apparent dip is zero along strike). --- ## 3.2 Rock Mass Quality Designation (RQD) – Derivation ### Definition RQD was proposed by **Deere (1964)** as an index of rock mass quality based on the percentage of intact core pieces longer than 10 cm in a core run. $$RQD = \frac{\sum L_i}{L_t} \times 100\%$$ where: - $L_i$ = length of each sound core piece ≥ 100 mm (10 cm) - $L_t$ = total length of the core run (usually 1.5 m or 3 m) ### Worked Numerical Example **Given:** A 3 m core run yields the following intact core lengths (in cm): 40, 8, 25, 6, 12, 5, 18, 22, 9, 14, 11, 30, 7, 16, 21, 4, 13, 19, 28, 32 **Step 1:** Identify pieces ≥ 10 cm: 40, 25, 12, 18, 22, 14, 11, 30, 16, 21, 13, 19, 28, 32 **Step 2:** Sum these lengths: $$\sum L_i = 40 + 25 + 12 + 18 + 22 + 14 + 11 + 30 + 16 + 21 + 13 + 19 + 28 + 32 = 301 \text{ cm}$$ **Step 3:** Apply formula: $$RQD = \frac{301}{300} \times 100\% = 100.3\%$$ Since RQD is capped at 100% (excess indicates very sound rock), the value is **100%** → **Excellent rock**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **RQD Classification (Deere, 1964):** | RQD (%) | Rock Quality | |---|---| | 90 – 100 | Excellent | | 75 – 90 | Good | | 50 – 75 | Fair | | 25 – 50 | Poor | | < 25 | Very Poor | --- ## 3.3 Stability of Rock Slope with Discontinuity (Markland's Test) A **kinematic feasibility test** is used to check whether sliding can occur along a discontinuity plane. **Condition for planar sliding:** $$\beta > \phi_p$$ and $$\beta < \psi_{critical}$$ where: - β = dip of discontinuity plane - $\phi_p$ = peak friction angle of the discontinuity surface - $\psi_{critical}$ = critical slope angle (function of slope geometry) The **Factor of Safety (FoS)** for infinite slope sliding on a planar discontinuity: $$FoS = \frac{c \cdot A + W \cdot \cos(\beta) \cdot \tan(\phi_p)}{W \cdot \sin(\beta)}$$ where: - c = cohesion of the discontinuity surface - A = area of sliding surface per unit width - W = weight of the sliding block - β = dip of sliding plane - $\phi_p$ = peak friction angle For **dry, cohesionless** case (typical rough rock joint): $$FoS = \frac{\tan(\phi_p)}{\tan(\beta)}$$ **Interpretation:** Sliding occurs if FoS < 1.0; stable if FoS > 1.3 (typical KTU benchmark). --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Geological Hazards for Dams | Geological Feature | Hazard Type | Mitigation Measure | |---|---|---| | **Active fault** under dam axis | Catastrophic rupture, displacement | Avoid site; if unavoidable, design for fault displacement | | **Open joints in abutment** | Leakage, uplift pressure | Grout curtain; dental concrete | | **Clay-filled joints** | Reduced shear strength | Excavate & backfill with concrete; drainage | | **Solution cavities (karst)** | Reservoir leakage, foundation collapse | Grouting, concrete diaphragm, blanket | | **Adversely dipping beds** (dip towards valley) | Slope failure, abutment sliding | Anchor cables, buttress, flattening | | **Thick alluvium** in river bed | Excessive settlement, piping | Excavate alluvium, cut-off wall, slurry trench | | **Highly weathered rock** | Low bearing capacity | Remove to sound rock, increase dam section | --- ## 3.5 Numerical Example: Strike and Dip Plotting **Given:** A fault plane has strike N30°E, dip 60° SE, with net slip of 5 m trending S40°E (rake = 30° from strike). **Find:** (a) True dip component, (b) Strike-slip component. **Solution:** **Step 1:** Identify angles. - Net slip = 5 m - Rake (pitch) ω = 30° from strike, measured in fault plane **Step 2:** Strike-slip component (parallel to strike): $$S_s = 5 \cdot \cos(30°) = 5 \times 0.866 = 4.33 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3:** Dip-slip component (along dip direction): $$S_d = 5 \cdot \sin(30°) = 5 \times 0.500 = 2.50 \text{ m}$$ **Step 4:** Vertical component (throw): $$\text{Throw} = S_d \cdot \sin(60°) = 2.50 \times 0.866 = 2.165 \text{ m}$$ **Step 5:** Horizontal component (heave, perpendicular to strike): $$\text{Heave} = S_d \cdot \cos(60°) = 2.50 \times 0.500 = 1.25 \text{ m}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Verification:** Total displacement = $\sqrt{S_s^2 + S_d^2} = \sqrt{4.33^2 + 2.50^2} = \sqrt{18.75 + 6.25} = \sqrt{25} = 5$ m ✓ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Fault Classification Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[FAULT - Planar Fracture with Relative Displacement] --> B[Identify Stress Regime] B --> C{Tensional} B --> D{Compressional} B --> E{Shear} C --> C1[Normal Fault
Hanging wall moves down] D --> D1[Reverse Fault
Hanging wall moves up] D --> D2[Thrust Fault
Low dip angle less than 45 deg] E --> E1[Strike Slip Fault
Horizontal movement] C1 --> F1[Forms Graben and Horst structures] D1 --> F2[Crustal shortening] D2 --> F3[Tunneling hazard in mountainous terrain] E1 --> F4[Offsets linear features e.g. streams] F1 --> G[KTU Engineering Significance] F2 --> G F3 --> G F4 --> G G --> G1[Dam Site - Avoid Active Faults] G --> G2[Tunnel - Grout and Support] G --> G3[Slope - Check Markland Stability] G --> G4[Reservoir - Grout Curtain Design] ``` ## 4.2 Joint Set Configuration Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[ROCK MASS WITH JOINTS] --> B[Set A - Master Joint] A --> C[Set B - Cross Joint] A --> D[Set C - Diagonal Joint] A --> E[Random Joints] B --> B1[Most continuous, most frequent] C --> C1[Less continuous, perpendicular to A] D --> D1[Short, oblique to A and B] E --> E1[Irregular orientation and length] B1 --> F[Joint Survey using Scanline Method] C1 --> F D1 --> F F --> F1[Record Strike, Dip, Spacing, Persistence, Aperture, Roughness, Infill] F1 --> G[KTU Engineering Output - RMR or Q System] G --> G1[Slope Design] G --> G2[Tunnel Support] G --> G3[Foundation Treatment] ``` ## 4.3 Dam Site Investigation — Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[PROJECT CONCEPT - DAM and RESERVOIR] --> B[RECONNAISSANCE STAGE] B --> B1[Toposheet and Geological Map Study] B --> B2[Aerial Photo and Satellite Imagery] B --> B3[Lineament Identification] B --> B4[Walkover Survey] B4 --> C[DETAILED SURFACE MAPPING] C --> C1[Scale 1:1000 to 1:5000] C --> C2[Joint Mapping - Cell or Scanline] C --> C3[Rock Mass Classification RMR and Q] C --> D[SUBSURFACE INVESTIGATION] D --> D1[Drill Boreholes - Rotary Core Drilling] D --> D2[Excavate Adits and Drifts] D --> D3[Geophysical Surveys - Seismic and Resistivity] D --> E[IN-SITU TESTS] E --> E1[Plate Load Test] E --> E2[Lugeon Permeability Test] E --> E3[In-situ Shear Test] E --> F[LABORATORY TESTS] F --> F1[UCS - Unconfined Compressive Strength] F --> F2[Point Load Index] F --> F3[Triaxial and Direct Shear] F --> G[GROUTING TRIAL and CURTAIN DESIGN] G --> H[CONSTRUCTION MONITORING] H --> H1[Piezometers for Pore Pressure] H --> H2[Extensometers for Movement] H --> H3[Inclinometers for Slope Stability] H --> I[SAFEGRO and RESERVOIR IMPOUNDING] ``` ## 4.4 Fault Architecture — Anatomy Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[FAULT PLANE STRIKE LINE - HORIZONTAL] --> B[HANGING WALL BLOCK] A --> C[FOOTWALL BLOCK] B --> B1[Block above the fault plane] C --> C1[Block below the fault plane] A --> D[FAULT PLANE MEASUREMENTS] D --> D1[Strike - Compass direction of horizontal line] D --> D2[Dip - Angle from horizontal 0 to 90 deg] D --> D3[Dip Direction - Compass bearing of steepest descent] A --> E[DISPLACEMENT COMPONENTS] E --> E1[Throw - Vertical component] E --> E2[Heave - Horizontal component perpendicular to strike] E --> E3[Net Slip - Actual displacement vector] A --> F[ENGINEERING FEATURES] F --> F1[Slickensides and Slickenlines] F --> F2[Fault Gouge - Pulverized Rock] F --> F3[Fault Breccia - Angular Fragments] F --> F4[Slickolites - Mineral Growths] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### Question 1: Define a fault. Distinguish between a fault and a joint. **Tags:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` `[CO2, Remember Level]` **Model Answer:** A **fault** is a fracture or discontinuity in a rock mass along which there has been **appreciable relative movement** of the two blocks (hanging wall and footwall) parallel to the fracture surface. It is produced by tectonic stresses and is associated with features like slickensides, fault gouge, breccia, and slickolites. | Property | Fault | Joint | |---|---|---| | Displacement | Appreciable, measurable relative movement | No visible movement | | Formation | Tectonic stress (shear, tension, compression) | Tensile stress, cooling, unloading | | Surface features | Slickensides, gouge, breccia | Plumose structures, hackle marks | | Engineering impact | Major — may cause dam rupture | Moderate — water seepage, slope failure | > [!NOTE] > A **shear joint** may represent an incipient fault — only measurable movement distinguishes them. --- ### Question 2: List the geological features that make a dam site unsuitable. **Tags:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` `[CO3, Understand Level]` **Model Answer:** The following geological features render a dam site **unsuitable or require heavy mitigation**: 1. **Active fault** crossing the dam axis — risk of sudden rupture and displacement. 2. **Open, continuous joints** in abutments or reservoir rim — leakage paths. 3. **Karst (solution cavities) in limestone** — severe reservoir leakage and foundation collapse. 4. **Adversely dipping strata** (dip parallel to valley slope, outward) — abutment slide. 5. **Highly weathered or weak rock** (deeply weathered granite, weak shale) at foundation level. 6. **Thick alluvium** in the river bed — excessive settlement, piping, seepage. 7. **Clay-filled joints or shear zones** — low shear strength, sliding planes. 8. **Existing landslide zones** on abutments. 9. **Permeable formations** above full reservoir level at the rim — leakage over the rim. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **(a) Describe with neat sketches the different types of faults and their engineering significance. (7 marks)** **(b) Discuss the geological investigations required for the construction of a dam. (7 marks)** **Tags:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` `[CO2, CO3 - Apply and Analyze Levels]` --- #### Model Solution to Part (a): **Types of Faults:** **1. Normal Fault (Gravity Fault):** - Produced by **tensional stress**. - Hanging wall moves **down** relative to footwall. - **Dip usually 45°–90°**. - Forms **graben** (down-thrown block) and **horst** (up-thrown block) structures. - Common in rift valleys (e.g., African Rift, Narmada valley). ``` Hanging wall ↓ ╲ ╱ ╲ ╱ ________╲__╱________ Footwall ``` **2. Reverse Fault:** - Produced by **compressional stress**. - Hanging wall moves **up** relative to footwall. - Dip usually steep, > 45°. ``` ___________↑___________ ╱ ╲ ╱ ╲ Hanging wall Footwall ``` **3. Thrust Fault:** - Compressional, with **low dip angle (< 45°)**. - Long horizontal displacement. - Common in **Himalayan** geology — e.g., Main Boundary Thrust (MBT). - Major **engineering hazard** in tunnels. **4. Strike-Slip Fault:** - Movement **horizontal**, parallel to strike. - Two types: **dextral (right-lateral)** and **sinistral (left-lateral)**. - Example: **San Andreas Fault**, **Great Boundary Fault** (Rajasthan). **5. Oblique-Slip Fault:** Combines dip-slip and strike-slip components. **Engineering Significance:** - **Dam sites:** Active faults are to be strictly avoided. Treatment by excavation and concrete plug. - **Tunnels:** Fault zones need heavy support, grouting, and may cause water inrush. - **Reservoirs:** Faults may provide leakage paths; need careful sealing. - **Slopes:** Faults with clay gouge are sliding surfaces. --- #### Model Solution to Part (b): **Stages of Geological Investigation for Dam Construction:** **1. Reconnaissance Stage:** - Study of toposheets, geological maps, and remote sensing data. - Identification of lineaments, major structures, and lithology. - Preliminary site walk-over to assess accessibility and outcrops. **2. Detailed Surface Mapping:** - Large-scale (1:1000 to 1:5000) geological mapping. - Recording of lithology, structure (joints, faults, folds), weathering profile. - **Joint mapping** using scanline survey — recording strike, dip, spacing, persistence, aperture, roughness, and infilling. - Rock mass classification (RMR, Q-system, GSI). **3. Subsurface Investigation:** - **Boreholes:** Rotary core drilling, typically 50–150 mm NX diameter, depths 30–100 m. - **Exploratory adits and drifts** in abutments for direct inspection. - **Trial pits and trenches** in overburden areas. - **Geophysical surveys:** Seismic refraction, electrical resistivity, gravity, and magnetic surveys to identify buried features. **4. In-Situ Testing:** - **Plate load test** for bearing capacity. - **Lugeon test** for permeability (acceptable < 3 Lu). - **In-situ shear test** on rock joints. **5. Laboratory Testing:** - Unconfined Compressive Strength (UCS). - Point Load Index (PLI). - Direct shear, triaxial tests. - Petrographic analysis (thin section study). **6. Grouting Trials & Curtain Design:** - **Consolidation grouting** (shallow, 5–15 m) on foundation surface. - **Curtain grouting** (deep, 30–100+ m) along dam axis and into abutments. - Determines grout mix, pressure, and hole spacing. **7. Monitoring During Construction:** - Piezometers (pore water pressure). - Extensometers (rock movement). - Inclinometers (slope stability). - Seismic monitoring. > [!NOTE] > **Acceptance Criteria for Dam Foundation Rock:** > - Rock should be **fresh, sound, unweathered**. > - **No active faults** should pass through the dam axis. > - **Lugeon value < 3** (low permeability). > - **RMR > 50**, **Q > 1** for good quality rock. > - **Joints should be tight** with no clay infill. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **(a) What are joints? Classify joints and explain their engineering significance. (7 marks)** **(b) Explain in detail the geological part of site investigation for a reservoir. (7 marks)** **Tags:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` `[CO2, CO3 - Apply Level]` --- #### Model Solution to Part (a): **Definition:** A joint is a **fracture or discontinuity** in a rock mass along which there has been **no visible relative movement** of the rock on either side. Joints are surfaces of mechanical weakness. **Classification of Joints:** **A. Geological Classification:** | Type | Origin | Characteristics | |---|---|---| | Tension joints | Tensile stress | Rough, irregular, no infilling, plumose marks | | Shear joints | Shear stress | Smooth, planar, conjugate sets | | Release joints | Unloading | Exfoliation, sheeting, parallel to topography | | Cooling joints | Igneous cooling | Columnar in basalts, polygonal | | Bedding joints | Sedimentary | Along bedding planes | **B. Engineering Classification (ISRM):** - By **spacing** (very close to very wide). - By **persistence** (continuity). - By **aperture** (tight, open, filled). - By **roughness** (rough, smooth, slickensided). - By **infilling** (none, sand, clay, gouge). **Engineering Significance:** 1. **Slope Stability:** Joint sets define potential failure planes. Adverse dip → wedge or planar failure. Markland's test determines kinematic feasibility. 2. **Tunnelling:** Joints control overbreak, wedge failures, support pressure. Excessive jointing → heavy steel rib support. 3. **Reservoir Leakage:** Open joints provide seepage paths from reservoir. May need grout curtain or surface blanket. 4. **Foundation Design:** Closely jointed rock has lower bearing capacity; differential settlement risk. 5. **Excavation:** Joints control blastability, ripping efficiency (P-wave velocity correlation). 6. **RQD & Rock Mass Classification:** Joint frequency directly determines RQD and RMR values. > [!NOTE] > **Key Empirical Parameter: Barton's Joint Shear Strength** > > $$\tau = \sigma_n \cdot \tan\left[\phi_r + JRC \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{JCS}{\sigma_n}\right)\right]$$ > > where: > - τ = shear strength of joint > - $\sigma_n$ = normal stress > - $\phi_r$ = residual friction angle > - JRC = Joint Roughness Coefficient (0–20) > - JCS = Joint Wall Compressive Strength --- #### Model Solution to Part (b): **Geological Investigation of a Reservoir Site:** The **reservoir** must retain water without excessive leakage. Geological investigation covers the **basin floor and the entire rim** up to the proposed Full Reservoir Level (FRL) plus freeboard. **1. Geological Mapping of the Basin and Rim:** - Detailed mapping (1:5000 to 1:25000) of the entire reservoir area. - Identify lithology, structure, and weathering in the rim. - Mark lineaments and dykes. **2. Detection of Permeable Formations:** - Limestone/karst regions must be checked for solution cavities. - **Sinkholes, swallow holes, dolines** indicate active karst — heavy leakage risk. - Permeable sandstones and fractured volcanics (e.g., Deccan basalts) need assessment. **3. Investigation of Seepage Paths:** - Permeable beds outcropping at the rim **below FRL** are potential leakage paths. - Three main leakage geometries: - **Percolation** through pervious strata. - **Over-the-rim leakage** through permeable beds. - **Through-reservoir** leakage via buried channels or faults. **4. Subsurface Investigation:** - **Boreholes** in the reservoir floor and rim. - **Pump-in tests** (Lugeon) to determine permeability. - **Tracer tests** using dyes (rhodamine, fluorescein) to trace groundwater flow paths. - **Geophysical surveys:** Electrical resistivity (VES) for locating cavities; GPR for shallow features. **5. Stability of Reservoir Rim:** - Identify landslides and unstable slopes that may be triggered by impounding. - **Rapid drawdown** conditions must be analysed for submerged slopes. - Sedimentation potential — assess soil erosion in catchment. **6. Special Geological Problems:** - **Karst reservoirs:** May require grout curtains, concrete diaphragms, or polyethylene blankets. - **Volcanic terrain (e.g., Deccan Traps):** Interflow shear zones and vesicles cause leakage. - **Glacial deposits:** Kettle holes and buried channels in moraines. **7. Water-tightness Criteria:** - Reservoir is considered watertight if **seepage < 1% of inflow** for the design life. - **Lugeon values < 1 Lu** in grouted zones. **8. Site Investigation Reports:** - A detailed **Geological Report** with maps, sections, borehole logs, and recommendations is mandatory before construction clearance. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Costing Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Confusing joints and faults:** A fracture with **NO displacement** is a joint — this is the most basic definition. Examiners **always** test this. > > 2. **Drawing faults without dip direction:** A fault must show dip direction (e.g., a small tick on the strike line). > > 3. **Not mentioning the Lugeon test:** For any dam foundation permeability question, the term **"Lugeon"** is mandatory. It is the international standard. > > 4. **Forgetting to discuss grouting:** Dam site investigation is incomplete without **consolidation grouting** and **curtain grouting**. > > 5. **Confusing throw and heave:** Throw = vertical; Heave = horizontal perpendicular to strike. Marks deducted for swapped definitions. > > 6. **Missing the "Rim Problem":** For reservoir questions, students often discuss only foundation, forgetting the **reservoir rim watertightness**. > > 7. **Not sketching:** In KTU 14-mark answers, **neat labelled diagrams are compulsory** for fault types. A text-only answer gets only 60–70% marks. > > 8. **RQD miscalculation:** Always convert cm to consistent units and cap RQD at 100%. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### Quick Revision Checklist (High-Density Bullets) #### **Faults** - ✅ Fault = fracture **with** displacement - ✅ 8 elements: fault plane, strike, dip, hanging wall, footwall, throw, heave, net slip - ✅ Normal (tensional), Reverse (compressional), Thrust (low-angle reverse), Strike-slip (shear) - ✅ Recognition: slickensides, gouge, breccia, slickolites, scarps, offset streams, springs - ✅ Major engineering hazard at dam sites, tunnels, slopes #### **Joints** - ✅ Joint = fracture **without** displacement - ✅ Types: tension, shear, release, cooling, sheeting, bedding - ✅ ISRM classification: spacing, persistence, aperture, roughness, infill - ✅ Joints control slope stability, tunnelling, reservoir leakage - ✅ JRC (Joint Roughness Coefficient) is key to Barton's formula #### **Engineering Geology Significance** - ✅ Bridges geology and civil engineering - ✅ Applied to: tunnels, dams, reservoirs, highways, bridges, slopes, foundations - ✅ Predict → Design → Monitor #### **Dam Site Investigation (Stages)** - ✅ Reconnaissance (toposheets, imagery, walkover) - ✅ Detailed surface mapping (1:1000 – 1:5000) - ✅ Subsurface (boreholes, adits, geophysics) - ✅ In-situ tests (Lugeon < 3 Lu, plate load, in-situ shear) - ✅ Lab tests (UCS, PLT, triaxial) - ✅ Grouting (consolidation + curtain) - ✅ Monitoring (piezometers, extensometers, inclinometers) #### **Reservoir Investigation (Special Points)** - ✅ Watertightness of rim is critical - ✅ Karst = high leakage risk → grout curtain/diaphragm - ✅ Adverse dips, open joints, permeable beds above FRL - ✅ Landslide & rapid drawdown stability at rim - ✅ Tracer tests, geophysics for hidden cavities #### **Numerical / Formula Items** - ✅ Apparent dip: $\tan(\alpha) = \tan(\beta) \cdot \sin(\theta)$ - ✅ RQD: $\text{RQD} = \frac{\sum L_i (\geq 10 \text{ cm})}{L_t} \times 100\%$ - ✅ Slope FoS: $\text{FoS} = \frac{\tan(\phi_p)}{\tan(\beta)}$ (cohesionless) - ✅ Barton's joint shear: $\tau = \sigma_n \tan[\phi_r + JRC \cdot \log_{10}(JCS / \sigma_n)]$ - ✅ 1 Lugeon = 1 L/min/m at 1 MPa; acceptable < 3 Lu - ✅ Throw = $S_d \cdot \sin(\beta)$; Heave = $S_d \cdot \cos(\beta)$ #### **Definition Box — Must Remember Verbatim for KTU** - ✅ **Fault:** Fracture with relative movement - ✅ **Joint:** Fracture without movement - ✅ **Hanging wall:** Block above fault plane - ✅ **Footwall:** Block below fault plane - ✅ **Lugeon:** Unit of rock permeability - ✅ **Grout curtain:** Deep linear grouting to cut seepage - ✅ **Watertight reservoir:** Seepage < 1% of inflow > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute KTU Strategy:** In the exam, always draw **a minimum of two diagrams** for 14-mark questions (one for fault type, one for dam cross-section). Use **bullet points** in answers, not long paragraphs. Mention **Lugeon, RQD, RMR, JRC, JCS** wherever relevant — these are high-recognition key terms examiners scan for.

topics.title: Tunnels and Highways
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET416 - ENGINEERING GEOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Tunnels and Highways

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Tunnels and Highways in Engineering Geology

**Engineering Geology** plays a decisive role in the planning, design, construction, and maintenance of **Tunnels** and **Highways**. Since both are long, linear, ground-penetrating or ground-supported structures, their success depends almost entirely on the geological medium they interact with.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> *Tunnel Geology* is the branch of applied geology dealing with the investigation of subsurface geological conditions — rock types, structural discontinuities, groundwater regimes, and stress fields — to determine the feasibility, alignment, support requirements, and stability of underground excavations.
>
> *Highway Geology* is the branch dealing with the study of surface and near-surface geological conditions — soil/rock profiles, slope stability, drainage, and weathering — to determine the most stable, safe, and economical alignment for a road or highway.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Earth's Artery" Concept:**
> Imagine the Earth as a human body. **Tunnels** are like *arteries* carved through the body — they need to avoid the "weak spots" (faults, fractured rock, soft soil) and pass through the "healthy bone" (massive, fresh, unjointed rock). A surgeon never cuts through diseased tissue if a healthier path exists.
>
> **Highways**, on the other hand, are like *skin laid over the body*. The skin must follow the natural contours of the body — it cannot be glued onto a steep, unstable mountain face. A geologist is the *dermatologist* who checks whether the "skin" (road) will hold on or slide off.

## 1.3 Why Geology Matters — Key Statistics

| Structure | Critical Geological Parameter | Standard Reference Metric |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Tunnels | **Stand-up time** | **Hours to Months** |
| Highways | **Factor of Safety (FoS)** | **1.3 to 1.5** |
| Both | **RMR (Rock Mass Rating)** | **0 to 100** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> According to KTU 2024 Scheme, students must be able to evaluate **geological feasibility** of tunnel and highway projects using *Rock Mass Classification Systems* (RMR, Q-system, SMR) and *Slope Stability Analyses*.

## 1.4 Core Sub-Topics Covered

1. **Tunnels:** Alignment selection, geological investigations, tunneling methods in different rocks, support systems, problems (squeezing, swelling, inflow).
2. **Highways:** Route selection, geological investigations, cuts and fills, slope stability, landslides, drainage, road aggregates.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geological Considerations for Tunnels

A tunnel is an underground passage whose stability depends on the **in-situ stress field**, **rock mass strength**, and **groundwater condition**. The geologist's job is to provide a 3-D geological model along the proposed alignment.

### 2.1.1 Key Geological Parameters for Tunnels

- **Lithology (Rock Type):** Hard, massive rocks (granite, basalt, quartzite) → stable. Soft, weathered, or soluble rocks (limestone with karst, schist, shale) → unstable.
- **Structure:** Folds, faults, joints, and foliations control failure.
- **Groundwater:** Inflow increases load and reduces shear strength.
- **In-situ Stress:** Governed by depth, tectonic regime, and topography.
- **Weathering Profile:** Determines stand-up time.

### 2.1.2 Tunneling Methods vs. Rock Conditions

| Rock Condition | Recommended Method | Support |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Hard, massive rock | Drill & Blast | Rock bolts + shotcrete |
| Soft rock / soil | Shield TBM / EPB | Segmental lining |
| Mixed face | Sequential excavation | Combined support |
| Squeezing rock | Yielding support | Heavy steel ribs |

## 2.2 Geological Considerations for Highways

Highways are **surface structures** with shallower depth of influence, but they extend over long distances, exposing them to a wide variety of geological conditions.

### 2.2.1 Key Geological Parameters for Highways

- **Slope Stability:** The single most important factor.
- **Soil/rock profile:** Determines bearing capacity and settlement.
- **Drainage:** Affects pore water pressure.
- **Aggregates:** Source, quality, and durability.
- **Landslide-prone zones:** Old landslides can reactivate.

### 2.2.2 Slope Stability Concepts

A slope fails when driving forces (gravity, water) exceed resisting forces (shear strength). The **Factor of Safety (FoS)** is:

$$FoS = \frac{\text{Resisting Forces}}{\text{Driving Forces}}$$

A FoS $\geq 1.3$ is generally acceptable for highways, while $\geq 1.5$ is for critical structures.

## 2.3 Rock Mass Classification Systems (KTU High-Yield)

These systems quantify "rock quality" into a numerical rating used to design tunnel supports.

### 2.3.1 Rock Mass Rating (RMR) — Bieniawski (1989)

$$RMR = R1 + R2 + R3 + R4 + R5 + R6$$

| Component | Parameter | Max Rating |
| :--- | :--- | :---: |
| $R1$ | Uniaxial Compressive Strength (UCS) | 15 |
| $R2$ | RQD (Rock Quality Designation) | 20 |
| $R3$ | Spacing of Discontinuities | 20 |
| $R4$ | Condition of Discontinuities | 30 |
| $R5$ | Groundwater Conditions | 15 |
| $R6$ | Orientation of Discontinuities | Adjusts to $-12$ to $0$ |

### 2.3.2 Q-System — Barton (1974)

$$Q = \frac{RQD}{J_n} \times \frac{J_r}{J_a} \times \frac{J_w}{SRF}$$

| Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| $RQD$ | Rock Quality Designation |
| $J_n$ | Joint Set Number |
| $J_r$ | Joint Roughness Number |
| $J_a$ | Joint Alteration Number |
| $J_w$ | Joint Water Reduction Factor |
| $SRF$ | Stress Reduction Factor |

### 2.3.3 RQD Calculation

$$RQD = \frac{\sum \text{Length of sound core pieces} \geq 100 \text{ mm}}{\text{Total core run length}} \times 100$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These classifications are not academic — they directly dictate the **support pressure**, **excavation method**, and **cost estimate** of the project. KTU questions frequently ask to classify a given rock mass and recommend support.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| RMR | $RMR = R1 + R2 + R3 + R4 + R5 + R6$ | Rock classification |
| Q-System | $Q = \frac{RQD}{J_n} \cdot \frac{J_r}{J_a} \cdot \frac{J_w}{SRF}$ | Tunnel support design |
| RQD | $RQD = \frac{\sum L \geq 100 \text{ mm}}{L_{total}} \times 100$ | Core logging |
| Support Pressure (Barton) | $P_{roof} = \frac{2}{J_r} \cdot Q^{-1/3}$ | Roof support (MPa) |
| Slope Factor of Safety | $FoS = \frac{c + \sigma \tan\phi}{W \sin\alpha}$ | Slope stability |
| Terzaghi's Rock Load | $H_p = 0.5 (B + H_t)$ | Tunnel overburden load |
| Overburden Pressure | $\sigma_v = \gamma \cdot z$ | Vertical stress at depth |
| SMR (Slope Mass Rating) | $SMR = RMR_{basic} + (F_1 \cdot F_2 \cdot F_3) + F_4$ | Highway cut slope stability |
| Los Angeles Abrasion | $LA = \frac{W_{loss}}{W_{initial}} \times 100$ | Aggregate quality |
| Slake Durability | $I_{d2} = \frac{W_{dry}}{W_{initial}} \times 100$ | Weathering resistance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Reminder:** UCS in **MPa**, RQD as a **percentage**, Q as a **dimensionless number**, depth $z$ in **metres**, unit weight $\gamma$ in **kN/m³**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: RQD from Core Recovery Data

The **RQD** is the most fundamental index in rock mass classification. The procedure is:

**Step 1:** Retrieve the drill core from a core run of total length $L_{total}$ (typically 1.5 m or 3 m).

**Step 2:** Measure the lengths of all intact, sound core pieces that are $\geq 100$ mm long.

**Step 3:** Sum these sound pieces:

$$L_{sound} = L_1 + L_2 + L_3 + \ldots + L_n$$

**Step 4:** Apply the formula:

$$RQD = \frac{L_{sound}}{L_{total}} \times 100 \quad (\%) $$

**Example:** A 3 m core run yields intact pieces of lengths: 0.18 m, 0.22 m, 0.11 m, 0.35 m, 0.09 m, 0.42 m, 0.27 m, 0.15 m, 0.30 m, 0.08 m, 0.25 m, 0.12 m, 0.46 m.

Summing sound pieces $\geq 100$ mm:

$$L_{sound} = 0.18 + 0.22 + 0.11 + 0.35 + 0.42 + 0.27 + 0.15 + 0.30 + 0.25 + 0.12 + 0.46 = 2.83 \text{ m}$$

Therefore:

$$RQD = \frac{2.83}{3.00} \times 100 = 94.3 \text{ \%}$$

> This corresponds to a **"Good Rock"** rating ($R2 = 17$).

## 3.2 Derivation: Q-System for a Given Rock Mass

**Given the following parameters from a tunnel site:**

| Parameter | Value |
| :--- | :---: |
| $RQD$ | 75 |
| $J_n$ (3 joint sets) | 9 |
| $J_r$ (rough, planar) | 2 |
| $J_a$ (slightly altered) | 2 |
| $J_w$ (medium inflow) | 0.66 |
| $SRF$ (moderate stress) | 2.5 |

**Step-by-step solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the block size ratio:

$$\frac{RQD}{J_n} = \frac{75}{9} = 8.33$$

**Step 2:** Compute the inter-block shear strength ratio:

$$\frac{J_r}{J_a} = \frac{2}{2} = 1.0$$

**Step 3:** Compute the active stress ratio:

$$\frac{J_w}{SRF} = \frac{0.66}{2.5} = 0.264$$

**Step 4:** Multiply all three ratios:

$$Q = 8.33 \times 1.0 \times 0.264 = 2.20$$

> A Q-value of $2.20$ corresponds to a **"Fair"** rock mass. According to Barton's support chart, this requires **systematic bolting** + **50–100 mm shotcrete** in the crown.

## 3.3 Derivation: Factor of Safety for a Planar Slope Failure

For a planar failure along a single discontinuity dipping into a cut slope:

**Step 1:** Identify the geometry — slope angle $\alpha$, discontinuity dip $\beta$, length of failure plane $L$, unit weight $\gamma$.

**Step 2:** Compute the driving force (parallel to the failure plane):

$$F_{driving} = W \sin\beta = \gamma \cdot A \cdot L \cdot \sin\beta$$

where $A$ is the area of the sliding block.

**Step 3:** Compute the resisting force (shear strength on the plane):

$$F_{resisting} = c \cdot L + W \cos\beta \cdot \tan\phi$$

**Step 4:** Factor of Safety:

$$FoS = \frac{c \cdot L + W \cos\beta \cdot \tan\phi}{W \sin\beta}$$

**Example:** A cut slope in granite has $\alpha = 60°$, $\beta = 35°$, $c = 25$ kPa, $\phi = 30°$, $W = 480$ kN/m, $L = 10$ m.

$$F_{resisting} = (25 \times 10) + (480 \cos 35°) \tan 30°$$
$$F_{resisting} = 250 + 480 \times 0.819 \times 0.577$$
$$F_{resisting} = 250 + 226.9 = 476.9 \text{ kN/m}$$

$$F_{driving} = 480 \sin 35° = 480 \times 0.574 = 275.3 \text{ kN/m}$$

$$FoS = \frac{476.9}{275.3} = 1.73$$

> A FoS of $1.73$ indicates a **stable slope** for highway purposes.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Rock Class & Tunnel Support

| RMR Range | Q Range | Rock Class | Tunnel Support Recommendation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 81 – 100 | $>$ 100 | Very Good | Spot bolting |
| 61 – 80 | 10 – 100 | Good | Systematic bolting, 50 mm shotcrete |
| 41 – 60 | 1 – 10 | Fair | Bolting + 50–100 mm shotcrete, mesh |
| 21 – 40 | 0.1 – 1 | Poor | Heavy support, ribs, 100–150 mm shotcrete |
| $<$ 20 | $<$ 0.1 | Very Poor | Special measures, yielding support |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flowchart: Tunnel Geological Investigation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Project Initiation] --> stepB[Desk Study: Toposheets, Aerial Photos]
    stepB --> stepC[Surface Geological Mapping]
    stepC --> stepD[Geophysical Surveys: Seismic, Resistivity]
    stepD --> stepE[Borehole Drilling and Core Logging]
    stepE --> stepF[In-situ Testing: Plate Load, Packer Test]
    stepF --> stepG[Laboratory Testing: UCS, Point Load, Direct Shear]
    stepG --> stepH[Rock Mass Classification: RMR, Q-system]
    stepH --> stepI[Support Design and Cost Estimate]
    stepI --> stepJ[Construction Monitoring and Validation]
    stepJ --> stepK[Project Completion]

    style startA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style stepH fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style stepJ fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Flowchart: Highway Alignment Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** 
> **Q1. Define Rock Mass Rating (RMR). List its six parameters.**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> *Rock Mass Rating (RMR)* is a geo-mechanical classification system developed by Bieniawski (1989) that provides a comprehensive index of rock mass quality using six parameters:
> 1. **R1 — Uniaxial Compressive Strength (UCS)** of intact rock (max 15)
> 2. **R2 — Rock Quality Designation (RQD)** (max 20)
> 3. **R3 — Spacing of discontinuities** (max 20)
> 4. **R4 — Condition of discontinuities** (max 30)
> 5. **R5 — Groundwater conditions** (max 15)
> 6. **R6 — Orientation of discontinuities** (adjustment, $-12$ to $0$)
>
> The total RMR guides support design for tunnels and stability assessment of slopes.

> **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** 
> **Q2. List any four geological factors that affect the alignment of a highway.**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> 1. **Lithology:** Weak or soluble rocks should be avoided.
> 2. **Structure:** Faults, shear zones, and major joints should be crossed perpendicularly.
> 3. **Slope Stability:** Existing landslides or steep unstable slopes must be avoided.
> 4. **Drainage / Groundwater:** Areas of high water table or springs cause slope failure.
> 5. *(Any other valid point such as weathering, river crossings, etc.)*

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`**
> **Question A (14 Marks):**
> **(a)** Explain the **geological investigations** required for the alignment of a tunnel with reference to surface and subsurface methods. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** A tunnel is to be driven through a rock mass with the following properties:
> $RQD = 60$, $J_n = 12$, $J_r = 1.5$, $J_a = 3$, $J_w = 0.66$, $SRF = 2.5$.
> Calculate the **Q-value** and suggest the **tunnel support** as per Barton's chart. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Geological Investigations for Tunnel Alignment:**

Geological investigations for a tunnel consist of **two stages**:

**Stage 1 — Surface Investigations:**
- **Topographic study:** Using Survey of India toposheets and DEM data.
- **Aerial photograph interpretation:** Identification of lineaments, faults, drainage patterns.
- **Surface geological mapping:** Lithology, structure, weathering profile, landslides.
- **Geophysical surveys:**
  - *Seismic refraction* → to determine depth of overburden and rippability.
  - *Electrical resistivity* → to detect groundwater, clay zones, and cavities.
  - *Ground Penetrating Radar (GPR)* → for shallow features.
- **Hydrological study:** Spring locations, river levels, recharge zones.

**Stage 2 — Subsurface Investigations:**
- **Boreholes:** Along proposed alignment at 200–500 m spacing (closer in complex geology).
- **Core drilling & logging:** For RQD, discontinuity frequency, weathering grade.
- **In-situ tests:**
  - Plate load test
  - Packer test (for permeability)
  - In-situ stress measurement (hydraulic fracturing, overcoring)
- **Laboratory tests:** UCS, triaxial, point load, direct shear.
- **Pilot tunnel / adit:** Sometimes driven to verify actual conditions.

**Part (b) — Q-System Calculation:**

Given:
$RQD = 60$, $J_n = 12$, $J_r = 1.5$, $J_a = 3$, $J_w = 0.66$, $SRF = 2.5$

$$Q = \frac{RQD}{J_n} \times \frac{J_r}{J_a} \times \frac{J_w}{SRF}$$

$$Q = \frac{60}{12} \times \frac{1.5}{3} \times \frac{0.66}{2.5}$$

$$Q = 5.0 \times 0.5 \times 0.264$$

$$Q = 0.66$$

**Interpretation:** A Q-value of $0.66$ falls in the range $0.1$ to $1.0$ — categorized as **"Poor Rock"**.

**Recommended Tunnel Support (Barton, 1974):**
- **Bolting:** Systematic bolting, 4–6 m long, spaced 1.0–1.5 m.
- **Shotcrete:** 100–150 mm thickness with steel fibres.
- **Steel ribs:** Light to medium steel sets at 1.0–1.5 m spacing.
- **Mesh:** Welded wire mesh reinforcement.

---

> **`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`**
> **Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Alternative]:**
> **(a)** Describe the **geological factors** that govern the selection of a highway alignment in hilly terrain. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Calculate the **RMR** for a rock mass with the following data and classify the rock mass:
> UCS = 80 MPa, RQD = 70%, Spacing = 200 mm, slightly rough joints, dry conditions, fair orientation. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Geological Factors for Highway Alignment in Hilly Terrain:**

1. **Lithology (Rock Type):** Massive igneous rocks (granite, basalt) are preferred. Sedimentary rocks (shale, poorly cemented sandstone) are avoided due to slaking and low strength.
2. **Geological Structure:**
   - **Dip and strike** of beds relative to slope direction.
   - *Dip-slope* conditions (beds dipping parallel to slope) are dangerous.
   - Tunnels and culverts must cross faults **perpendicularly**.
3. **Slope Stability:**
   - Natural slopes in old landslides are unstable.
   - SMR (Slope Mass Rating) is used to evaluate cut slopes.
4. **Drainage & Groundwater:**
   - Springs and seepage zones indicate potential slope failure.
   - Alignment should follow ridges (better drained) over valleys.
5. **Weathering Profile:** Deep weathering implies weak foundations for embankments.
6. **River Crossings:** At narrow, stable, geologically sound points.

**Part (b) — RMR Calculation:**

| Parameter | Value | Rating |
| :--- | :--- | :---: |
| UCS = 80 MPa | Strong | $R1 = 12$ |
| RQD = 70% | Good | $R2 = 13$ |
| Spacing = 200 mm | Moderate | $R3 = 10$ |
| Slightly rough joints, aperture $<$ 1 mm | Fair | $R4 = 20$ |
| Dry conditions | Completely dry | $R5 = 15$ |
| Fair orientation | Adjustment | $R6 = -5$ |

$$RMR = R1 + R2 + R3 + R4 + R5 + R6$$
$$RMR = 12 + 13 + 10 + 20 + 15 - 5$$
$$RMR = 65$$

**Classification:** RMR = 65 falls in the range **61 – 80 → "Good Rock"**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the orientation adjustment $R6$:** Many students sum only $R1$ to $R5$ and lose 1–2 marks. Always include $R6$ (can be negative).
> 2. **Mixing up RQD and Core Recovery:** RQD considers **only sound pieces $\geq$ 100 mm**, not all recovered core.
> 3. **Wrong unit for UCS:** Use MPa in tables; some students incorrectly use kg/cm².
> 4. **No units in Q-system:** All six parameters are dimensionless, so the final Q is also dimensionless — do not write any unit.
> 5. **Confusing RMR with RQD:** RQD is **one parameter** of RMR (rating up to 20), not the total rating.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Tunnels and Highways**
>
> - **Tunnel alignment** is selected to avoid **faults**, **shear zones**, **karst**, and **high groundwater inflow** zones.
> - **RMR (Bieniawski)** = 6 parameters; range **0 to 100**; classes from *Very Poor* to *Very Good*.
> - **Q-system (Barton)** uses 6 parameters in 3 ratios; logarithmic-scale; widely used for **tunnel support design**.
> - **RQD** = percentage of sound core pieces $\geq$ 100 mm; *Excellent* if $\geq$ 90%, *Very Poor* if $<$ 25%.
> - **Highway alignment** prefers **ridge routes** over valley routes in hilly terrain for better drainage and stability.
> - **Slope stability** governed by **Factor of Safety**; minimum FoS = **1.3** for highways.
> - **SMR (Slope Mass Rating)** = $RMR_{basic} + (F_1 \cdot F_2 \cdot F_3) + F_4$; used for cut slope design.
> - **Aggregate tests:** Los Angeles Abrasion $<$ 30% (wearing course) and Slake Durability Index $>$ 90% (for tunnels).
> - **Tunnel problems:** Squeezing rock, swelling rock, rock bursts, methane, high water inflow.
> - **Highway problems:** Landslides, rockfall, settlement, frost heave, sinkholes (in karst).
> - **Avoidance zones:** Active faults, landslides, marshes, floodplains, karst regions, and mine workings.
> - **Investigation sequence:** Desk study → Surface mapping → Geophysical survey → Boreholes → Lab tests → Classification.
> - **Stand-up time** is the time a tunnel opening remains stable without support — depends on rock mass quality and span.
> - **Squeezing rock** → high in-situ stress + soft rock; requires yielding support.
> - **Rock burst** → high stress + brittle rock; requires stress relief techniques.
